L2_Genomic.d: Real-time tracking of pathogen evolution

profile.overview: Nextstrain is an open-source bioinformatics platform for real-time molecular-epidemiology and phylogeographic tracking of pathogen evolution. Co-founded in 2015 by Trevor Bedford (Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center, Seattle) and Richard Neher (Biozentrum, University of Basel). It merges continuously updated genomic sequences with geographic and temporal metadata into interactive phylogenetic trees and maps. Covers SARS-CoV-2, influenza, Ebola, Zika, dengue, mpox, RSV, measles, West Nile virus, and 30+ other pathogens. Became the de-facto standard for real-time COVID-19 genomic surveillance used by WHO and 100+ national public-health agencies.
profile.functional_scope: Real-time phylogenetic analysis, molecular epidemiology, phylodynamic inference (BEAST-like dating), ancestral state reconstruction, geographic spread modeling, mutation tracking and clade assignment, frequency estimation of lineages and mutations. Nextclade provides browser-based QC and clade assignment for individual sequences. Nextstrain Groups enable private institutional datasets. Nextstrain Narratives allow guided scrollable data-stories linking trees to maps. Version 3 (Jan 2024) added support for complex genome annotations, circular genomes, and splicing. March 2025 update expanded frequency-based analyses for scaling beyond phylogenetic limits. September 2025 introduced standardized multiple-input workflows.
profile.tech_stack: Augur (Python bioinformatics pipeline: alignment via MAFFT, tree inference via IQ-TREE/RAxML, TreeTime for molecular clock, ancestral trait inference, clade frequency estimation). Auspice (React.js / D3.js interactive visualization frontend, client-side rendering). Nextclade (Rust/WebAssembly clade assignment and QC, runs entirely in browser). Nextstrain CLI for workflow orchestration. Docker and Conda runtimes. Source on GitHub under GNU AGPL v3. Data feeds from GISAID (suspended Oct 2025), GenBank/NCBI, and community uploads. Infrastructure on AWS.
profile.operator: Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center (Seattle, USA) and University of Basel (Switzerland). Core team of ~12 developers and scientists. Funded by NIH/NIAID (R01 AI127893), Howard Hughes Medical Institute, Wellcome Trust (220542/Z/20/Z), Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, and the European Research Council (ERC StG 101039416). Independent open-source project; not formally part of any government agency.
profile.data_model: JSON-based tree structures (Auspice JSON v2) with node annotations: mutations (nucleotide + amino acid), collection dates, geographic coordinates, clade/lineage assignments, branch lengths (substitutions and temporal). Metadata TSV files with fields: strain, date, region, country, division, host, originating_lab, submitting_lab, authors. Sequences stored as aligned FASTA. Source data: GISAID EpiCoV (16M+ SARS-CoV-2 seqs, feed suspended Oct 2025), NCBI GenBank/SRA, INSDC. Nextclade reference datasets versioned and tagged.
profile.users_scale: Millions of monthly page views during pandemic peaks. Cited in 3,735+ papers (Hadfield et al. 2018 alone). Used by WHO, ECDC, CDC, Public Health England, Africa CDC, and 100+ national agencies. Nextstrain Groups serve >50 institutional teams including SPHERES consortium. Thousands of researchers run local Augur analyses monthly. Key infrastructure during COVID-19 pandemic response worldwide.
profile.access_model: Fully open-source (GNU AGPL v3) and free to use. No login required for public builds at nextstrain.org. Nextstrain Groups require registration for private dataset sharing. All code on GitHub (nextstrain org). API access via Nextstrain REST endpoints. Nextclade web tool at clades.nextstrain.org is entirely client-side (no data leaves browser). GISAID-sourced builds require separate GISAID Data Access Agreement.

deep_research.executive_summary: Nextstrain is the gold-standard open-source platform for real-time pathogen phylogenetics, used by WHO and 100+ national agencies. It became indispensable during COVID-19 and continues to expand to 30+ pathogens. Score 95/100 reflects exceptional analytics, visualization, and community. Key risk: GISAID data feed suspension (Oct 2025) forced a pivot to open GenBank/INSDC data.

L2_Genomic.d: Standardized access to epidemiological and genomic data
st: Open data access policy enabling broad research use

profile.overview: outbreak.info is an open-source genomic and epidemiological data integration platform developed by the Center for Viral Systems Biology (CViSB) at Scripps Research Institute (La Jolla, CA). Launched in early 2020, it provides standardized access to SARS-CoV-2 variant surveillance data, epidemiological case/death counts, and a searchable library of 300,000+ COVID-19 research publications. Integrates data from GISAID, Johns Hopkins CSSE, NIH LitCovid, and other sources into unified dashboards with variant prevalence reports, mutation tracking, and location-based comparisons.
profile.functional_scope: SARS-CoV-2 variant surveillance reports with lineage prevalence by location, mutation reports for any lineage or mutation combination, epidemiological case/death tracking by country/region, Research Library with 300,000+ publications searchable by topic and metadata, situation reports for variants of concern/interest, comparison tools for multiple locations/lineages, API access for programmatic data retrieval, customizable data visualizations, and integration of genomic and epidemiological data streams. Supports Pango lineage nomenclature and WHO variant labels.
profile.tech_stack: Vue.js frontend with D3.js visualizations, Python data processing pipelines, Elasticsearch backend for Research Library, BioThings API framework (developed at Scripps), RESTful API, data sources include GISAID EpiCoV (feed terminated Jan 2025), Johns Hopkins CSSE, NIH LitCovid/PubMed, WHO, bioRxiv/medRxiv preprints. Hosted on cloud infrastructure. Open-source on GitHub under MIT license. Automated daily data ingestion and processing pipelines.
profile.operator: Center for Viral Systems Biology (CViSB), Scripps Research Institute, La Jolla, California. Led by Laura Hughes, Karthik Gangavarapu, and Andrew Su. Funded by NIH/NIAID, NCATS, and Scripps Research. Part of the NIAID Centers of Excellence for Genomic Sciences. Academic non-profit project.
profile.data_model: Standardized JSON schema integrating: (1) genomic data – lineage prevalence, mutation frequencies, per-location temporal trends from GISAID; (2) epidemiological data – daily case counts, deaths, testing rates from JHU CSSE; (3) research publications – structured metadata from LitCovid, preprint servers, clinical trials. BioThings API provides unified query interface. Data updated daily (genomic updates halted Jan 2025 due to GISAID feed termination). Geographic hierarchy: global > country > admin1 > admin2.
profile.users_scale: Widely used by researchers, public health officials, journalists, and the general public during the COVID-19 pandemic. Published in Nature Methods (2023) for the Research Library. Cited in hundreds of publications. API serves thousands of queries daily. Situation reports referenced by WHO, CDC, and ECDC for variant monitoring. One of the most-visited COVID-19 data dashboards alongside JHU and Our World in Data.
profile.access_model: Fully free and open-access. No registration required for web interface. API access available without authentication for most endpoints. Open-source code on GitHub (outbreak-info org). Data reuse governed by source-specific terms (GISAID Data Access Agreement for genomic data). Research Library metadata freely available. MIT license for codebase.

deep_research.executive_summary: outbreak.info is a leading COVID-19 data integration platform from Scripps Research, providing unified access to genomic variant surveillance, epidemiological data, and a 300,000+ publication Research Library. Score 90/100 reflects excellent data aggregation, strong API, and open access. Critical disruption: GISAID terminated their data feed on Jan 11, 2025, halting genomic updates and prompting a joint public response in Feb 2026.

L2_Genomic.d: Chan Zuckerberg metagenomic pathogen detection and discovery platform

profile.overview: CZ ID (Chan Zuckerberg ID, formerly IDseq) is a free, open-source, cloud-based metagenomics analysis platform developed by the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative (CZI) and Chan Zuckerberg Biohub. Originally launched as IDseq in 2018, rebranded to CZ ID in 2022. Enables researchers with no bioinformatics expertise to upload raw sequencing data (Illumina short-read and Oxford Nanopore long-read) and automatically identify pathogens from metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) data. Designed for outbreak detection and infectious disease surveillance in clinical and environmental samples worldwide.
profile.functional_scope: Automated metagenomic pathogen detection from mNGS data: host subtraction, quality filtering, taxonomic classification against comprehensive reference databases (NCBI NT/NR), coverage visualization, phylogenetic placement, consensus genome assembly, antimicrobial resistance (AMR) gene detection (AMR module launched 2024-2025), bulk sample processing, sample comparison and heatmaps, pathogen flagging against curated pathogen list. Supports both short-read (Illumina) and long-read (ONT) workflows. No-code browser interface with downloadable reports. CZ ID AMR module integrates microbial and AMR detection in a single workflow.
profile.tech_stack: AWS cloud infrastructure (S3, SQS, EC2, ECS, Step Functions), Python/Ruby backend pipelines, React.js frontend, NCBI NT/NR databases for classification, minimap2 and STAR for alignment, GSNAP for host subtraction, SPAdes/MEGAHIT for assembly, DIAMOND for protein-level search, AMRFinderPlus for AMR detection, Docker containerized workflows, REST API. Open-source on GitHub (chanzuckerberg/czid-web, czid-workflows). Reference database updated monthly.
profile.operator: Chan Zuckerberg Initiative (CZI) and Chan Zuckerberg Biohub, San Francisco, CA. Scientific leadership by Joe DeRisi (UCSF/CZ Biohub co-president) and CZI infectious disease team. Funded entirely by CZI (philanthropic arm of Mark Zuckerberg and Priscilla Chan). Free for all researchers globally.
profile.data_model: Sample-centric: each upload contains raw FASTQ files linked to host organism, sample type, collection location, and date. Pipeline outputs: taxon hit lists with NT/NR rpm (reads per million), z-scores, coverage depth, assembly metrics. AMR module outputs: detected AMR genes mapped to drug classes with organism context. Consensus genomes in FASTA format. All results stored in project workspaces with sharing controls. Supports bulk metadata upload via CSV. Pathogen list curated by CZI epidemiologists.
profile.users_scale: Used by researchers in 90+ countries. Over 400+ institutions registered. Key deployments in sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and Latin America for emerging pathogen detection. Published in Nature Communications, Nature Microbiology, and 200+ citing publications. Used to identify novel pathogens including during COVID-19 and mpox outbreaks. AMR manuscript (Lu et al. 2025) cited 13 times within months. Impact page documents case studies from global health labs.
profile.access_model: Free for all researchers globally. Registration required for sample uploads and project creation. No-code web interface at czid.org. Open-source code on GitHub (chanzuckerberg org). No data usage limits. Cloud-based (no local installation needed). Funded entirely by CZI – no licensing fees. Data sharing controls per project (private, shared, public). API available for programmatic access. Published results can be shared via permanent project URLs.

deep_research.executive_summary: CZ ID is a transformative free cloud platform democratizing metagenomic pathogen detection for researchers worldwide, funded by CZI. Score 89.2/100 reflects automated no-code workflows, comprehensive pathogen databases, and global accessibility. The 2024-2025 AMR module addition significantly expanded utility. Key limitation: cloud dependency and potential long-term sustainability questions tied to single philanthropic funder.

L2_Genomic.d: Identifying and clustering closely related pathogen genomes

profile.overview: NCBI Pathogen Detection is a real-time automated pipeline operated by the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) at NIH/NLM. Launched in 2016 as part of the FDA GenomeTrakr initiative for foodborne pathogen surveillance. It continuously integrates bacterial and fungal pathogen genomic sequences from food safety, environmental, and clinical sources worldwide. The system performs automated real-time SNP clustering to identify potential outbreak clusters and transmission chains. As of 2025, contains >1 million bacterial isolate genomes with >25 organisms tracked. Used by FDA, CDC, USDA, and PulseNet for foodborne illness outbreak detection.
profile.functional_scope: Automated SNP-based phylogenetic clustering of pathogen genomes (identifies isolates within 0-50 SNP range), real-time outbreak cluster detection, AMR gene prediction, virulence factor identification, serotype/sequence type assignment, isolate browser with filterable metadata, SNP tree visualization, reference gene catalog, organism-specific analysis pages (Salmonella, Listeria, E. coli, Campylobacter, Vibrio, Klebsiella, etc.), integration with SRA for raw reads, BioSample for metadata, and Assembly for genome records. Supports submission from GenomeTrakr labs worldwide. FDA used it for 1,643 consumer protection actions (as of 2025).
profile.tech_stack: Custom NCBI bioinformatics pipeline: SKESA assembler, AMRFinderPlus for AMR detection, reference SNP system for clustering (pathogens with closed reference genomes), MinHash for rapid genome comparison, BLAST for sequence similarity, SRA Toolkit, PostgreSQL metadata store, cloud-scale compute on NIH infrastructure. Public web interface at ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pathogens. REST API via NCBI Datasets. Integrated with Pathogen Detection FTP site for bulk data download. All code and databases maintained by NCBI.
profile.operator: National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI), National Library of Medicine (NLM), National Institutes of Health (NIH), USA. Core partnership with FDA (GenomeTrakr), CDC (PulseNet), USDA FSIS. International contributors include public health labs from UK, Canada, EU, Australia, and 30+ countries via PulseNet International. Funded by NIH intramural program and interagency agreements with FDA/CDC.
profile.data_model: Isolate-centric: each genome linked to BioSample (metadata: organism, collection date, geographic location, isolation source, host, serovar), BioProject, SRA (raw reads), Assembly (genome), and Pathogen Detection cluster ID. SNP clusters defined by pairwise SNP distances with hierarchical thresholds. AMR predictions stored as gene-phenotype associations. Data freely available via FTP, NCBI Datasets API, and Isolates Browser. FAIR-compliant. Standardized metadata schemas per organism.
profile.users_scale: Over 1 million bacterial isolate genomes processed. 800+ GenomeTrakr labs in 30+ countries submit sequences. FDA reports 1,643 consumer protection actions facilitated by the pipeline (2025). Used daily by CDC PulseNet for cluster detection. USDA FSIS uses for meat/poultry inspection. Cited in hundreds of foodborne outbreak investigations. Key infrastructure for US national food safety system. California (2025) highlighted among success stories for state-level foodborne surveillance.
profile.access_model: Fully public and free. No registration needed for searching the Isolates Browser or downloading data. Data available via web interface, FTP bulk download, and NCBI Datasets REST API. GenomeTrakr lab submissions require NCBI BioProject registration. All data FAIR-compliant with open accession IDs. AMRFinderPlus available as standalone tool. No restrictions on data reuse or publication. US government open-data policy applies.

deep_research.executive_summary: NCBI Pathogen Detection is the backbone of US and international foodborne pathogen genomic surveillance, integrating >1M bacterial genomes from 800+ labs in 30+ countries. Score 88.9/100 reflects massive database, automated real-time clustering, and critical role in food safety. Strength is deep FDA/CDC integration. Limitation is US-centric governance and focus primarily on bacterial foodborne pathogens.

L2_Genomic.d: Largest repository of influenza and SARS-CoV-2 sequences

profile.overview: GISAID (Global Initiative on Sharing All Influenza Data) is the world's largest genomic data-sharing platform for respiratory pathogen sequences, established in 2008 as a non-profit (GISAID Initiative e.V., registered in Germany). Originally created for influenza sequences to overcome the reluctance of developing-country labs to share data via fully open databases. Expanded massively during COVID-19 to become the primary repository for SARS-CoV-2 sequences, accumulating >16 million SARS-CoV-2 genomes by early 2024. Also hosts sequences for RSV, hMPV, and other pathogens. Operates on a unique Data Access Agreement (DAA) model requiring acknowledgment of data generators.
profile.functional_scope: Genomic sequence database with submission, curation, and retrieval for influenza (all types), SARS-CoV-2, RSV, and hMPV. Analytical tools: FluSurver (influenza mutation analysis), CoVsurver (SARS-CoV-2 mutation analysis), EpiCoV (SARS-CoV-2-specific database), EpiFlu (influenza-specific database), AUDACITY (phylogenetic analysis), spike mutation comparisons. Variant tracking dashboards. Data feed agreements for approved downstream platforms. Clade/lineage nomenclature support (GISAID clades for SARS-CoV-2). Metadata includes collection date, location, patient demographics (age, sex), sequencing technology, originating/submitting lab.
profile.tech_stack: Custom proprietary web platform (not open-source). Java-based backend with custom database infrastructure. FluSurver and CoVsurver are integrated web tools. No public API; data access via web downloads, FASTA/metadata bulk downloads, and approved data feed agreements. Data hosted on servers in Germany and Singapore. Two-factor authentication for registered users. Proprietary accession system (EPI_ISL for SARS-CoV-2, EPI for influenza). GISAID maintains its own clade nomenclature system alongside Pango/Nextstrain.
profile.operator: GISAID Initiative e.V. (eingetragener Verein – registered non-profit association, Germany). President: Peter Bogner. Hosted by Freie Universitat Berlin. Founding partners include German government, WHO collaborating centres. Advisory board includes representatives from WHO, US CDC, China CDC, and other national agencies. Funded through a combination of government grants (German Federal Foreign Office), institutional partnerships, and operational support from Freie Universitat Berlin. Independent governance, not directly controlled by any single government or international body.
profile.data_model: Sequence-centric: consensus genomes (FASTA) with structured metadata including: accession (EPI_ISL_xxx), virus name, collection date, location (continent/country/region), host, patient age/sex, sequencing technology, originating lab, submitting lab, authors. Clade assignments (GISAID clades, Pango lineages, Nextstrain clades). Influenza: hemagglutinin/neuraminidase subtype classification. Quality metrics: coverage, completeness. Data Access Agreement governs reuse: must acknowledge data generators, cannot redistribute raw sequences without permission.
profile.users_scale: 500,000+ registered users from 200+ countries. >16 million SARS-CoV-2 sequences and >2 million influenza sequences deposited. Cited in >70,000 scientific publications. Used by WHO for global influenza surveillance (GISRS), WHO COVID-19 variant tracking, national public health agencies worldwide. Single largest genomic database for respiratory viruses. During pandemic peak, received >100,000 new SARS-CoV-2 sequences daily.
profile.access_model: Free registration required. Users must agree to GISAID Data Access Agreement (DAA): sequences can be analyzed and published with proper acknowledgment of data generators, but cannot be redistributed to non-GISAID users or deposited in open databases. Approved downstream platforms (e.g., Nextstrain, outbreak.info) received data feeds under special agreements (several terminated in 2025). No open API. Controversial restricted-access model: criticized by open-science advocates but defended as necessary to incentivize data sharing from countries with limited resources.

deep_research.executive_summary: GISAID is the world's largest respiratory virus genomic database with 16M+ SARS-CoV-2 and 2M+ influenza sequences, used by 500K+ registered users and cited 70K+ times. Score 88.5/100 reflects unmatched scale and global adoption but penalized for restricted access model and 2025 controversies. Major disruption: terminated data feeds to Nextstrain (Oct 2025) and outbreak.info (Jan 2025), sparking open-science backlash and threatening downstream surveillance platforms.

L2_Genomic.d: Open-source genomic epidemiology data visualization

profile.overview: Microreact is an open-source web application for interactive visualization and sharing of genomic epidemiology data, developed by the Centre for Genomic Pathogen Surveillance (CGPS), a partnership between the Wellcome Sanger Institute and Big Data Institute at University of Oxford. Launched in 2016, it provides linked views of phylogenetic trees, geographic maps, timelines, and metadata tables. Designed as a shareable endpoint for any bioinformatics pipeline that produces a phylogenetic tree. Used by UKHSA (UK Health Security Agency), ECDC, and public health labs worldwide for pathogen outbreak visualization and communication.
profile.functional_scope: Interactive visualization of phylogenetic trees linked to geographic maps (Mapbox/Leaflet), temporal timelines, and customizable metadata tables. Supports Newick/Nexus tree formats, CSV/TSV metadata, GeoJSON boundaries. Features: color/shape coding by any metadata field, tree filtering and zooming, map clustering, timeline animation, network views, block diagrams. Permanent shareable URLs for each project. Embeddable in publications. Data upload via drag-and-drop. No bioinformatics analysis performed (visualization only). API for programmatic access. Partnership with UKHSA for UK genomic surveillance dashboards. Used with Pathogenwatch and other CGPS tools.
profile.tech_stack: React.js frontend, Phylocanvas (tree rendering library, developed by CGPS), Mapbox GL JS / Leaflet for maps, D3.js for charts, deck.gl for large-scale visualization, Node.js backend, MongoDB for project storage, REST API, Docker deployment. Open-source on GitHub (microreact org) under MIT license. Hosted at microreact.org on cloud infrastructure (CGPS/Sanger). No heavy compute required (client-side rendering). Supports project export as PNG, SVG, and data download.
profile.operator: Centre for Genomic Pathogen Surveillance (CGPS), a collaboration between Wellcome Sanger Institute (Hinxton, UK) and Big Data Institute, University of Oxford. Led by David Aanensen. Funded by Wellcome Trust, Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, UK NIHR, and European Commission. Partnerships with UKHSA, ECDC, WHO, and national public health agencies.
profile.data_model: Project-based: each Microreact project contains a Newick/Nexus tree file, metadata CSV/TSV (rows = samples, columns = any metadata fields including geographic coordinates, dates, categorical/continuous values), and optional GeoJSON. No prescribed schema; any metadata fields accepted. Projects stored with unique URLs. Data remains with the user (server stores uploaded data for sharing). Supports large datasets (10,000+ tips). Export as JSON project file for portability.
profile.users_scale: Thousands of public projects hosted at microreact.org. Used by UKHSA for UK S. pneumoniae and other pathogen surveillance dashboards. ECDC uses Microreact for AMR surveillance visualization. Referenced in 500+ publications. Part of the JUNO project for S. pneumoniae global genomic surveillance. Key visualization tool for CGPS pathogen surveillance programs in LMICs. Used in training courses and workshops worldwide (WHO SEARO, Africa CDC).
profile.access_model: Free to use, no registration required for viewing public projects. Registration needed for creating and saving projects. Open-source under MIT license. API available for programmatic access. No data limits for uploads. Self-hosting possible via Docker. All public projects accessible via permanent URLs. Documentation at docs.microreact.org.

deep_research.executive_summary: Microreact is the leading open-source tool for interactive visualization of genomic epidemiology data, linking phylogenetic trees to maps and timelines. Score 87.5/100 reflects excellent visualization and ease of sharing but limited built-in analytics. Developed by CGPS (Wellcome Sanger/Oxford), it's used by UKHSA, ECDC, and WHO for pathogen surveillance communication. Companion to Pathogenwatch for analysis.

key_publications.title: Microreact: visualizing and sharing data for genomic epidemiology and phylogeography
key_publications.authors: Argimon S, Abudahab K, Goater R, et al.

controversies_and_changes: Visualization-only tool: does not perform bioinformatics analysis, requiring upstream pipelines.
Depends on CGPS funding (Wellcome/Gates) for long-term sustainability.
Next-generation Microreact ('next.microreact.org') being developed with enhanced features.
2024 release notes show continued active development (MLST scheme updates, new organisms).

ecosystem_connections.relationship: Sister tool from CGPS; Pathogenwatch analysis results can be viewed in Microreact
Complementary: Nextstrain for phylodynamic analysis, Microreact for shareable visualization
Uses Microreact for UK public health genomic surveillance dashboards
AMR surveillance visualization partner
Tree rendering library developed by CGPS, used in Microreact

Adopted by UKHSA and ECDC for surveillance dashboards
Expanded for COVID-19 genomic visualization; Pathogenwatch integration deepened
Continued updates: MLST schemes, new organisms, UI improvements
Next-generation Microreact in development

L2_Genomic.d: Genomic surveillance of bacterial pathogens

profile.overview: Pathogenwatch is a web-based platform for genomic surveillance and analysis of bacterial and some fungal pathogens, developed by the Centre for Genomic Pathogen Surveillance (CGPS), a collaboration between the Wellcome Sanger Institute and Big Data Institute at University of Oxford. Provides automated genome assembly analysis including species identification, multi-locus sequence typing (MLST), antimicrobial resistance (AMR) prediction, virulence factor detection, and phylogenetic clustering. Supports >100 bacterial species including priority WHO AMR pathogens (Klebsiella, Staphylococcus, Neisseria, Streptococcus, Salmonella, E. coli, etc.).
profile.functional_scope: Upload FASTA genome assemblies or raw reads for automated analysis: species identification, MLST typing (7-gene and cgMLST), AMR gene/mutation detection (using curated databases), virulence factor identification, core-genome phylogeny construction, population clustering, geographic mapping of isolates, temporal trend analysis, collection management. Supports >100 species with organism-specific typing schemes. Integration with Microreact for interactive visualization. Comparison against global reference datasets (e.g., 150,000+ Klebsiella public genomes). New AMR Watch module (amr.watch, 2025) for global AMR trend monitoring from 620,000+ genomes.
profile.tech_stack: Node.js/Express backend, React.js frontend, MongoDB for data storage, custom bioinformatics pipelines: Speciator (species ID), cgMLST engine, PAARSNP (AMR prediction), PopPUNK (population clustering), wgMLST analysis. Hosted on cloud infrastructure (CGPS/Sanger). Open-source components on GitHub (pathogenwatch org). Web-based with no software installation required. REST API for programmatic access. Docker for self-hosting. Next-generation Pathogenwatch in development (next.pathogen.watch).
profile.operator: Centre for Genomic Pathogen Surveillance (CGPS), Wellcome Sanger Institute & Big Data Institute, University of Oxford. Led by David Aanensen. Funded by Wellcome Trust, Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, NIHR (UK), European Commission, and WHO. Key partnerships: UKHSA, ECDC, WHO, Africa CDC, national reference laboratories in LMICs.
profile.data_model: Genome-centric: each uploaded assembly linked to metadata (species, collection date, location, source, patient info). Analysis outputs: MLST profile, AMR predictions (gene + phenotype), virulence factors, clustering membership, phylogenetic position. Collections group genomes for comparative analysis. Public genomes from NCBI integrated as reference context. Organism-specific databases: curated typing schemes from PubMLST, AMR databases from CARD/ResFinder. Supports metadata export as CSV/JSON.
profile.users_scale: Tens of thousands of registered users worldwide. Thousands of public collections hosted. Used by UKHSA for UK national genomic surveillance. ECDC partnership for EU AMR surveillance. WHO-supported deployment in LMICs for AMR monitoring. Key tool in JUNO project (Global Surveillance of S. pneumoniae). 620,700+ pathogen genomes with geotemporal data in AMR Watch (as of March 2025). Cited in 167+ publications (Argimon et al. 2021 alone). Training deployed through WHO SEARO, Africa CDC workshops.
profile.access_model: Free to use for web-based analysis. Registration required for saving collections and accessing advanced features. No software installation needed (browser-based). Open-source components available on GitHub (pathogenwatch-oss). Self-hosting possible via Docker. API access for programmatic queries. Public genomes freely browsable. No data limits for uploads. AMR Watch (amr.watch) publicly accessible. Documentation at next-docs.pathogen.watch. Supported by Wellcome/Gates funding – no commercial licensing.

deep_research.executive_summary: Pathogenwatch is the leading browser-based platform for automated bacterial genomic surveillance, offering AMR prediction, MLST typing, and phylogenetic clustering for 100+ species. Score 87/100 reflects comprehensive analysis capabilities, strong WHO/ECDC partnerships, and AMR focus. New amr.watch module (2025) expands to global AMR trend monitoring. Developed by CGPS (Wellcome Sanger/Oxford), companion tool to Microreact.

Organism coverage expanding but still limited compared to universal tools.

L2_Genomic.d: Accessible, reproducible computational research

profile.overview: Galaxy is an open-source, web-based platform for accessible, reproducible, and transparent computational research. Founded by Anton Nekrutenko (Penn State) and James Taylor (Johns Hopkins), it provides a browser-based environment for running bioinformatics analyses without programming knowledge.
profile.functional_scope: Over 10,000 integrated bioinformatics tools covering genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, metagenomics, and more. Workflow creation and sharing, interactive visualizations, Jupyter/RStudio notebook integration. Specialized COVID-19 analysis workflows, antimicrobial resistance analysis, and variant calling pipelines.
profile.tech_stack: Python backend (Galaxy framework), PostgreSQL database, Slurm/HTCondor job scheduling. Frontend in JavaScript/Backbone.js transitioning to Vue.js. Deployable on cloud (AWS, GCP, Azure), HPC, or local infrastructure via Ansible/Docker/Kubernetes. Tools wrapped in Galaxy XML tool definitions. Open-source under Academic Free License.
profile.operator: Galaxy Project community, led by teams at Penn State University, Johns Hopkins University, University of Freiburg, and University of Melbourne. Funded by NIH, NSF, and European Commission.
profile.data_model: History-based data management tracking complete analysis provenance. Supports any bioinformatics file format (FASTQ, BAM, VCF, etc.). Published workflows and datasets can be shared via DOIs.
profile.users_scale: 400,000+ registered users, 750,000+ jobs per month, 22,000+ citations, used in 150+ countries. Three major public servers: usegalaxy.org, usegalaxy.eu, usegalaxy.org.au.
profile.access_model: Free public servers with generous quotas. Self-hosted deployments for institutions. All code open-source.

L2_Genomic.d: Integrated bacterial and viral bioinformatics resource center (formerly PATRIC + IRD/ViPR)

profile.overview: The Bacterial and Viral Bioinformatics Resource Center (BV-BRC) is a comprehensive NIAID-funded resource for infectious disease research, combining the former PATRIC (bacterial) and IRD/ViPR (viral) databases into a unified platform launched in 2022.
profile.functional_scope: Integrated genomic and epidemiological data for bacteria and viruses. Provides genome assembly, annotation (RAST), comparative genomics, phylogenetics, protein family analysis, metabolic modeling, and outbreak tracking (Measles 2025, Mpox 2024, H5N1).
profile.tech_stack: Java/Solr backend, React frontend, RAST annotation engine, custom genome assembly pipelines, REST API, CLI tools. Hosted on NIAID cloud infrastructure with high-performance computing cluster.
profile.operator: University of Chicago (lead), Virginia Tech, Fellowship for Interpretation of Genomes. Funded by NIAID/NIH contract HHSN272201400027C.
profile.data_model: Integrated bacterial/viral genomes with structured metadata (host, geography, isolation source). Supports FASTA, GenBank, GFF3. Over 1.5M bacterial and 1M+ viral genomes indexed.
profile.users_scale: Thousands of registered researchers globally. Over 400 peer-reviewed publications cite PATRIC/BV-BRC. Serves NIH, CDC, USDA, academic labs worldwide.
profile.access_model: Free public access. Registration required for private workspace and analysis tools. Open-source components on GitHub. No usage limits for registered users.

L2_Genomic.d: Next-generation open pathogen sequence data sharing platform

profile.overview: Pathoplexus is a transparent, non-profit pathogen sequence data sharing platform founded by members from 14 countries across 5 continents. It offers flexible data-sharing options with time-limited protections for proper attribution, integrating smoothly with INSDC databases (NCBI, ENA, DDBJ).
profile.functional_scope: Sequence data sharing with flexible access models (open data or restricted-use with time-limited protections up to one year), filtering and searching of sequences, API for computational pipelines, integration with INSDC (open data appears in NCBI/ENA/DDBJ), pathogen-specific databases (currently supports multiple pathogens).
profile.tech_stack: Modern web platform with REST API, built in collaboration with PHA4GE (Public Health Alliance for Genomic Epidemiology). Demo instance for testing. Open-source development.
profile.operator: Pathoplexus Association (non-profit, registered association). Executive Board from around the globe. Community-driven with no major corporate influence.
profile.data_model: Genomic sequences with metadata, submitted per pathogen. Data can be Open (immediately accessible, forwarded to INSDC) or Restricted-Use (protected for up to 1 year with attribution requirements).
profile.users_scale: Growing community of researchers and public health institutions. Launched as an alternative/complement to GISAID with focus on transparency and flexibility.
profile.access_model: Free. Account required for submission. All data accessible to everyone (browsing/analysis), but Restricted-Use data has publication restrictions during the protection period.

L2_Genomic.d: Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database with Resistance Gene Identifier

profile.overview: The Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database (CARD) and Resistance Gene Identifier (RGI) are developed by the McArthur Lab at McMaster University, Canada. CARD is the gold-standard curated AMR reference database, integrated with the Antibiotic Resistance Ontology (ARO).
profile.functional_scope: Curated AMR gene sequences, point mutations, and resistance mechanisms organized via the ARO ontology. RGI provides sequence-based AMR prediction using BLAST, Hidden Markov Models, and curated detection models with defined bitscores. Parallel classification by Drug Class, Resistance Mechanism, and AMR Gene Family.
profile.tech_stack: Python-based RGI tool, BLAST+, HMMER3, DIAMOND. ARO ontology in OWL/OBO format. PostgreSQL database backend, Django web interface. Available via Bioconda, Docker, Galaxy. Source on GitHub (arpcard).
profile.operator: McArthur Lab, McMaster University, Hamilton, Ontario, Canada. Funded by Genome Canada, CIHR, NSERC, and Ontario Genomics.
profile.data_model: Antibiotic Resistance Ontology (ARO) with gene families, drug classes, resistance mechanisms, and detection models. Curated reference sequences with bitscores. Monthly releases with changelog. Integrated with Genomic Epidemiology Ontology and OBO Foundry.
profile.users_scale: Among the most-cited AMR databases globally with 3,000+ citations. Used by NCBI Pathogen Detection, WHO GLASS partners, public health labs, and thousands of academic researchers. Integrated into IRIDA, Galaxy, and major bioinformatics pipelines.
profile.access_model: Free and open-source. Web interface at card.mcmaster.ca. RGI available via pip, Bioconda, Docker. Database downloadable in JSON/FASTA formats. CC-BY-4.0 license.

st: HL7 FHIR-compliant interoperability for health data exchange
Multi-laboratory network support with centralized management
L2_Genomic.d: Open-source laboratory information system for managing lab workflows, sample tracking, and result reporting with HL7/FHIR interoperability for public health laboratories

profile.overview: OpenELIS Global is the leading open-source Laboratory Information System (LIS) for public health laboratory networks. Originally developed by the University of Washington with CDC support, it manages the complete laboratory workflow from sample collection to result reporting with enterprise-grade security.
profile.functional_scope: Complete laboratory workflow management: patient registration, sample collection and tracking, work plans, result entry, validation, and reporting. Analyzer integration (bidirectional instrument interfaces), quality control, inventory management, reporting dashboards. Native FHIR R4 interoperability for connecting to EMRs, client registries, and health information exchanges.
profile.tech_stack: Java/Spring Boot backend, React.js frontend, PostgreSQL database. HL7 FHIR R4 compliant. Bidirectional analyzer interfaces for laboratory instruments. Docker deployment. REST APIs. Open-source under Mozilla Public License.
profile.operator: OpenELIS Global community, supported by University of Washington, CDC, and I-TECH (International Training and Education Center for Health). Implementation partners in multiple countries.
profile.data_model: Relational model covering patients, samples, tests, results, quality control, and inventory. FHIR resources for interoperability. HL7v2 messaging for instrument communication.
profile.users_scale: 1,000+ labs deployed across 20+ countries, supporting 18.7+ million patients. 15+ years of proven deployment.
profile.access_model: Free, open-source (zero licensing cost). Self-hosted with Docker. Role-based access control. Demo instance available.

L2_Genomic.d: Comprehensive biosurveillance combining genomic and epidemiological intelligence

profile.overview: Ginkgo Biosecurity (formerly Concentric by Ginkgo) is a biosurveillance division of Ginkgo Bioworks, a leading synthetic biology company. Launched during COVID-19 to provide pathogen monitoring through wastewater and environmental surveillance.
profile.functional_scope: Integrated biosurveillance combining genomic sequencing, metagenomic analysis, wastewater monitoring, and epidemiological intelligence. Provides early warning detection, variant tracking, and risk assessment for biological threats including natural pandemics and engineered agents.
profile.tech_stack: Ginkgo Bioworks' foundry platform for high-throughput sequencing. Metagenomic pipelines, custom bioinformatics, cloud analytics. Integrates with existing public health laboratory infrastructure.
profile.operator: Ginkgo Biosecurity (division of Ginkgo Bioworks Inc.), Boston, MA, USA. Backed by $350M+ in biosecurity-specific contracts from CDC, BARDA, and DoD.
profile.data_model: Wastewater pathogen concentration data, genomic sequences, variant frequencies. Structured surveillance reports with geospatial context. Integration with CDC and state/local public health systems.
profile.users_scale: Contracted by CDC's national wastewater program, serving hundreds of wastewater treatment facilities. Also serves DoD, BARDA, international governments. School and airport surveillance programs.
profile.access_model: Government/enterprise contract model. Public health agencies access through federal contracts. No free public tier. Results shared with contracting agencies and integrated into national surveillance.

L2_Genomic.d: CDC Traveler-based Genomic Surveillance for early detection of novel variants

profile.overview: CDC's Travelers' Genomic Surveillance (TGS) program monitors international travelers arriving at U.S. airports for emerging infectious diseases through voluntary nasal swab testing and genomic sequencing. It serves as an early warning system for detecting novel variants and pathogens before they spread domestically.
profile.functional_scope: Voluntary sampling of arriving international travelers, PCR testing for respiratory pathogens, whole genome sequencing of positive samples for variant identification, genomic analysis and reporting to CDC surveillance teams, tracking of variant importation patterns by travel origin.
profile.tech_stack: Nasal swab collection kits, RT-qPCR testing, whole genome sequencing (Illumina), bioinformatics pipelines for variant calling and lineage assignment (Pangolin, Nextclade), CDC data reporting systems.
profile.operator: U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), in partnership with XpresCheck and airport authorities.
profile.data_model: Traveler demographic data (anonymized), travel origin, sample collection date, pathogen detection results, genomic sequence data, variant classification.
profile.users_scale: Operates at multiple major U.S. international airports (JFK, SFO, ATL, IAD, etc.). Screens tens of thousands of travelers annually.
profile.access_model: Voluntary participation by travelers. Data used internally by CDC. Aggregate findings published in MMWR and peer-reviewed journals.

L2_Genomic.d: Flexible pipeline for complete analysis of bacterial genomes using Nextflow
wk: Command-line interface only; no web GUI limits accessibility for non-technical users

profile.overview: Bactopia is a flexible, portable Nextflow pipeline for the complete analysis of bacterial genomes, developed by Robert Petit at the Wyoming Public Health Laboratory. First released in 2020 and actively maintained.
profile.functional_scope: End-to-end bacterial genomics: quality control, assembly (Shovill/SPAdes), annotation (Prokka/Bakta), MLST, AMR detection (AMRFinderPlus), variant calling, pangenome analysis, phylogenetics. 50+ Bactopia Tools for extended analyses including serotyping, plasmid detection, and population genomics.
profile.tech_stack: Nextflow workflow manager with Conda/Docker/Singularity execution. Python tools. Integrates 60+ bioinformatics tools. Available via Bioconda. Tested CI/CD via GitHub Actions.
profile.operator: Robert Petit (Wyoming Public Health Lab), with contributions from Tim Read Lab (Emory University). Open-source community project on GitHub.
profile.data_model: Standard bioinformatics formats: FASTQ input, FASTA assemblies, GFF annotations, VCF variants. Structured output directories per sample. Summary reports in TSV/JSON.
profile.users_scale: Hundreds of citations in peer-reviewed literature. Used by public health laboratories, academic microbiology labs, and surveillance networks globally. Active GitHub community.
profile.access_model: Fully open-source under MIT license. Available via Bioconda, Docker Hub, GitHub. Free to use. Comprehensive documentation at bactopia.github.io.

L2_Genomic.c: NCBI AMR Gene & Point Mutation Detection
L2_Genomic.d: NCBI tool for identifying antimicrobial resistance genes and mutations
wk: Command-line interface reduces accessibility for epidemiologists without coding skills

profile.overview: AMRFinderPlus is NCBI's flagship tool for identifying antimicrobial resistance genes, resistance-associated point mutations, and select virulence/stress genes. Developed by the NCBI Pathogen Detection team and integrated into their production pipeline processing hundreds of thousands of isolates.
profile.functional_scope: Identifies AMR genes via protein/nucleotide BLAST against curated Reference Gene Database. Detects resistance-associated point mutations. Classifies hits by drug class, resistance mechanism, and gene family. Also identifies stress response and virulence genes. Runs on assembled genomes or protein annotations.
profile.tech_stack: C++ core with BLAST+ and HMMER3 (NCBIfam-AMRFinder HMMs). Curated reference database updated with each release. Available via Bioconda, Docker, Galaxy. Source code on GitHub (ncbi/amr). MicroBIGG-E web interface for results.
profile.operator: National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI), National Library of Medicine, NIH, Bethesda, MD, USA.
profile.data_model: Curated reference gene sequences in FASTA with hierarchical ontology. Detection models with bitscores. Output: TSV with gene name, allele, drug class, mechanism, element type, method, coverage. Integrated with NCBI Pathogen Detection Isolates Browser.
profile.users_scale: Runs on 800,000+ bacterial/fungal isolates in NCBI Pathogen Detection. Used by CDC, FDA, PulseNet, USDA, and labs worldwide. Thousands of academic citations. Successor to legacy tools like ABRicate.
profile.access_model: Completely free and open-source (public domain). Command-line tool, web interface via Isolates Browser, API access. Database freely downloadable. No restrictions.

wk: High commercial licensing cost limits adoption in resource-constrained settings
L2_Genomic.d: Commercial bioinformatics software suite for microbial whole-genome sequencing, typing, epidemiological clustering, and phylogenetic analysis used by public health labs worldwide

profile.overview: BioNumerics is a comprehensive commercial bioinformatics platform developed by Applied Maths (now bioMerieux) for microbial genomics, molecular typing, and epidemiological analysis. It is the industry standard for public health and food safety laboratories performing pathogen characterization.
profile.functional_scope: Whole genome sequence analysis (assembly, annotation, SNP calling), molecular typing (MLST, cgMLST, wgMLST, PFGE, rep-PCR, MALDI-TOF), phylogenetic analysis, cluster detection, epidemiological mapping, AMR gene detection, and outbreak investigation. Integrated database management with audit trails and compliance support.
profile.tech_stack: Windows desktop application with client-server architecture. Proprietary algorithms for sequence analysis. Oracle/SQL Server database backend. Calculation Engine for distributed processing. REST API for integration.
profile.operator: bioMerieux (acquired Applied Maths in 2018). Headquarters in Sint-Martens-Latem, Belgium.
profile.data_model: Relational database model linking molecular typing data (fingerprints, sequences, profiles) with epidemiological metadata (source, date, location, clinical data). Supports import from most sequencing platforms.
profile.users_scale: Used by 1,000+ laboratories worldwide including CDC, FDA, ECDC, PHE, and national reference laboratories. Standard tool for PulseNet International partners.
profile.access_model: Commercial license (per-seat and server pricing). Free evaluation available. Academic pricing. Enterprise licensing for public health networks.

L2_Genomic.d: Global network for malaria genomic epidemiology with large-scale genetic data resources
st: Open data access policy enabling broad research use

profile.overview: MalariaGEN (Malaria Genomic Epidemiology Network) is a global research network studying the genomics of malaria parasites, mosquito vectors, and human hosts. Founded in 2005, now based at the Liverpool School of Tropical Medicine (LSTM), formerly at the Wellcome Sanger Institute.
profile.functional_scope: Large-scale genomic epidemiology for malaria: Plasmodium falciparum (Pf8 dataset: 33,000+ WGS), P. vivax (Pv4: 1,895 samples), Anopheles mosquito genomics (15,000+ samples from 26 countries). Drug resistance tracking, population genetics, and evolutionary analysis.
profile.tech_stack: Illumina whole-genome sequencing. Custom bioinformatics pipelines for variant calling (GATK). Interactive data portals. Partner Portal (Conflux) for collaborator access. Cloud computing infrastructure.
profile.operator: MalariaGEN Coordinating Centre at Liverpool School of Tropical Medicine (LSTM), UK. Previously at Wellcome Sanger Institute. Funded by Wellcome Trust, Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, MRC.
profile.data_model: Whole-genome sequence data with structured metadata (location, date, drug resistance phenotypes). VCF variant files, BAM alignments. Parasite, vector, and human host datasets. Open data releases with version control (Pf8, Pv4, Ag3).
profile.users_scale: Collaborators across 40+ malaria-endemic countries. Datasets used in 500+ publications. Serves WHO, national malaria control programs, and hundreds of research groups.
profile.access_model: Free access to processed data via malariagen.net. Raw sequence data in ENA/SRA. Partner Portal for collaborators. Data sharing agreements required for some datasets. Open-access publications.

L2_Genomic.d: Large-scale web platform for automated MLST/cgMLST genotyping and population-level genomic analysis of bacterial pathogens including Salmonella, E. coli, and Clostridioides

profile.overview: EnteroBase is a web-based platform for the genomic epidemiology of enteric pathogens, developed by Mark Achtman's group at the University of Warwick (now hosted with DSMZ for Mycobacterium). It assembles, stores, and provides integrated analysis of bacterial genomic data at massive scale.
profile.functional_scope: Automated genome assembly from raw reads (SRA), MLST typing (7-gene, cgMLST, wgMLST, rMLST), Hierarchical Clustering (HierCC) for population structure, SNP-based phylogenetics, GrapeTree visualization (minimum spanning trees), metadata search and filtering. Covers Salmonella, E. coli/Shigella, Clostridioides, Vibrio, Yersinia, Helicobacter, Moraxella, Streptococcus, and M. tuberculosis.
profile.tech_stack: Custom web application (Python/Flask), PostgreSQL database, automated assembly pipeline (Velvet, SPAdes), custom MLST calling engine, GrapeTree (JavaScript MST visualization). High-performance computing cluster for genome assembly. REST API.
profile.operator: University of Warwick (Mark Achtman lab) for most species; DSMZ (Leibniz Institute) for M. tuberculosis. Funded by Wellcome Trust and BBSRC.
profile.data_model: Over 1.65 million bacterial strains with assembled genomes, MLST profiles (multiple schemes per species), HierCC clusters, and metadata (source, country, date, serovar). Linked to SRA, BioSample, and PubMLST.
profile.users_scale: Contains 1,656,331+ strains. Used by thousands of researchers and public health agencies globally for tracking Salmonella, E. coli, and other enteric pathogen outbreaks.
profile.access_model: Free, web-based access. Login required for querying and analysis. Data downloads available. API for programmatic access.

L2_Genomic.d: DTU/Novo Nordisk Foundation funded Global Pathogen Analysis Platform providing free AI-based genomic analytical toolkit for scientists and public health authorities worldwide

profile.overview: The Global Pathogen Analysis Platform (GPAP) is a web-based bioinformatics resource developed by the Technical University of Denmark (DTU) National Food Institute, funded by the Novo Nordisk Foundation's Genomics Challenge Programme.
profile.functional_scope: Automated analysis of bacterial pathogen genomes for public health surveillance: species identification, MLST/cgMLST typing, AMR prediction (ResFinder), virulence gene detection, plasmid identification, phylogenetic analysis. Focused on food safety and One Health surveillance.
profile.tech_stack: Web-based platform integrating DTU's Center for Genomic Epidemiology (CGE) tools: ResFinder, MLST, VirulenceFinder, PlasmidFinder, KmerFinder. Nextflow pipelines. Docker containers. REST API.
profile.operator: Technical University of Denmark (DTU), National Food Institute, Center for Genomic Epidemiology. Funded by Novo Nordisk Foundation Challenge Programme.
profile.data_model: Bacterial genome assemblies (FASTA) and raw reads (FASTQ) as input. Structured output: species ID, sequence types, resistance genes, virulence factors, plasmid replicons. Standardized reports in JSON/TSV.
profile.users_scale: Used by European food safety laboratories, WHO collaborating centres, ECDC, and national reference labs in 30+ countries. Part of the CGE ecosystem serving thousands of users monthly.
profile.access_model: Free web-based access at gpap.global. Registration required for analysis. Open-source tool components. No cost for academic or public health use.

L2_Genomic.d: Web-based tool for identification of acquired antimicrobial resistance genes

profile.overview: ResFinder is a web-based tool for identification of acquired antimicrobial resistance genes in bacterial whole genome sequence data, developed by the Center for Genomic Epidemiology (CGE) at DTU, Denmark. One of the most widely used AMR detection tools globally.
profile.functional_scope: Identifies acquired AMR genes from assembled genomes or raw reads using curated database of known resistance genes. Reports gene identity, coverage, drug class affected. Complemented by PointFinder (chromosomal mutations) and DisinFinder (disinfectant resistance).
profile.tech_stack: Python backend, BLAST-based sequence alignment. Web interface built on CGE server infrastructure. Available as command-line tool, Docker container, and web service. REST API. Database updated regularly.
profile.operator: Center for Genomic Epidemiology (CGE), DTU National Food Institute, Technical University of Denmark, Lyngby, Denmark.
profile.data_model: Curated database of acquired resistance gene sequences organized by drug class. Input: FASTA/FASTQ files. Output: gene matches with percent identity, coverage, drug class, and phenotype predictions. Tab-separated results files.
profile.users_scale: Millions of analyses performed. Cited in 2,000+ publications. Used by WHO GLASS network, ECDC, national reference laboratories, and academic researchers globally. Key tool in European genomic surveillance.
profile.access_model: Free web access at cge.food.dtu.dk. Command-line version available on GitHub. Database downloadable. No registration required for basic web analysis. Open-source under Apache 2.0.

L2_Genomic.d: Canada's integrated rapid infectious disease analysis platform connecting genomic data, epidemiological metadata, and analytical pipelines for public health genomics

profile.overview: IRIDA (Integrated Rapid Infectious Disease Analysis) is an open-source bioinformatics platform designed to make genomic epidemiology accessible to public health workers, epidemiologists, and clinical microbiologists. Developed primarily by the National Microbiology Laboratory of Canada.
profile.functional_scope: Sequencing data management (upload, organize, share), integrated bioinformatics pipelines for public health genomics (SNVPhyl for SNP phylogenetics, SISTR for Salmonella typing, refseq_masher for species identification, assembly/annotation), metadata management with genomic epidemiology ontology, data visualization (phylogenetic trees, metadata overlays), and REST API for external tool integration.
profile.tech_stack: Java/Spring Boot backend, AngularJS frontend, Galaxy for pipeline execution, PostgreSQL database. Docker deployment. REST API. Open-source on GitHub under Apache 2.0 license.
profile.operator: Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC) / National Microbiology Laboratory (NML), in collaboration with Simon Fraser University. Funded by PHAC and Genome Canada.
profile.data_model: Project-based organization with samples, sequence files (FASTQ), metadata, and analysis results. Ontology-based metadata harmonization linking genomic, epidemiological, lab, and clinical data.
profile.users_scale: Deployed in Canadian public health laboratories and international partners. Public demonstration instance hosted by Simon Fraser University.
profile.access_model: Free, open-source. Self-hosted deployment. Role-based access control with project-level sharing.

L2_Genomic.d: Commercial cloud platform for microbial identification and AMR analysis

profile.overview: One Codex is a commercial cloud-based metagenomics analysis platform founded in 2014, specializing in microbial identification and antimicrobial resistance analysis for clinical, pharmaceutical, and research applications.
profile.functional_scope: Metagenomic classification using curated database of 130,000+ microbial genomes. Species identification, AMR gene detection, microbiome profiling, pathogen detection from clinical samples. Supports shotgun metagenomics, 16S, and targeted panels. CLIA-validated workflows for clinical use.
profile.tech_stack: Proprietary k-mer based classification algorithms. Curated reference database (One Codex Database). Cloud computing infrastructure (AWS). RESTful API. Web dashboard and Python CLI. Jupyter notebook integration.
profile.operator: One Codex (Reference Genomics Inc.), San Francisco, CA, USA. VC-backed. Partnerships with clinical labs, pharma companies, and academic institutions.
profile.data_model: Taxonomic classification results with abundance estimates. AMR gene presence/absence. Sample metadata. FASTQ/FASTA input. JSON/CSV output. Interactive visualizations (Krona charts, heatmaps, PCA).
profile.users_scale: Used by top academic labs, biotech companies developing live biotherapeutics (LBPs), and clinical laboratories. Customers include research institutions, pharmaceutical companies, and diagnostic labs.
profile.access_model: Commercial SaaS platform with tiered pricing. Free tier for academic/small-scale use (limited analyses). Enterprise plans for clinical/pharma. API access included. CLIA-validated option for clinical laboratories.

L2_Genomic.d: Platform for managing Nextflow bioinformatics pipelines at scale (formerly Nextflow Tower)
st: Horizontally scalable architecture supporting growing data volumes

profile.overview: Seqera Platform (formerly Nextflow Tower) is the commercial workflow orchestration platform built by Seqera Labs, the creators of the Nextflow workflow language. Provides enterprise-grade management of bioinformatics pipelines at scale.
profile.functional_scope: Pipeline orchestration and monitoring for bioinformatics workflows. Manages Nextflow pipelines across cloud (AWS, GCP, Azure) and on-premises HPC infrastructure. Provides data management (Data Studios), compute environments, workflow launchpad, run monitoring, and team collaboration. AI agents for scientific workflows.
profile.tech_stack: Built on Nextflow (Groovy/Java). Supports AWS Batch, Google Life Sciences, Azure Batch, Slurm, PBS, LSF, SGE. Kubernetes deployment. REST API. Web UI (React). Integrates with nf-core community pipelines (100+ validated workflows).
profile.operator: Seqera Labs, Barcelona, Spain. Founded by Paolo Di Tommaso (Nextflow creator). Backed by $50M+ in funding. Partners include Roche, AstraZeneca, Moderna, Sanger Institute, Oxford Nanopore.
profile.data_model: Workflow definitions (Nextflow DSL2), pipeline parameters, compute environment configs, run metadata, task-level resource consumption. Supports all bioinformatics data formats through pipeline encapsulation.
profile.users_scale: Used by world's leading scientific teams: Roche, AstraZeneca, Moderna, Sanger Institute, SciLifeLab, CRG. Nextflow has 5,000+ GitHub stars and is the most widely used scientific workflow manager.
profile.access_model: Commercial SaaS (Seqera Cloud) and self-hosted (Seqera Enterprise) options. Free community tier for small teams. Enterprise licensing for large organizations. Nextflow itself is open-source (Apache 2.0).

L2_Genomic.d: Cloud-based, no-code genomic pathogen surveillance platform from Israel

profile.overview: Solu Genomics (now Solu) is an Israeli cloud-based genomic pathogen surveillance platform providing no-code bioinformatics tools for public health laboratories and researchers.
profile.functional_scope: Cloud-based no-code platform for genomic pathogen surveillance. Automated analysis pipelines for pathogen identification, lineage assignment, phylogenetic analysis, AMR detection, and outbreak investigation. Supports SARS-CoV-2, TB, foodborne pathogens, and other infectious agents.
profile.tech_stack: Cloud-native SaaS platform. Automated bioinformatics pipelines (assembly, mapping, variant calling). Web-based interface with drag-and-drop functionality. API integration. No local computational requirements.
profile.operator: Solu Genomics Ltd., Israel. Startup backed by venture capital. Partners with national public health agencies and international organizations.
profile.data_model: FASTQ sequence reads as input. Automated processing to assemblies, variant calls, lineage assignments, phylogenetic trees. Structured reports with resistance profiles and epidemiological context.
profile.users_scale: Deployed in public health laboratories across multiple countries. Used by national reference laboratories for routine surveillance. Focused on low-resource settings needing accessible bioinformatics.
profile.access_model: Commercial SaaS subscription model. Institutional pricing for public health labs. Free trial/demo available. Cloud-based (no local installation required).

L2_Genomic.d: Interactive exploration of extremely large phylogenetic trees in the browser

profile.overview: Taxonium is an open-source web-based tool for exploring extremely large phylogenetic trees, developed by Theo Sanderson at the Francis Crick Institute/Wellcome Sanger Institute. Capable of rendering trees with millions of nodes interactively in the browser.
profile.functional_scope: Interactive exploration of massive phylogenetic trees (millions of tips). Search by metadata, zoom/pan, color by attributes, time-scaled views. Supports SARS-CoV-2 global trees (USHER/UShER), bacterial phylogenies, and any Newick/Nexus tree with metadata.
profile.tech_stack: WebGL-based rendering for performance with millions of nodes. React frontend, Node.js backend. Supports Newick, Nexus, and UShER protobuf tree formats. Deployed on Vercel. Open-source on GitHub (theosanderson/taxonium).
profile.operator: Theo Sanderson, Francis Crick Institute / Wellcome Sanger Institute, London, UK. Community open-source project.
profile.data_model: Phylogenetic tree structures (Newick/Nexus/protobuf) with associated metadata (CSV/TSV). Supports date, location, lineage, and custom annotations. Client-side rendering with server-side data processing for large trees.
profile.users_scale: Widely used for SARS-CoV-2 phylogenetics (6M+ sequence global tree). Used by genomic epidemiologists, public health agencies, and researchers. Taxonium.org public instance for SARS-CoV-2.
profile.access_model: Free and open-source (MIT license). Public instance at taxonium.org. Self-hostable. No registration required. Upload your own trees or explore public datasets.

L2_Genomic.d: Ultra-fast taxonomic classification and abundance estimation for metagenomic data
wk: Command-line interface only; no web GUI limits accessibility for non-technical users

profile.overview: Kraken2 is an ultrafast taxonomic classification system for metagenomic sequences, developed by Derrick Wood and Steven Salzberg at Johns Hopkins University. Bracken (Bayesian Reestimation of Abundance with KrakEN) provides abundance estimation from Kraken2 output.
profile.functional_scope: Taxonomic classification of DNA/RNA sequences against reference databases using exact k-mer matching. Classifies reads to species/genus/family level in seconds. Bracken re-estimates species-level abundance using Bayesian statistics. Supports custom database building.
profile.tech_stack: C++ implementation with memory-mapped hash tables. Minimizer-based k-mer indexing (16GB standard database, 8GB mini). Bracken in Python. Available via Bioconda, Docker. Source on GitHub (DerrickWood/kraken2). Integration with Galaxy, QIIME2.
profile.operator: Center for Computational Biology (CCB), Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, MD, USA. Derrick Wood, Steven Salzberg lab. Open-source community contributions.
profile.data_model: Input: FASTQ/FASTA reads. K-mer based lookup against reference databases (RefSeq complete genomes, NCBI nt). Output: per-read classification, Krona-compatible reports, species abundance tables. MPA-style reports for downstream tools.
profile.users_scale: One of the most-cited metagenomics tools globally (10,000+ citations). Used by virtually every metagenomics research group. Integrated into clinical metagenomics pipelines, CDC workflows, and thousands of research projects.
profile.access_model: Completely free and open-source (MIT license). Pre-built databases available for download. Bioconda, Docker, and source installation. No restrictions. Standard reference databases hosted at Johns Hopkins.

L2_Genomic.d: UK Health Security Agency's national-scale bioinformatics pipeline for standardized processing of pathogen whole-genome sequencing data across England

profile.overview: The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA, formerly Public Health England/PHE) national-scale bioinformatics pipeline for standardized genomic analysis of pathogens. Powers the UK's world-leading genomic surveillance infrastructure.
profile.functional_scope: National-scale standardized bioinformatics for pathogen genomic surveillance: assembly, mapping, variant calling, typing (MLST, cgMLST), AMR detection, phylogenetics, and cluster detection. Supports surveillance of SARS-CoV-2, TB, Salmonella, E. coli, Listeria, and dozens of other pathogens.
profile.tech_stack: Nextflow-based pipelines running on UKHSA cloud/HPC infrastructure. Integrates ARIBA, Snippy, SPAdes, Trimmomatic, BWA, SAMtools. SnapperDB for SNP database clustering. Internal pipeline registry. Integration with CLIMB cloud computing.
profile.operator: UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA), London, UK. Part of the Department of Health and Social Care. Genomic services division.
profile.data_model: Standardized output per pathogen: assemblies, VCFs, typing results, cluster assignments. Integration with UKHSA's SGSS (Second Generation Surveillance System). Submits to ENA/INSDC. Structured reports for public health action.
profile.users_scale: Processes all pathogen genomic data for England (population 56M). During COVID-19, processed 3M+ SARS-CoV-2 genomes (more than any other country per capita). Serves NHS labs, PHE reference laboratories.
profile.access_model: Internal UKHSA system. Some pipeline components open-sourced on GitHub. Data submissions to public archives (ENA). Aggregate surveillance data published through GOV.UK. Not directly accessible to external users.

L2_Genomic.d: CDC pipeline for whole-genome sequencing analysis of bacterial isolates
wk: Command-line interface only; no web GUI limits accessibility for non-technical users

profile.overview: PHoeNIx (Public Health bioinformaticsvNIx) is CDC's production WGS analysis pipeline for bacterial pathogens, hosted on GitHub under CDCgov. Standardizes genomic analysis for public health surveillance and outbreak response.
profile.functional_scope: Complete bacterial WGS analysis: quality control, de novo assembly, species identification, MLST/cgMLST typing, AMR detection (AMRFinderPlus), virulence gene identification, and phylogenetic analysis. Supports food-borne, healthcare-associated, and emerging pathogens.
profile.tech_stack: Nextflow pipeline with Docker/Singularity containers. Integrates SPAdes, Trimmomatic, AMRFinderPlus, MLST, Kraken2. Runs on HPC and cloud. CDC's internal production pipeline. Open-source on GitHub.
profile.operator: CDC Division of Healthcare Quality Promotion and other CDC divisions. CDC Advanced Molecular Detection (AMD) program.
profile.data_model: FASTQ input, structured output with QC metrics, assembly stats, typing results (MLST, cgMLST), AMR genes, species ID. TSV/JSON reports. Integration with CDC's internal surveillance databases.
profile.users_scale: Used by CDC for routine national surveillance. Adopted by state public health labs via AMD program. Open-source for international adoption.
profile.access_model: Open-source on GitHub (CDCgov/phoenix). Free to download, use, and modify. No registration. MIT-like public domain license. CDC provides documentation and training.

L2_Genomic.d: WDL workflows for genomic characterization and surveillance (TheiaCoV, TheiaProk, TheiaEuk)

profile.overview: Theiagen Public Health Bioinformatics (PHB) is a collection of WDL (Workflow Description Language) workflows developed by Theiagen Genomics for genomic characterization and surveillance of pathogens in public health laboratories.
profile.functional_scope: WDL workflows for: SARS-CoV-2, TB, influenza, RSV, and bacterial pathogen analysis. Includes assembly, mapping, lineage assignment (Pangolin, Nextclade), AMR detection, quality control, and data submission pipelines. Runs on Terra.bio cloud platform.
profile.tech_stack: WDL (Workflow Description Language) workflows. Cromwell execution engine. Terra.bio cloud platform. Docker containers. Integrates Nextclade, Pangolin, AMRFinderPlus, VADR. Open-source on GitHub.
profile.operator: Theiagen Genomics, Highlands Ranch, CO, USA. Social enterprise supporting public health labs. Partnerships with CDC, state health departments, and international agencies.
profile.data_model: FASTQ/FASTA input. Structured output: lineage assignments, QC metrics, AMR profiles, assembly statistics. Terra.bio data tables. Integrated data submission to NCBI/GISAID.
profile.users_scale: Used by 50+ US state/territorial public health labs and international partners. Key tool in CDC AMD program. Thousands of samples processed weekly during COVID-19.
profile.access_model: Open-source on GitHub (theiagen/public_health_bioinformatics). Free to use. Runs on Terra.bio (free tier available for public health). Professional support available from Theiagen.

wk: Primarily US-focused network with limited international participation
L2_Genomic.d: CDC's national molecular subtyping network using whole-genome sequencing to detect and investigate foodborne disease outbreaks across all US states

profile.overview: PulseNet is CDC's national laboratory network for detecting foodborne, waterborne, and One Health-related disease outbreaks through molecular surveillance. Established in 1996, it uses DNA fingerprinting (now primarily whole genome sequencing) to connect illness cases and identify outbreaks across the United States and internationally.
profile.functional_scope: Whole genome sequencing (WGS) of foodborne pathogens, SNP-based clustering for outbreak detection, allele-based typing (cgMLST), real-time comparison of isolate fingerprints across the network, outbreak investigation support, and antimicrobial resistance monitoring. PulseNet International coordinates similar activities globally across 86 countries.
profile.tech_stack: WGS-based surveillance using Illumina sequencing. Bioinformatics pipeline: NCBI Pathogen Detection for clustering, BioNumerics for legacy data. Cloud-based data sharing via NCBI. PFGE (legacy) and WGS databases.
profile.operator: U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), with 83 participating state and local public health laboratories. PulseNet International extends to 86 countries.
profile.data_model: Isolate data: organism, WGS/PFGE fingerprint, patient demographics (anonymized), food source, date, location. Clusters defined by SNP distance thresholds. Integrated with NCBI Pathogen Detection trees.
profile.users_scale: Detects ~1,750+ clusters annually in the U.S. Credited with preventing an estimated 270,000+ illnesses per year. All 50 U.S. states plus territories participate.
profile.access_model: Network access for participating public health laboratories. Cluster data shared with epidemiologists via secure systems. Summary data publicly available through CDC.

L2_Genomic.d: Nanopore sequencing protocols and bioinformatics pipelines for outbreak genomics

profile.overview: The ARTIC Network provides open-source nanopore sequencing protocols and bioinformatics pipelines for real-time genomic epidemiology of outbreak pathogens. Founded in 2017 by Nick Loman (Birmingham), Andrew Rambaut (Edinburgh), and others.
profile.functional_scope: Tiling amplicon schemes and sequencing protocols for rapid pathogen genome sequencing using Oxford Nanopore Technology. Covers SARS-CoV-2, Ebola, Zika, Mpox, and other outbreak pathogens. Bioinformatics pipeline (fieldbioinformatics) for consensus genome generation.
profile.tech_stack: Oxford Nanopore MinION/GridION sequencing. Primal Scheme for primer design. Medaka/Nanopolish for variant calling. Minimap2 for mapping. Python pipeline (fieldbioinformatics). GitHub-hosted protocols.
profile.operator: Academic consortium: University of Birmingham (Nick Loman), University of Edinburgh (Andrew Rambaut), Fred Hutch, and others. Funded by Wellcome Trust, ARTIC consortium.
profile.data_model: Amplicon scheme BED files, nanopore FAST5/FASTQ reads, consensus FASTA sequences with quality metrics. Integration with GISAID/INSDC for data sharing.
profile.users_scale: Used globally during COVID-19 (millions of SARS-CoV-2 genomes generated using ARTIC protocols). Deployed in 100+ countries. Standard protocol for WHO and national surveillance programs.
profile.access_model: Fully open-source. Protocols at artic.network and GitHub. Free primer schemes. Community-supported. No licensing restrictions.

L2_Genomic.d: Open-source platform for managing and analyzing large-scale biomedical data

profile.overview: Arvados is an open-source platform for managing, processing, and sharing large-scale biomedical data, developed by Curii Corporation (formerly Curoverse). Used for genomic data management and reproducible bioinformatics workflows.
profile.functional_scope: Cloud-native data management and workflow execution for genomics. Supports CWL (Common Workflow Language) pipelines. Provides data provenance tracking, access control, and reproducibility. Scales from small datasets to population-level genomics.
profile.tech_stack: Go/Ruby/Python backend. CWL workflow engine (Crunch2). Keep distributed storage system. Kubernetes/Docker orchestration. REST API. Web workbench UI. AWS/GCP/Azure/on-premises deployment.
profile.operator: Curii Corporation, Somerville, MA, USA. Open-source community with contributions from academic and commercial users.
profile.data_model: Content-addressed storage (Keep) with collections of files. CWL workflow definitions. Structured metadata and provenance chains. Access control lists for data sharing.
profile.users_scale: Used by Veritas Genetics, Harvard Personal Genome Project, clinical genomics labs, and research institutions. Processes petabyte-scale datasets.
profile.access_model: Open-source under Apache 2.0 license. Self-hosted or Curii-managed cloud. Community edition free. Enterprise support available. Public playground for testing.

L2_Genomic.d: Interactive web-based visualization tool for phylogenetic trees and genomic epidemiology data, part of the Nextstrain ecosystem

profile.overview: Auspice is the open-source interactive visualization application developed by the Nextstrain team for exploring phylogenomic datasets. It renders phylogenetic trees with temporal, geographic, and mutation annotations, powering all Nextstrain.org visualizations and available as a standalone tool via auspice.us.
profile.functional_scope: Interactive phylogenetic tree visualization with zoom, filter, and animation. Geographic mapping of pathogen spread over time. Mutation frequency tracking. Diversity panels showing entropy and mutation distribution. Support for narratives (guided walkthroughs). Drag-and-drop local file viewing (no data uploaded to server).
profile.tech_stack: React.js frontend, D3.js for tree rendering and charts, Leaflet.js for geographic maps, deck.gl for transmission lines. WebAssembly for performance-critical operations. Installable via npm or usable via auspice.us. Open-source under AGPL v3.
profile.operator: Nextstrain project (Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center & University of Basel). Funded by NIH, HHMI, Wellcome Trust.
profile.data_model: Auspice JSON format: tree topology with node annotations (mutations, dates, locations, clade assignments, confidence intervals). Sidecar files for additional metadata, frequencies, and tip counts.
profile.users_scale: Powers all Nextstrain.org builds viewed by millions. auspice.us used by thousands of researchers for private dataset exploration.
profile.access_model: Free, open-source. auspice.us requires no login, all processing client-side (privacy-preserving). NPM package for self-hosting.

L2_Genomic.d: Computational tool for profiling microbial communities from metagenomic shotgun sequencing
wk: Command-line interface only; no web GUI limits accessibility for non-technical users

profile.overview: MetaPhlAn 4 (Metagenomic Phylogenetic Analysis) is a computational tool for profiling microbial communities from metagenomic data, developed by the Huttenhower Lab at Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. One of the most cited metagenomics tools.
profile.functional_scope: Species-level taxonomic profiling of microbiomes from metagenomic shotgun sequencing. Maps reads to unique clade-specific marker genes. Provides relative abundance estimates. Also supports strain-level profiling via StrainPhlAn.
profile.tech_stack: Python/Cython with Bowtie2 for read mapping. SGB (Species-Level Genome Bins) database. Integrated with bioBakery suite (HUMAnN, StrainPhlAn). Available via Bioconda, Docker, Galaxy.
profile.operator: Huttenhower Lab, Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, Boston, MA, USA. Funded by NIH/NHGRI, Broad Institute.
profile.data_model: Input: FASTQ metagenomic reads. Database: species-level genome bins (SGBs) with marker genes from 200,000+ reference genomes. Output: species abundance profiles (TSV), phylogenetic placement.
profile.users_scale: 20,000+ citations. Standard tool in Human Microbiome Project and MetaHIT. Used by virtually every microbiome research group. Integrated into thousands of bioinformatics pipelines.
profile.access_model: Free and open-source (MIT license). Available on GitHub (biobakery/MetaPhlAn), Bioconda, Docker. Database freely downloadable. No restrictions.

L2_Genomic.d: Co-visualization of phylogenetic trees with genome data
