profile.overview: Treenome Browser is an extension of Taxonium that enables co-visualization of phylogenetic trees with genome-level data, allowing researchers to explore mutations and evolutionary patterns alongside tree topology in the browser.
profile.functional_scope: Co-visualization of phylogenetic trees with genomic data: mutations mapped onto tree branches, genome browser integration, nucleotide-level exploration of variants alongside evolutionary context. Enables rapid identification of convergent evolution and lineage-defining mutations.
profile.tech_stack: Extension of Taxonium (WebGL, React). Genome browser component integrated with tree viewer. Handles trees with millions of tips. Open-source on GitHub (theosanderson/taxonium).
profile.operator: Theo Sanderson, Francis Crick Institute / Wellcome Sanger Institute, London, UK. Community open-source project.
profile.data_model: Phylogenetic trees (Newick/protobuf) linked with genome annotations. Mutation data mapped to tree nodes. Supports VCF-style variant data alongside tree topology.
profile.users_scale: Used by SARS-CoV-2 genomics community. Integrated with Taxonium.org public instance. Cited in genomic epidemiology publications.
profile.access_model: Free and open-source. Available at taxonium.org/treenome. Self-hostable. No registration required.

L2_Genomic.d: Bayesian evolutionary analysis for molecular clock and phylodynamic inference

profile.overview: BEAST 2 (Bayesian Evolutionary Analysis Sampling Trees) is an open-source software platform for Bayesian phylogenetics, molecular clock analysis, and phylodynamics. Successor to BEAST 1, developed by an international team led by Alexei Drummond and Remco Bouckaert.
profile.functional_scope: Bayesian inference of molecular phylogenies with evolutionary time estimates. Molecular clock models (strict, relaxed), coalescent demographic models, birth-death models, phylogeography, and structured population models. Extensible via packages.
profile.tech_stack: Java application with XML configuration. MCMC sampling algorithms. BEAUti GUI for model setup. Tracer for convergence diagnostics. FigTree/DensiTree for visualization. 300+ community packages. Open-source on GitHub.
profile.operator: International developer community. Led by University of Auckland (Drummond) and ETH Zürich (Stadler). Funded by Marsden Fund, ERC, and community contributions.
profile.data_model: Input: aligned sequences (NEXUS/FASTA) with metadata (dates, locations). XML model specification. Output: posterior tree distributions (NEXUS), parameter traces (log files). MCC summary trees.
profile.users_scale: 30,000+ citations. Standard tool for molecular epidemiology and viral phylodynamics. Used by every major genomic surveillance group including Nextstrain, COG-UK, CDC.
profile.access_model: Fully open-source (LGPL 2.1). Available at beast2.org. Cross-platform (Java). Free to use. Extensive tutorials and documentation.

L2_Genomic.d: Public Health Alliance for Genomic Epidemiology developing data standards and pipelines

profile.overview: PHA4GE (Public Health Alliance for Genomic Epidemiology) is a global coalition developing open data standards, bioinformatics tools, and best practices to enable interoperable genomic epidemiology for public health. Founded in 2019.
profile.functional_scope: Develops standardized metadata templates, contextual data specifications, bioinformatics tool benchmarks, and quality assurance protocols for pathogen genomic surveillance. Focus on SARS-CoV-2, AMR, food safety, and other public health priorities.
profile.tech_stack: GitHub-hosted specifications and tools. YAML/JSON metadata schemas. Validation tools (DataHarmonizer). Bioinformatics pipeline specifications. Integration with INSDC, GISAID standards.
profile.operator: Community-driven alliance with representation from CDC, ECDC, WHO, Wellcome Sanger Institute, academic institutions, and public health labs globally.
profile.data_model: Standardized metadata specifications for pathogen sequences (organism-specific templates). Contextual data dictionaries. Quality control metrics. Ontology-based controlled vocabularies.
profile.users_scale: Members from 40+ countries. Standards adopted by major surveillance programs (COG-UK, PulseNet, national reference labs). Used to harmonize SARS-CoV-2 data globally.
profile.access_model: Fully open and community-driven. All specifications on GitHub (pha4ge). Free to use and contribute. No membership fees. Working groups open to participation.

st: Horizontally scalable architecture supporting growing data volumes
L2_Genomic.d: Broad Institute's cloud-native bioinformatics platform for scalable genomic analysis, enabling collaborative research on pathogen genomics and population health data

profile.overview: Terra is a secure, scalable, cloud-based platform for biomedical data analysis, developed by the Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard in collaboration with Microsoft and Verily (Alphabet). It is the world's most trusted platform for large-scale genomic and clinical data analysis.
profile.functional_scope: Cloud-based data analysis with support for WDL (Workflow Description Language) and Cromwell execution engine. Jupyter notebooks, RStudio, and Galaxy integration. Secure data enclaves for sensitive datasets. Access to major genomic datasets (TCGA, gnomAD, 1000 Genomes, All of Us). Federated analysis framework for multi-institutional collaboration without data movement.
profile.tech_stack: Google Cloud Platform (primary) and Microsoft Azure. WDL/Cromwell workflow engine. Jupyter/RStudio interactive analysis. React.js frontend. Python/Scala backend. SAM (Security and Access Management) for authorization. Open-source components on GitHub.
profile.operator: Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard, in partnership with Microsoft and Verily (Alphabet/Google). Funded by NIH, NCI, and NHGRI.
profile.data_model: Workspace-based model with data tables, reference data, workflows, and analysis outputs. Supports any file format. Terra Data Repository for managed datasets with FAIR principles.
profile.users_scale: 50,000+ registered users. Hosts petabytes of biomedical data. Used by NIH All of Us, TCGA, AnVIL, and BioData Catalyst programs.
profile.access_model: Free to register. Pay-per-use cloud computing (Google Cloud billing). Free tier for small analyses. Institutional billing available. Secure workspaces for controlled-access data.

L2_Genomic.d: Interactive visualization of minimum spanning trees for genomic epidemiology

profile.overview: GrapeTree is an interactive minimum spanning tree (MST) visualization tool designed for large-scale genomic epidemiology datasets, developed by the EnteroBase team. It efficiently handles trees with hundreds of thousands of nodes and provides integrated metadata overlay.
profile.functional_scope: Visualization of minimum spanning trees for MLST/cgMLST profiles, interactive node coloring by metadata, dynamic filtering and searching, tree layout optimization for large datasets (MSTree V2 algorithm for 100,000+ nodes), export in SVG/PNG, and integration with EnteroBase.
profile.tech_stack: JavaScript/D3.js web application, MSTree V2 algorithm (C++ with Python bindings for efficient MST computation), Electron desktop app. Open-source on GitHub.
profile.operator: EnteroBase team, University of Warwick. Developed by Zhemin Zhou and Mark Achtman.
profile.data_model: Input: allelic profiles (MLST/cgMLST) and metadata tables. Output: interactive MST visualization with node annotations.
profile.users_scale: Standard visualization tool for EnteroBase (1.6M+ strains). Widely used in the microbial genomics community.
profile.access_model: Free, open-source. Available as web app, desktop app (Electron), and embeddable component.

L2_Genomic.d: Africa Genome Archiving for Response and Insight - continent-wide pathogen genomic data sharing platform launched Nov 2025 with biosecurity integration

profile.overview: AGARI (Africa Genome Amplification Resource for Infectious Diseases) is the Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention's continental genomic surveillance initiative, aimed at building genomic sequencing capacity across African nations for infectious disease detection and monitoring.
profile.functional_scope: Continental coordination of pathogen genomic sequencing, laboratory capacity building, bioinformatics training, data sharing across African Union member states, variant tracking and early warning, integration with Africa CDC's continental surveillance systems.
profile.tech_stack: Oxford Nanopore and Illumina sequencing platforms deployed across member state laboratories. Bioinformatics training using Galaxy, Nextstrain, and Nextclade. Data shared through GISAID and regional databases.
profile.operator: Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention (Africa CDC), African Union, Addis Ababa, Ethiopia.
profile.data_model: Pathogen genomic sequences with epidemiological metadata from African Union member states.
profile.users_scale: Covers 55 African Union member states. Building sequencing capacity in laboratories across the continent.
profile.access_model: Access through Africa CDC and member state public health agencies. Data sharing coordinated at continental level.

L2_Genomic.d: Real-time tracking and visualization of SARS-CoV-2 variants, mutations, and lineage distributions globally with clear frequency plots and mutation tables

profile.overview: CoVariants is an open-source web resource providing an overview of SARS-CoV-2 variants and mutations of interest. Created by Dr. Emma Hodcroft (University of Basel and Swiss Institute of Bioinformatics), it tracks variant prevalence globally using data from GISAID, with Nextstrain clade nomenclature.
profile.functional_scope: Variant information pages with mutation details and literature links, per-country variant frequency distributions over time, per-variant geographic spread visualization, shared mutation analysis across variants, 3D protein structure viewer showing mutation locations, and links to focused Nextstrain builds for each variant.
profile.tech_stack: React.js/Gatsby static site, D3.js for charts and visualizations, Python scripts for data processing from GISAID, 3D protein visualization using Mol* viewer. Hosted on GitHub Pages. Open-source on GitHub.
profile.operator: Dr. Emma Hodcroft, University of Basel / Swiss Institute of Bioinformatics. Independent research project.
profile.data_model: Aggregated variant frequency data by country and time from GISAID sequences. Mutation definitions and annotations per Nextstrain clade. Cross-referenced with WHO variant designations.
profile.users_scale: Hundreds of thousands of monthly visitors. Widely cited by public health agencies and media for variant tracking during the COVID-19 pandemic.
profile.access_model: Free, open-source, publicly accessible. No login required. All code and derived data on GitHub.

L2_Genomic.d: Canadian SNP-based phylogenomic pipeline for outbreak investigation

profile.overview: SNVPhyl (Single Nucleotide Variant PHYLogenomics) is a Canadian pipeline for SNP-based phylogenomic analysis developed by the National Microbiology Laboratory (NML) of the Public Health Agency of Canada, integrated with IRIDA and Galaxy.
profile.functional_scope: Automated SNP-based phylogenetic analysis for outbreak investigation: reference mapping, SNP calling, core SNP alignment, maximum likelihood phylogeny, and SNP distance matrix. Designed for public health lab use with minimal bioinformatics expertise.
profile.tech_stack: Galaxy workflow. SMALT/BWA mapping, FreeBayes variant calling, PhyML for phylogenetics. Docker containers. Integrated with IRIDA platform. Open-source on GitHub (phac-nml/snvphyl-galaxy).
profile.operator: Public Health Agency of Canada, National Microbiology Laboratory (NML), Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada.
profile.data_model: Input: paired-end FASTQ reads + reference genome. Output: SNP alignment, phylogenetic tree (Newick), SNP distance matrix, mapping quality metrics. Galaxy history for reproducibility.
profile.users_scale: Standard pipeline for Canadian public health genomic surveillance. Used by PulseNet Canada and international labs. Integrated into IRIDA platform deployments.
profile.access_model: Free and open-source on GitHub. Available as Galaxy workflow. Docker deployment. No registration required. Documentation and tutorials provided.

st: Dual-mode access via command-line and web interface
L2_Genomic.d: Standard computational tool for SARS-CoV-2 lineage assignment using the Pango nomenclature system, providing rapid classification of consensus sequences

profile.overview: Pangolin (Phylogenetic Assignment of Named Global Outbreak Lineages) is a software tool and nomenclature system for classifying SARS-CoV-2 genome sequences into epidemiological lineages. Developed by the Rambaut Lab at the University of Edinburgh, the Pango nomenclature is the global standard used by WHO and public health agencies.
profile.functional_scope: Assignment of SARS-CoV-2 sequences to Pango lineages using trained machine learning models (pangoLEARN) or UShER phylogenetic placement. Related tools: Scorpio (constellation-based variant calling), Civet (cluster investigation), Polecat (automated cluster flagging). Lineage designation proposals via GitHub.
profile.tech_stack: Python command-line tool, pangoLEARN (decision tree model trained on curated lineage assignments), UShER (C++ ultrafast phylogenetic placement). Installable via conda/pip. Web version available at pangolin.cog-uk.io. Open-source on GitHub.
profile.operator: Rambaut Lab, University of Edinburgh, with contributions from University of Oxford, University of Sydney, and COG-UK. Funded by Wellcome Trust, MRC, and UKRI.
profile.data_model: FASTA input sequences mapped to Pango lineage designations (e.g., B.1.1.7, BA.2.86). Lineage definitions stored as constellation files with defining mutations.
profile.users_scale: Standard tool used globally - millions of sequences classified. Used by WHO, CDC, ECDC, PHE, and virtually every public health agency worldwide.
profile.access_model: Free, open-source (GPL v3). Command-line tool, web interface, and API. Lineage definitions updated regularly via GitHub.

L2_Genomic.d: SecureBio nonprofit developing pathogen-agnostic metagenomic early warning and LLM-based AI-bio risk assessment tools

profile.overview: SecureBio is a nonprofit developing pathogen-agnostic metagenomic biosecurity technologies and AI biosafety tools. Works closely with the Nucleic Acid Observatory (NAO) at MIT/Broad Institute for early catastrophic biological threat detection.
profile.functional_scope: Metagenomic biosecurity: pathogen-agnostic sequencing of wastewater/environmental samples for novel pathogen detection, AI-based biosafety screening for synthetic biology, early warning of engineered biological agents, and pandemic preparedness analytics.
profile.tech_stack: Metagenomic sequencing (Illumina, Nanopore). Computational pipelines for novel pathogen detection. AI/ML models for DNA synthesis screening. Cloud analytics platform.
profile.operator: SecureBio (501c3 nonprofit), Cambridge, MA. Co-founded by Kevin Esvelt (MIT). Partnerships with MIT, Broad Institute, public health agencies.
profile.data_model: Metagenomic sequence data from environmental samples. Novel sequence detection alerts. DNA synthesis screening results. Integrated with NAO wastewater monitoring data.
profile.users_scale: Growing influence in biosecurity policy. Advises US/UK governments. NAO pilot programs in Boston-area wastewater. DNA screening tools used by synthesis providers.
profile.access_model: Nonprofit with open publications. Some tools and research publicly available. Policy recommendations published openly. Screening tools available to DNA synthesis industry.

st: Integrated quality control checks for sequence data validation
L2_Genomic.d: Fast web-based and CLI tool for quality control, clade assignment, and mutation calling of pathogen sequences including SARS-CoV-2, influenza, and mpox

profile.overview: Nextclade is a web-based and command-line tool for viral genome clade assignment, mutation calling, and sequence quality assessment. Part of the Nextstrain project, it provides instant analysis of SARS-CoV-2, influenza, RSV, mpox, and other pathogen sequences directly in the browser using WebAssembly.
profile.functional_scope: Clade/lineage assignment (Nextstrain clades, Pango lineages, WHO labels), mutation calling (nucleotide and amino acid), phylogenetic placement on reference trees, sequence quality control (missing data, mixed sites, frame shifts, stop codons), and batch processing of thousands of sequences.
profile.tech_stack: Rust core compiled to WebAssembly for browser execution (no server upload needed), React.js frontend, Nextstrain Augur for reference tree generation. Also available as CLI tool. Open-source on GitHub under MIT license.
profile.operator: Nextstrain project (Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center & University of Basel).
profile.data_model: FASTA input sequences analyzed against curated reference datasets per pathogen. Output includes clade assignment, mutation list, QC metrics, and phylogenetic placement.
profile.users_scale: Millions of sequences analyzed. Used by public health labs worldwide for routine SARS-CoV-2 and influenza surveillance.
profile.access_model: Free, open-source. Browser-based (all processing local - no data leaves user's machine). CLI for batch processing.

L2_Genomic.d: Metagenomic sentinel surveillance for early warning of catastrophic pandemics

profile.overview: The Nucleic Acid Observatory (NAO) is a research initiative at MIT and the Broad Institute developing metagenomic surveillance of wastewater and environmental samples for pathogen-agnostic early warning of biological threats, including novel pandemics.
profile.functional_scope: Pathogen-agnostic metagenomic surveillance of wastewater. Sequencing all nucleic acids in samples to detect known and unknown pathogens. Statistical methods for anomaly detection. Goal: provide weeks of early warning for catastrophic biological events.
profile.tech_stack: Deep metagenomic sequencing (Illumina). Computational pipelines for novel sequence detection, anomaly identification, and threat assessment. Cloud computing (Terra/Google Cloud). Custom statistical frameworks.
profile.operator: MIT Media Lab (Kevin Esvelt), Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard, Cambridge, MA. Funded by Open Philanthropy, various foundations.
profile.data_model: Metagenomic sequence data (billions of reads per sample). Taxonomic assignments, novel sequence flags, temporal trend analyses. Environmental metadata (wastewater site, date, flow).
profile.users_scale: Pilot programs in Boston-area wastewater facilities. Growing network. Influential in biosecurity policy community. Publications in Nature, Science.
profile.access_model: Academic research program. Data and methods published openly. Software on GitHub. Sequence data deposited in public archives. Policy recommendations published.

L2_Genomic.d: UK's COVID-19 Genomics Consortium that sequenced over 2 million SARS-CoV-2 genomes, establishing the blueprint for national pathogen genomic surveillance

profile.overview: COG-UK (COVID-19 Genomics UK Consortium) was a national consortium for SARS-CoV-2 genome sequencing in the United Kingdom, established in March 2020 and closed on March 31, 2023. It sequenced over 2.5 million SARS-CoV-2 genomes, making the UK the world leader in COVID-19 genomic surveillance.
profile.functional_scope: Large-scale SARS-CoV-2 whole genome sequencing, real-time variant detection and tracking, integration of genomic data with epidemiological information, generation of phylogenetic analyses for outbreak investigation, informing public health policy and vaccine strategy. Developed Pangolin, Civet, and other tools.
profile.tech_stack: Oxford Nanopore and Illumina sequencing. ARTIC protocol for amplicon-based WGS. CLIMB (Cloud Infrastructure for Microbial Bioinformatics) for data processing. Custom bioinformatics pipelines. Data shared via GISAID and ENA.
profile.operator: UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA), Wellcome Sanger Institute, University of Edinburgh, University of Oxford, and 20+ partner institutions. Funded by UKRI/MRC, DHSC, and Wellcome Trust.
profile.data_model: SARS-CoV-2 consensus sequences with lineage assignments, sample metadata (collection date, region, patient demographics), and epidemiological linkage data.
profile.users_scale: Sequenced 2.5+ million SARS-CoV-2 genomes (largest national sequencing program globally). Consortium of 20+ academic and public health institutions.
profile.access_model: Closed program (archived). Sequencing data publicly available via GISAID and ENA. Tools (Pangolin, etc.) remain actively maintained and open-source. Website archived by UK National Archives.

st: Browser-based interface requiring no local installation
L2_Genomic.d: Web-based bioinformatics platform for influenza and SARS-CoV-2 whole-genome sequencing analysis, providing automated variant calling and phylogenetic analysis

profile.overview: INSaFLU is a free, web-based bioinformatics platform for influenza and SARS-CoV-2 whole genome sequencing analysis. Developed by the National Institute of Health (INSA) of Portugal, it provides automated pipelines from raw reads to phylogenetic analysis.
profile.functional_scope: Automated reference-based genome assembly, variant calling (SNPs and indels), consensus sequence generation, clade/lineage assignment, minor variant detection (intra-host diversity), phylogenetic tree building, and surveillance reporting. Supports both Illumina and Oxford Nanopore data.
profile.tech_stack: Web platform built on Django (Python), using bioinformatics tools: snippy (variant calling), MAFFT (alignment), FastTree/IQ-TREE (phylogenetics), Abricate (AMR), Nextclade integration. Docker deployment. Open-source on GitHub.
profile.operator: National Institute of Health Doutor Ricardo Jorge (INSA), Lisbon, Portugal.
profile.data_model: Raw reads (FASTQ) and reference genomes as input. Outputs: consensus sequences, variant tables, phylogenetic trees, quality reports. User-managed private workspace.
profile.users_scale: Used by public health laboratories in 30+ countries for influenza and SARS-CoV-2 genomic surveillance.
profile.access_model: Free. Account required for upload and analysis. User data stored privately. No sharing of submitted data by INSaFLU team.

L2_Genomic.d: ECDC's Genomic Epidemiology Coordination platform for EU/EEA-wide genomic surveillance standardization, harmonizing pathogen sequencing and data sharing across member states

profile.overview: GECO (Genomic Epidemiology Coordination) is the European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control's platform for coordinating genomic surveillance across EU/EEA member states, facilitating cross-border pathogen tracking.
profile.functional_scope: EU-wide genomic surveillance coordination: standardized sequencing protocols, cross-border sequence data sharing, variant monitoring, cluster detection, and integrated epidemiological-genomic analysis for EU/EEA member states.
profile.tech_stack: TESSy (The European Surveillance System) integration. Sequencing data standards. ECDC pathogen-specific platforms. Integration with GISAID and ENA.
profile.operator: European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control (ECDC), Stockholm, Sweden. All 30 EU/EEA member states participate.
profile.data_model: Pathogen genomic sequences with structured metadata (country, date, specimen type). Linked with TESSy epidemiological data. ECDC-standardized reporting formats.
profile.users_scale: Serves 30 EU/EEA countries (~450M population). Coordinates national reference laboratories. Key tool for EU pandemic response.
profile.access_model: EU/EEA member state access through ECDC portal. Aggregate data publicly available through ECDC surveillance reports.

L2_Genomic.d: Asia Pathogen Genomics Initiative for regional genomic surveillance capacity

profile.overview: The Asia Pathogen Genomics Initiative (APGI) is a regional capacity-building program for genomic surveillance of infectious diseases across Asia, supported by international partners.
profile.functional_scope: Regional genomic surveillance capacity building: training public health laboratories in sequencing, bioinformatics, data sharing, and genomic epidemiology. Supports pathogen genomic surveillance networks across Asian countries.
profile.tech_stack: Sequencing technology training (Illumina, Nanopore). Bioinformatics pipeline deployment. Data sharing infrastructure. Quality assurance protocols.
profile.operator: Multi-partner initiative with support from WHO, USCDC, and Asian Development Bank. Regional coordination across Southeast and South Asian countries.
profile.data_model: Pathogen genomic sequences with epidemiological metadata. Harmonized data standards. Regional sequence databases.
profile.users_scale: Participating laboratories across multiple Asian countries. Building capacity for routine genomic surveillance in the region.
profile.access_model: Institutional participation through national public health agencies. Training and resources provided to member countries.

st: Comprehensive training materials and capacity-building resources
L2_Genomic.d: Open-source R package ecosystem (TRACE initiative) providing modular tools for outbreak analytics, disease modeling, and genomic epidemiology in R/Python

profile.overview: EpiVerse TRACE (Tools for Real-time Analytics of Epidemics and Collaborative Epidemiology) is an open-source R package ecosystem for outbreak analytics, developed by a consortium funded by data.org and London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine.
profile.functional_scope: Suite of R packages for epidemic analysis: outbreak reconstruction, transmission chain analysis, genomic-epidemiological integration, forecasting, severity estimation, and contact tracing data analysis. Designed for field epidemiologists.
profile.tech_stack: R packages (R/C++). Includes linelist (data cleaning), epiparameter (pathogen parameters), cfr (case fatality), epichains (transmission chains). GitHub-hosted. CI/CD tested.
profile.operator: LSHTM, data.org, Imperial College London, Jagiellonian University, and international consortium. Funded by data.org.
profile.data_model: Standardized line list data, transmission trees, contact tracing records, genomic metadata. Interoperable with WHO GOARN data formats.
profile.users_scale: Growing adoption by WHO, CDC, and field epidemiologists. Active open-source community. Training workshops delivered globally.
profile.access_model: Free and open-source (MIT license). All packages on CRAN and GitHub (epiverse-trace). Comprehensive documentation and tutorials.

L2_Genomic.d: KAUST Pathogen Genomics Lab tools including AssayM for RT-PCR primer surveillance

profile.overview: KAUST (King Abdullah University of Science and Technology) Pathogen Genomics Lab (PGL) develops genomic tools including AssayM for automated design and validation of RT-PCR assays for pathogen surveillance.
profile.functional_scope: Pathogen genomics research and tool development: AssayM software for automated RT-PCR assay design and in silico validation, SARS-CoV-2 genomic surveillance, metagenomic analysis, and capacity building for Middle East region.
profile.tech_stack: Python bioinformatics tools (AssayM). Illumina/Nanopore sequencing. Custom analysis pipelines. Cloud computing. Published on GitHub.
profile.operator: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST), Thuwal, Saudi Arabia. Funded by KAUST.
profile.data_model: Pathogen genome sequences, PCR primer/probe designs, in silico validation results, surveillance metadata. Submissions to GISAID/NCBI.
profile.users_scale: Regional genomic surveillance capacity. Published research. Tools used by Middle East public health labs. International collaborations.
profile.access_model: Open-source tools on GitHub. Research publications freely available. Academic collaborations. KAUST institutional access for internal resources.

L2_Genomic.d: FDA's distributed network of laboratories performing whole-genome sequencing of foodborne pathogens for rapid identification and outbreak source tracking

profile.overview: GenomeTrakr is the FDA's distributed network of state and federal laboratories performing whole-genome sequencing of foodborne pathogens for surveillance and outbreak detection, integrated with NCBI Pathogen Detection.
profile.functional_scope: Foodborne pathogen genomic surveillance: WGS of Salmonella, Listeria, E. coli, Campylobacter, Vibrio, and Cronobacter isolates. Real-time sequence sharing through NCBI. Cluster detection for outbreak identification. Source tracking.
profile.tech_stack: Illumina whole-genome sequencing. NCBI Pathogen Detection pipeline for automated clustering. BaseSpace for sequencing. BioNumerics for analysis. Sequences deposited in NCBI SRA.
profile.operator: FDA Center for Food Safety and Applied Nutrition (CFSAN). Partnership with USDA FSIS, CDC PulseNet, and state public health laboratories.
profile.data_model: Whole-genome sequences with structured metadata (organism, source, location, date, isolation source). NCBI BioSample/SRA format. SNP clusters for outbreak detection.
profile.users_scale: 25+ participating laboratories (FDA, state, international). 650,000+ pathogen genomes sequenced. Integrated with PulseNet for US foodborne disease outbreak detection.
profile.access_model: Sequence data publicly available in NCBI SRA. GenomeTrakr network data freely accessible. Open collaboration for laboratories to join the network.

L2_Genomic.d: R package for automated generation of epidemiological situation reports, developed with MSF and WHO templates for outbreak response documentation

profile.overview: Sitrep is an R package from the R4Epid project providing templates for automated generation of epidemiological situation reports during outbreak response.
profile.functional_scope: Automated situation report generation: standardized templates for outbreak reports, descriptive epidemiology tables, epidemic curves, attack rate calculations, and mortality summaries. Designed for field epidemiologists.
profile.tech_stack: R package using R Markdown templates. Integrates with dplyr, ggplot2, flextable. Produces Word/HTML/PDF reports. Open-source on GitHub (R4EPI/sitrep).
profile.operator: R4EPI project. Community-developed open-source. Supported by MSF (Doctors Without Borders) Epicentre.
profile.data_model: Line list input data (CSV/Excel). Standardized output: summary tables, epidemic curves, geographic distribution maps, demographic breakdowns.
profile.users_scale: Used by MSF, WHO GOARN, and field epidemiologists during outbreaks. Training tool for Field Epidemiology Training Programs.
profile.access_model: Free and open-source (MIT license). Available on GitHub. Documentation and tutorials. Training materials for epidemic response.

profile.overview: CanPath (Canadian Partnership for Tomorrow's Health) is Canada's largest population health cohort platform, following 330,000+ participants across 7 regional cohorts (BC Generations Project, Alberta's Tomorrow Project, Manitoba Tomorrow Project, Ontario Health Study, CARTaGENE, Atlantic PATH, Healthy Future Sask). Launched in 2009, it integrates questionnaire data, biological samples (blood, urine, saliva), and linked administrative health records. In 2024, CanPath partnered with Lifebit and AWS to launch a cloud-based Trusted Research Environment (TRE) for secure, ethical access to anonymized health data. CanPath is expanding its genomic data landscape with genotyping arrays and whole-genome sequencing from Ontario and Quebec cohorts.
profile.functional_scope: Large-scale population health research: longitudinal cohort tracking, biobank management (blood, urine, saliva), genomic data access (SNP arrays, WGS), environmental exposure assessment, lifestyle and behavioral data collection, linked health administrative records, and secure research data access via Trusted Research Environment.
profile.tech_stack: AWS cloud-based Trusted Research Environment (Lifebit platform), REDCap for data collection, Illumina genotyping arrays, whole-genome sequencing pipelines, secure CanPath Access Portal for researcher queries, OHDSI/OMOP common data model for harmonization.
profile.operator: CanPath National Office (hosted at Ontario Institute for Cancer Research, OICR). Funded by Canadian Partnership Against Cancer, provincial health authorities, and Genome Canada. Member of NCI Cohort Consortium.
profile.data_model: Harmonized cohort data covering demographics, health history, lifestyle, environmental exposures, biological samples, and genomic data. Over 1 billion data points across 330,000+ participants. Schema.org-compatible metadata.
profile.users_scale: 330,000+ enrolled participants, 7 provincial cohorts, hundreds of active research projects. Data accessible to qualified Canadian and international researchers.
profile.access_model: Researcher access via CanPath Access Portal (application-based, requires ethics approval). Cloud-based TRE for in-situ analysis. Biological samples available for approved studies. Free for academic researchers.

L2_Genomic.d: Canada's largest population cohort platform following 330,000+ participants across 7 regional cohorts, integrating questionnaire, biobank, and genomic data

wk: Command-line only tool requiring bioinformatics expertise to operate
L2_Genomic.d: Cluster Investigation and Virus Epidemiology Tool for genomic epidemiological investigation of SARS-CoV-2 transmission clusters using sequence and metadata

profile.overview: CIVET (Cluster Investigation and Virus Epidemiology Tool) is a bioinformatics tool from the ARTIC Network for investigating genomic clusters during pathogen outbreaks using phylogenetic context.
profile.functional_scope: Genomic cluster investigation: places outbreak sequences in broader phylogenetic context, identifies closest relatives in background datasets, generates focused phylogenetic trees, and provides cluster summaries for epidemiological interpretation.
profile.tech_stack: Python tool using Minimap2, gofasta, snipit, IQTREE2. Requires background alignment and metadata. Open-source on GitHub (artic-network/civet).
profile.operator: ARTIC Network. Developed by Áine O'Toole and Andrew Rambaut, University of Edinburgh, UK.
profile.data_model: Input: query FASTA sequences + background alignment + metadata CSV. Output: phylogenetic trees, cluster reports, timeline figures, interactive HTML reports.
profile.users_scale: Used during COVID-19 for local outbreak investigation. Popular with UK and international genomic surveillance teams. COG-UK standard tool.
profile.access_model: Free and open-source (GPL-3.0). Available on GitHub and Bioconda. Documentation and tutorials provided.

L2_Genomic.d: West African laboratory network strengthening diagnostic capacity and quality assurance across francophone Africa for pathogen detection and surveillance

profile.overview: RESAOLAB is a West African laboratory network strengthening diagnostic capacity across francophone West Africa, improving quality of clinical laboratory services for disease surveillance.
profile.functional_scope: Laboratory network strengthening: quality management, external quality assessment, training of laboratory technicians, supply chain management, laboratory information system deployment, and AMR surveillance support across West African laboratories.
profile.tech_stack: Laboratory quality management systems. SLIPTA (Stepwise Laboratory Improvement Process) framework. Electronic sample tracking. LIMS deployment.
profile.operator: Fondation Mérieux and partner governments (Senegal, Mali, Burkina Faso, Guinea, Niger, Togo, Benin). Funded by Agence Française de Développement (AFD).
profile.data_model: Laboratory quality indicators, EQA results, testing volumes, supply chain data. Surveillance data from participating laboratories.
profile.users_scale: Network of 700+ laboratories across 7 West African countries. Training of thousands of laboratory professionals.
profile.access_model: Institutional program. Access for participating laboratories and health ministries. Aggregate reports published. Website at resaolab.org.

L2_Genomic.d: Open-source customizable dashboard template for epidemiological and genomic surveillance data visualization, adaptable for various pathogen monitoring use cases

profile.overview: Open-source customizable surveillance dashboard template providing a framework for epidemiological data visualization and situation reporting during disease outbreaks.
profile.functional_scope: Template framework for surveillance dashboards: epidemic curves, geographic maps, summary statistics, trend indicators, and comparison charts. Customizable for any disease/outbreak scenario.
profile.tech_stack: HTML/JavaScript/CSS. Chart.js or D3.js visualization. Leaflet.js mapping. Responsive design. GitHub template repository.
profile.operator: Open-source community project. Multiple contributors from public health informatics community.
profile.data_model: Configurable data input (CSV/JSON). Standard epidemiological visualizations. Customizable indicators and geographic boundaries.
profile.users_scale: Template used by dozens of organizations for COVID-19 and other disease dashboards. Community-maintained with contributions.
profile.access_model: Free and open-source. GitHub template. Fork and customize. MIT license. Documentation included.

L1_Surveillance.d: End-to-end outbreak management and analysis

profile.overview: SORMAS (Surveillance Outbreak Response Management and Analysis System) is an open-source digital health platform for epidemiological surveillance and outbreak management. Developed jointly by the Helmholtz Centre for Infection Research (HZI, Germany) and the Nigeria Centre for Disease Control (NCDC) starting in 2014. Covers 40+ diseases including Ebola, Lassa fever, cholera, COVID-19, measles, and mpox. Designed specifically for low- and middle-income countries with intermittent connectivity. Deployed as the national surveillance system in Nigeria, Ghana, and multiple other African and Asian nations.
profile.functional_scope: End-to-end outbreak management: case registration and classification, contact tracing with follow-up scheduling, event-based surveillance, laboratory sample management (request-to-result), aggregate reporting, immunization campaigns, statistical analysis and dashboards, GIS mapping, task management, and automated alerts. Supports 40+ disease-specific case definitions. Mobile offline-capable Android app for field workers. Role-based workflows for surveillance officers, contact tracers, lab technicians, clinicians, and administrators. WHO-recommended tool for outbreak response in resource-limited settings.
profile.tech_stack: Java EE backend on WildFly/JBoss application server, PostgreSQL database, Vaadin web UI framework, Android mobile application (offline-first with sync), REST APIs for integration, Docker/Docker Compose deployment, Keycloak for authentication. Supports HL7 FHIR interoperability standards. Source code on GitHub under GPL v3. CI/CD via GitHub Actions. i18n for 10+ languages. Can be deployed on-premise or cloud (AWS, Azure). The SORMAS Foundation maintains a central demo server.
profile.operator: SORMAS Foundation (non-profit, registered in Germany) with core development by the Helmholtz Centre for Infection Research (HZI, Braunschweig). Partnerships with Nigeria Centre for Disease Control (NCDC), Ghana Health Service, and national agencies in Fiji, Nepal, Cote d'Ivoire, Senegal, and others. Funded by German Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development (BMZ) through GIZ, WHO, UNDP, and the EU. Academic support from University of Braunschweig.
profile.data_model: Relational PostgreSQL schema with entities: Person, Case, Contact, Event, Sample, Task, Immunization, Aggregate Report, Travel Entry. Follows FHIR/HL7 standards where possible. Case classification system: suspect, probable, confirmed, not a case. Geographic hierarchy: country > region > district > community > facility. Supports data export in CSV, JSON, and SORMAS-specific formats. GDPR-compliant data handling with pseudonymization options. De-identified aggregate data for public dashboards.
profile.users_scale: Deployed in 15+ countries as of 2024: Nigeria (national system, 774 LGAs), Ghana (national), Fiji, Nepal, Cote d'Ivoire, Senegal, Guinea, Burkina Faso, Cameroon, and others. 20,000+ active users. Managed millions of COVID-19 cases in Nigeria during the pandemic. 2024 Annual Report highlights adoption as national system by additional countries. Cited in 50+ peer-reviewed publications. 100% score on WHO Digital Health Global Goods Maturity Assessment.
profile.access_model: Free, open-source under GPL v3. Self-hosted deployment with full data sovereignty. No licensing fees. Technical support available through SORMAS Foundation and partner network. Community contributions welcome via GitHub. Cloud hosting options through certified partners. Role-based access control (RBAC) with fine-grained permissions. Demo instance available at demo.sormas.org for evaluation.

deep_research.executive_summary: SORMAS is the leading open-source outbreak management platform purpose-built for resource-limited settings, earning a perfect WHO Digital Health maturity score. Deployed nationally in Nigeria and Ghana with 20,000+ users across 15+ countries. Score 90/100 reflects comprehensive functionality, mobile-first design, and strong institutional backing from HZI and German BMZ funding.

key_publications.title: SORMAS: open-source mobile health surveillance and response system for infectious diseases in Africa
key_publications.authors: Adeoye O, Tom-Aba D, Ameh C, et al.

key_publications.title: Digital Health Global Goods Maturity Assessment: SORMAS
key_publications.authors: Tom-Aba D, Olaleye A, Olayinka AT, et al.

key_publications.title: Assessment of the utility and performance of SORMAS in LMICs: a scoping review
SORMAS: Establishing a surveillance and outbreak response system in Ebola-affected countries
key_publications.authors: Fähnrich C, Denecke K, Adeoye O, et al.
key_publications.journal: BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making

official_guidelines.title: WHO Digital Health Global Goods guideline (rates SORMAS 100%)
German BMZ Digital Health in Africa strategy (features SORMAS)

controversies_and_changes: 2024: Adopted as national surveillance system by additional countries beyond Nigeria and Ghana.
Sustainability concerns: heavy reliance on German BMZ/GIZ funding; SORMAS Foundation established to ensure long-term viability.
Complex deployment: requires significant local IT capacity for self-hosting; some LMICs struggle with infrastructure.
2025 scoping review (Danso et al.) highlighted need for more rigorous evaluation evidence from field deployments.

ecosystem_connections.relationship: Interoperability via FHIR; some countries use both (DHIS2 for aggregate, SORMAS for case-level)
WHO recommends SORMAS for outbreak response deployment
Competing/complementary contact tracing tool; interoperability explored
SORMAS implements IDSR protocols digitally
Follows FHIR standards for health data exchange

timeline.event: Development started at HZI during West Africa Ebola outbreak
First peer-reviewed publication; deployed in Nigeria
100% WHO Digital Health maturity score; COVID-19 response in Nigeria
SORMAS Foundation established for sustainability
Adopted nationally in additional countries; Annual Report published
Scoping review of LMIC deployments published (Danso et al.)

L1_Surveillance.d: World's largest health management information system

profile.overview: DHIS2 (District Health Information Software 2) is the world's largest open-source health management information system, developed by the Health Information Systems Programme (HISP) at the University of Oslo (UiO), Norway, since 1994. Used as the national health information system in 100+ countries, covering 30% of the world's population (~2.4 billion people). A WHO-recommended digital health platform. Supports aggregate and individual-level health data collection, analysis, visualization, and sharing across all levels of health systems from community to national. Extended during COVID-19 for case-based surveillance and vaccination tracking.
profile.functional_scope: Aggregate data collection and reporting, individual/event-based data (Tracker), Android mobile data capture (offline-capable), interactive dashboards and GIS mapping, data quality and validation tools, analytics engine with pivot tables and charts, program indicators and calculated metrics, messaging and notifications, user management with organizational unit hierarchy, metadata management, integration engine (FHIR, ADX, CSV, JSON), COVID-19 surveillance packages, immunization registry, malaria/TB/HIV program modules, health campaigns management, logistics and supply chain (LMIS), CRVS integration, nutrition tracking, and education sector applications. App Platform for custom extensions.
profile.tech_stack: Java Spring backend, PostgreSQL/PostGIS database, React.js frontend (DHIS2 App Platform), Android SDK for mobile, REST/Web API, FHIR R4 adapter, Apache Tomcat server, Hibernate ORM, Flyway migrations, Redis for caching, Elasticsearch for analytics, Docker deployment support. DHIS2 App Hub for community apps. Supported on Linux/Ubuntu servers. Cloud hosting available through HISP partners. Open-source under BSD 3-clause license. Continuous releases with LTS versions. Extensive developer documentation and API reference.
profile.operator: HISP Centre at the University of Oslo (UiO), Norway. Global HISP network of regional organizations: HISP East Africa, HISP West Africa, HISP India, HISP Vietnam, HISP South Africa, HISP Colombia, and others. Funded by Norad (Norwegian Agency for Development Cooperation), PEPFAR, Global Fund, Gavi, WHO, UNICEF, CDC, Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, and national governments. DHIS2 is governed by UiO with community input.
profile.data_model: Hierarchical organizational unit model (country > province > district > facility > community). Data elements grouped into datasets for aggregate reporting. Tracker programs for individual-level longitudinal data with enrollment, events, and relationships. Program rules engine for skip logic and validation. Indicators and program indicators for calculated metrics. Periods: daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, annual. Data dimensions: organizational unit, period, data element, category combinations. Supports GeoJSON for boundaries. Import/export: CSV, JSON, XML, ADX, FHIR. Audit trail for all data changes.
profile.users_scale: National HMIS in 100+ countries. 2.4+ billion people in DHIS2-covered areas. Largest deployments: India (national), Bangladesh, Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, Mozambique, South Africa, Indonesia, Vietnam, Nigeria. During COVID-19, 60+ countries used DHIS2 for pandemic surveillance. 40,000+ server instances registered. 600+ community apps on DHIS2 App Hub. Annual DHIS2 conferences with 500+ attendees. Cited in 1,000+ academic publications. Used by WHO, UNICEF, CDC, Global Fund, and virtually all major global health organizations.
profile.access_model: Free, open-source under BSD 3-clause license. Self-hosted with full data sovereignty. Cloud hosting available through HISP network partners. No licensing fees. DHIS2 Academy provides training. Online documentation at docs.dhis2.org. Community of Practice forum for support. Customizable through App Platform. DHIS2 in a Box for quick deployment. Annual conference and regional workshops.

deep_research.executive_summary: DHIS2 is the world's most deployed health information system, serving as the national HMIS in 100+ countries covering 2.4 billion people. Score 89/100 reflects massive scale, comprehensive features, and strong global health community. Key strength is the HISP network providing local support. Limitations include limited native genomic integration and complexity of customization for smaller organizations.

key_publications.title: DHIS2: The tool that revolutionized health information management
The DHIS2 open source software platform: evolution over 15 years and beyond
Using DHIS2 for COVID-19 surveillance and vaccination tracking
Health Information Systems in Developing Countries
key_publications.authors: Braa J, Monteiro E, Sahay S

official_guidelines.title: WHO Classification of Digital Health Interventions (references DHIS2)
DHIS2 Health Data Toolkit (with WHO, UNICEF, CDC)

controversies_and_changes: Scale brings complexity: local customization often requires significant HISP partner support.
Limited native genomic/sequencing data support; requires integration with external genomic platforms.
Data quality challenges in some deployments due to connectivity and training gaps.
March 2025: Health Campaigns module webinar highlighting expanded use for vaccination campaigns.

ecosystem_connections.relationship: Interoperable via FHIR; some countries use DHIS2 for aggregate + SORMAS for case-level
Clinical EMR that feeds data to DHIS2 aggregate reporting
Contact tracing tool integrated with DHIS2 in some deployments
Community health worker tools that integrate with DHIS2
Native FHIR R4 adapter for interoperability

timeline.event: HISP project started at University of Oslo/Cape Town
DHIS2 web-based platform launched
Adopted as national HMIS in 40+ countries
100+ countries using DHIS2
COVID-19 surveillance packages deployed in 60+ countries
Health Campaigns module; continued expansion to non-health sectors
Health Data Toolkit updates with WHO/UNICEF/CDC

L1_Surveillance.d: National Syndromic Surveillance Program with ESSENCE analytics engine
wk: Limited to US domestic coverage with no international data integration

profile.overview: BioSense Platform / ESSENCE (Electronic Surveillance System for the Early Notification of Community-based Epidemics) is the United States' national syndromic surveillance system, operated by the CDC's National Syndromic Surveillance Program (NSSP). Originally developed by Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory (JHU/APL) in the early 2000s, ESSENCE was integrated into the CDC BioSense Platform in 2013-2014. It processes near-real-time emergency department (ED) visit data, urgent care visits, and other clinical data from 6,000+ healthcare facilities across all 50 US states, DC, and territories. Serves as the primary early-warning system for disease outbreaks, environmental exposures, and bioterrorism events in the US.
profile.functional_scope: Real-time syndromic surveillance using chief complaint text, discharge diagnosis codes (ICD-10-CM), and clinical data from ED visits. Core capabilities: anomaly detection algorithms (CUSUM, EWMA, regression), customizable syndrome definitions, temporal trend analysis, geographic heat maps, facility-level monitoring, ad-hoc querying, automated alerting, data quality dashboards, myESSENCE for personalized views. Supports CDC-defined syndrome categories (COVID-like illness, influenza-like illness, drug overdose, heat-related illness, firearm injuries, etc.). Weekly NSSP snapshots published. Integration with BioSense Platform for data storage, processing, and sharing.
profile.tech_stack: ESSENCE analytics engine (Java-based, developed by JHU/APL), BioSense Platform cloud infrastructure (AWS GovCloud), HL7 2.5.1 messaging standard for data ingestion, ICD-10-CM coding, Rhino data processing tools, LOINC for lab data, FHIR adaptation in progress. SAS for statistical analysis. Data visualization via ESSENCE web interface with customizable dashboards. Secure data access via SAMS (Secure Access Management Service). CDC WONDER integration for public aggregate data. Master Facility Table for standardized facility identification.
profile.operator: CDC Division of Health Informatics and Surveillance (DHIS), Center for Surveillance, Epidemiology, and Laboratory Services (CSELS). ESSENCE engine maintained by Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory (JHU/APL). Operated under the National Syndromic Surveillance Program (NSSP). Funded by CDC and participating state/local health departments. Data use agreements with each jurisdiction. Recent integration with Navy/Marine Corps Force Health Protection Command (NMCFHPC).
profile.data_model: Visit-centric: each record represents an ED/urgent care visit with fields: chief complaint free text, discharge diagnosis (ICD-10-CM), patient demographics (age, sex, race, ethnicity – de-identified), facility ID, visit date/time, disposition. Data transmitted via HL7 2.5.1 ADT messages. Processed through syndrome classifiers (keyword + code-based). Aggregated by syndrome, geography, time period, and facility. Data stored in BioSense Platform with jurisdictional data ownership. Public aggregate data available via CDC WONDER. Master Message Guide (MMG) defines standard fields.
profile.users_scale: Covers all 50 US states, DC, and territories. 6,000+ healthcare facilities reporting. ~75% of US ED visits captured. Thousands of epidemiologists and public health analysts as registered users. Used during every major US public health event: COVID-19, opioid crisis, vaping illness (EVALI), heat events, natural disasters. NSSP Community of Practice connects users. Weekly NSSP Update newsletter. Annual NSSP conference. US military (Navy/Marine Corps) recently integrated. Estimated coverage of 250+ million Americans.
profile.access_model: Access restricted to authorized public health personnel. Jurisdictions must sign Data Use Agreements (DUA) with CDC. Registered users access via SAMS (Secure Access Management Service). Public aggregate data available through CDC WONDER and NSSP weekly snapshots. ESSENCE analytics require approved NSSP account. Data ownership retained by submitting jurisdictions. Not open-source; government-operated platform. Research access possible through NSSP data request process.

deep_research.executive_summary: BioSense/ESSENCE is the US national syndromic surveillance system capturing ~75% of ED visits across all states. Score 87.2/100 reflects critical national infrastructure, real-time anomaly detection, and proven utility in COVID-19, opioid crisis, and bioterrorism preparedness. Limitations include US-only coverage, complex onboarding for new jurisdictions, and ongoing modernization challenges.

key_publications.title: The ESSENCE II Electronic Surveillance System for detecting unusual clusters of health events
key_publications.authors: Lombardo J, Burkom H, Pavlin J

key_publications.title: Evaluation of syndromic surveillance systems
Federal Register notice: NSSP BioSense Platform data collection (2025)

official_guidelines.title: About NSSP and the BioSense Platform

controversies_and_changes: Ongoing CDC modernization efforts: transitioning from HL7 2.5.1 to FHIR for data exchange.
2025: COVID-19 to be removed from nationally notifiable condition list; impacts syndromic surveillance integration.
2025: Federal Register proposed data collection update for NSSP/BioSense.
Complex onboarding: new jurisdictions face technical and political barriers to participation.
Data quality challenges: chief complaint free-text varies widely across facilities.
2025: Navy/Marine Corps Force Health Protection Command integrated with NSSP/ESSENCE.

ecosystem_connections.relationship: Complementary: NNDSS for notifiable disease case reporting, NSSP for syndromic early warning
Public aggregate data from NSSP available via WONDER
Develops and maintains the ESSENCE analytics engine
Case-based surveillance system that NSSP syndromic data can trigger investigations in
Separate military ESSENCE instance using same technology; Navy/Marine recently integrated with CDC NSSP

timeline.event: ESSENCE developed at JHU/APL post-9/11 and anthrax attacks
ESSENCE II published; BioSense program launched by CDC
BioSense Platform modernized; NSSP established
Critical role in COVID-19 surveillance; rapid syndrome definition deployment
Continued modernization; expanded syndrome categories
Federal Register update; COVID-19 removed from notifiable conditions; Navy/Marine integration; FHIR transition begins

cbrn_assessment.assessment: BioSense/ESSENCE is not a dedicated CBRN platform but serves as a critical early-warning layer for bioterrorism and CBRN events. Originally developed post-9/11/anthrax attacks specifically for biosurveillance. Detects unusual syndrome clusters that could indicate biological, chemical, or radiological exposures via ED visit data. The separate DoD ESSENCE instance provides military-specific CBRN syndromic surveillance. Navy/Marine Corps recently integrated with CDC NSSP instance.

L1_Surveillance.d: National Wastewater Surveillance System for pathogen monitoring in municipal wastewater

profile.overview: The National Wastewater Surveillance System (NWSS) is CDC's public health infrastructure for monitoring infectious diseases through wastewater testing. Established in September 2020 during COVID-19, it has expanded to track SARS-CoV-2, influenza, RSV, mpox, measles, and H5 bird flu across the United States.
profile.functional_scope: Wastewater sample collection and testing for pathogen genetic material, trend analysis and visualization dashboards, community-level disease activity estimation (independent of clinical testing), early warning detection of emerging variants, geographic comparison of viral activity levels across states and territories.
profile.tech_stack: Laboratory: RT-qPCR and RT-dPCR for pathogen detection, sequencing for variant identification. Data platform: DCIPHER (Defense Consortium for Health Analytics) for data management, CDC data visualization dashboards (R/Shiny). Biobot Analytics and Verily partnership for laboratory analysis. Cloud-based data infrastructure.
profile.operator: CDC (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention), with state and local health departments, wastewater utilities, and laboratory partners (Biobot Analytics, Verily/Google).
profile.data_model: Wastewater sampling data: collection date, treatment plant location, population served, pathogen concentration (gene copies/mL), flow rate normalization, variant sequences. Aggregated at community, state, and national levels.
profile.users_scale: Covers 1,500+ wastewater sampling sites serving ~140 million Americans. Monitoring for 6+ pathogens. Data published on CDC's public dashboards.
profile.access_model: Free. Public dashboards at cdc.gov/nwss. Data downloads available for researchers. Participation open to all U.S. wastewater utilities through state health departments.

L1_Surveillance.d: Free mobile data collection for field epidemiology
st: Freely available with no licensing costs

profile.overview: Epicollect5 is a free, open-source mobile and web application for data collection developed by Imperial College London and the Big Data Institute at the University of Oxford. It enables researchers and organizations to create customizable data collection forms and deploy them via smartphone apps with offline capability.
profile.functional_scope: Customizable form builder (drag-and-drop, conditional logic, photo/audio/video capture, GPS location, barcode scanning), mobile data collection (iOS and Android with offline sync), web-based data viewing and export (JSON, CSV), map visualization of geotagged entries, basic filtering and search.
profile.tech_stack: Laravel (PHP) backend, Vue.js web frontend, native Android and iOS mobile apps. MySQL/MariaDB database. REST API for data access and integration. Hosted on cloud infrastructure. Open-source on GitHub.
profile.operator: Imperial College London and Big Data Institute, University of Oxford. Funded by Wellcome Trust.
profile.data_model: Hierarchical form-based data model with configurable fields (text, numeric, date, location, media, branching). Entries stored with timestamps, GPS coordinates, and user attribution.
profile.users_scale: 488,000+ registered users, 189,000+ projects, 76+ million entries collected. Used for disease surveillance, ecology, citizen science, and field research worldwide.
profile.access_model: Free. No login required for public projects. Free account to create and manage projects. Data export in JSON and CSV.

L1_Surveillance.d: AI-enabled health analytics platform covering 160+ infectious diseases with real-time forecasting and biorisk surveillance

profile.overview: Airfinity is a commercial predictive analytics and intelligence platform for the life sciences sector, headquartered in London. It provides AI-powered forecasting, simulation, and infectious disease intelligence, serving 9 out of 10 of the world's largest pharmaceutical companies in infectious diseases.
profile.functional_scope: Nexa (AI-enabled scenario engine for drug/vaccine demand forecasting), OneID Infectious Disease Intelligence (continuously updated disease intelligence with epidemiological modeling), OneID Biorisk Intelligence (biological threat monitoring for pandemic preparedness), burden of disease modeling, competitive intelligence, and second-opinion commercial forecasts.
profile.tech_stack: Proprietary AI/ML forecasting models trained on hundreds of proprietary predictive health signals. Cloud-based SaaS platform with real-time data ingestion from global sources. Custom dashboards and API access for enterprise clients.
profile.operator: Airfinity Ltd., London, UK. Independent predictive analytics company, cited over 70,000 times in media including NYT, Reuters, Fortune.
profile.data_model: Proprietary data lake integrating epidemiological data, clinical trial registries, regulatory filings, manufacturing capacity data, supply chain data, and real-time news feeds.
profile.users_scale: Major pharmaceutical companies (9/10 top infectious disease pharma), government agencies, international organizations. Subscription-based enterprise client base.
profile.access_model: Commercial subscription (SaaS). Free public reports and press releases. Enterprise pricing for platform access and API.

st: Global coverage spanning multiple continents and regions
L1_Surveillance.d: Automated real-time disease intelligence system using AI/NLP to mine news, social media, and official reports for early detection of global infectious disease outbreaks

profile.overview: HealthMap is an automated real-time surveillance system developed at Boston Children's Hospital and Harvard Medical School. Launched in 2006, it aggregates data from diverse online sources to provide a comprehensive view of the global state of infectious diseases and their effects on human and animal health.
profile.functional_scope: Automated event-based surveillance: aggregates and classifies disease alerts from news media, government reports, ProMED-mail, WHO, and social media in multiple languages. Interactive global map visualization with disease/location/species filtering. Historical outbreak data archive. Disease Daily news summary. Flu Near You participatory surveillance.
profile.tech_stack: Natural language processing (NLP) and machine learning classifiers for automated alert extraction and categorization. GIS mapping (Leaflet/Google Maps), web scraping infrastructure, multi-language text processing. Cloud-hosted web application.
profile.operator: Computational Epidemiology Lab, Boston Children's Hospital / Harvard Medical School. Founded by Dr. John Brownstein. Funded by NIH, Google.org, and Skoll Foundation.
profile.data_model: Structured alerts with attributes: source, date, disease, location (geocoded), species, case/death counts, significance rating. Temporal and spatial indexing for trend analysis.
profile.users_scale: Millions of annual visitors. Used by CDC, WHO, DHS, DoD, and public health agencies worldwide as an early warning tool.
profile.access_model: Free, publicly accessible web application. No login required. API available for research use.

L1_Surveillance.d: AI-powered infectious disease intelligence for early warning and prediction

profile.overview: BlueDot is a Canadian AI-powered infectious disease intelligence company founded in 2013 by Dr. Kamran Khan (University of Toronto). Notably alerted clients to COVID-19 five days before the WHO's official statement in December 2019.
profile.functional_scope: Global surveillance monitoring 190+ infectious diseases/syndromes. AI-driven early warning alerts, disease forecasting (weeks to years ahead), risk assessments, outbreak suitability modeling, population vulnerability analysis, and medical countermeasure demand prediction.
profile.tech_stack: Proprietary AI/ML platform processing news, airline ticketing data, climate data, and official reports in 65+ languages. Natural language processing, epidemiological modeling, geospatial analytics. Cloud-hosted SaaS platform.
profile.operator: BlueDot Inc., Toronto, Canada. VC-backed with investment from Diagram Ventures. 100+ peer-reviewed scientific publications co-authored by the team.
profile.data_model: Multi-source data integration: global news feeds, airline passenger itineraries, official surveillance reports, climate/environmental data. Structured disease event objects with geolocation, severity, and trajectory projections.
profile.users_scale: Intelligence protects over 840 million people worldwide. Clients include governments, WHO, top 10 pharma companies, major enterprises. Saves users 3+ months annually in curated alert processing.
profile.access_model: Commercial SaaS subscription model. Tiered pricing for governments, pharma/life sciences, and enterprise. Demo available via request. Some public-facing reports and research publications freely available.

L1_Surveillance.d: Expert-curated global outbreak reporting system operated by ISID since 1994, providing early event-based surveillance of emerging infectious diseases through moderated alerts

profile.overview: ProMED (Program for Monitoring Emerging Diseases) is a global electronic reporting system for outbreaks of emerging infectious diseases and toxins, operated by the International Society for Infectious Diseases (ISID). Established in 1994, it is the largest publicly available system conducting global reporting of infectious disease outbreaks.
profile.functional_scope: Moderated global disease reporting covering human, animal, and plant diseases. Expert-curated alerts with analysis and commentary from a global network of subject-matter experts. Multilingual reporting (English, Spanish, Portuguese, Russian, others). Covers all infectious agents: bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites, prions, and toxins.
profile.tech_stack: Web-based publishing platform with email distribution system. Expert moderation workflow. Searchable archive database. RSS feeds and email subscriptions for real-time alerts.
profile.operator: International Society for Infectious Diseases (ISID), Brookline, MA, USA. Founded by Dr. Jack Woodall and Dr. Stephen Morse. Partly funded by NLM, Google.org, and the Skoll Global Threats Fund.
profile.data_model: Structured disease reports with date, disease, location, species, case/death counts, source attribution, and expert commentary. Archive of 100,000+ reports since 1994.
profile.users_scale: Over 80,000 subscribers in 185+ countries. Reports are cited by WHO, CDC, FAO, and OIE as primary intelligence sources.
profile.access_model: Free and publicly accessible. Email subscription available. Archive searchable online. No login required for reading.

L1_Surveillance.d: WHO-supported software for management and analysis of antimicrobial susceptibility testing data from microbiology laboratories, used in 130+ countries

profile.overview: WHONET is a free Windows desktop application for antimicrobial resistance (AMR) surveillance, developed and maintained by the WHO Collaborating Centre for Surveillance of Antimicrobial Resistance at Brigham and Women's Hospital, Boston. Available in 54 languages, it is the standard tool for AMR data management worldwide.
profile.functional_scope: Laboratory configuration, microbiology data entry, automated interpretation (CLSI and EUCAST breakpoints, updated annually), data analysis (resistance rates, trends, organism profiles), public health reporting (GLASS, EARS-Net), data encryption. BacLink module for importing data from existing LIS, instruments, and desktop applications.
profile.tech_stack: Microsoft Windows desktop application (C++/Visual Basic). Microsoft Access/SQL Server database. BacLink data import utility. Compatible with Windows 10/11 (32-bit and 64-bit versions). Integration with WHONET-SaTScan for spatial cluster detection.
profile.operator: WHO Collaborating Centre for Surveillance of Antimicrobial Resistance, Brigham and Women's Hospital, Boston, MA. Led by Dr. John Stelling. Supported by WHO.
profile.data_model: Microbiology data: organism identifications, antimicrobial susceptibility test results (MIC, disk diffusion), specimen types, patient demographics. Standardized coding (SNOMED, LOINC, ATC).
profile.users_scale: Over 2,300 hospital, public health, animal health, and food laboratories in 130+ countries.
profile.access_model: Free download from whonet.org. No login required. Training materials freely available. Community forum for support.

L1_Surveillance.d: Biothreats Emergence Analysis and Communications Network - open-source AI+human expert global disease surveillance platform launched April 2025 by Boston University CEID

profile.overview: BEACON (Biothreats Emergence Analysis and Communications Network) is an open-source AI+human hybrid epidemic intelligence platform developed at Boston University's Center for Emerging Infectious Diseases Policy and Research, building on the HealthMap foundation.
profile.functional_scope: AI-powered open-source epidemic surveillance combining automated event detection, natural language processing of news/social media/ProMED reports, human analyst verification, and alert dissemination for emerging biothreats.
profile.tech_stack: Python/NLP pipeline for multilingual text mining. Machine learning classifiers for disease event extraction. Web-based analyst dashboard. Open-source codebase. Integration with HealthMap data feeds.
profile.operator: Boston University Center for Emerging Infectious Diseases Policy and Research. Funded by DTRA, NIH, and philanthropic sources. Academic partnership with Harvard/Children's Hospital (HealthMap).
profile.data_model: Structured disease events extracted from unstructured text sources. Event objects with disease, location, date, source URL, severity, and analyst confidence scores. Historical archive for trend analysis.
profile.users_scale: Used by US government agencies (DTRA, DoD), international health organizations, and academic researchers. Thousands of subscribers to alert feeds. Complements HealthMap and ProMED networks.
profile.access_model: Open-source platform. Free access to public alerts. Enhanced analytics available to government/institutional partners. API access for integration with other surveillance systems.

L1_Surveillance.d: National Notifiable Diseases Surveillance System for US reportable conditions
wk: Limited to US domestic coverage with no international data integration

profile.overview: The National Notifiable Diseases Surveillance System (NNDSS) is the CDC's foundational case surveillance system collecting reportable disease data from all US states and territories. Operational since 1961 (predecessor system since 1878).
profile.functional_scope: Collects case-level surveillance data for 120+ notifiable conditions (infectious and non-infectious) from state/territorial health departments. Produces MMWR weekly reports, annual summary tables, and provisional data. Supports Message Mapping Guide (MMG) standardized reporting.
profile.tech_stack: HL7 messaging (transitioning to FHIR). National Electronic Disease Surveillance System (NEDSS) as backend. BioSense Platform integration. CDC WONDER for public data access. EDX (Enterprise Data Exchange) for case notifications.
profile.operator: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), Atlanta, GA, USA. Division of Health Informatics and Surveillance. All 50 states, DC, and territories report mandatorily.
profile.data_model: Case-level surveillance data with standardized fields per condition (demographics, lab results, outcomes). MMG format for electronic transmission. Deidentified aggregates published weekly/annually. Provisional vs finalized data distinction.
profile.users_scale: Covers the entire US population (~330M). Every state/territorial health department reports. Data used by thousands of epidemiologists, researchers, and policymakers. MMWR reaches millions of public health professionals.
profile.access_model: Public aggregate data via CDC WONDER and MMWR tables. Case-level data restricted to authorized public health personnel. No registration for published tables. Research access through CDC data request process.

L1_Surveillance.d: CDC's free cloud-based data exchange pipeline for routing public health test results from labs to health departments using HL7/FHIR standards

profile.overview: ReportStream is CDC's free, interoperable data platform that connects healthcare organizations, labs, and public health agencies for streamlined electronic reporting of public health data. It is a key component of CDC's Data Modernization Initiative.
profile.functional_scope: Automated routing and transformation of public health reports from healthcare providers and labs to state and local health departments. Supports Electronic Lab Reports (ELR), Electronic Case Reports (eCR), and Electronic Test Orders and Results (ETOR). Data format translation between HL7v2, FHIR, and CSV. Universal data pipeline with configurable routing rules.
profile.tech_stack: Kotlin/Java backend, React.js frontend, Azure cloud infrastructure. Supports HL7v2.5.1, FHIR R4, CSV data formats. REST API with OAuth 2.0 authentication. Open-source on GitHub (USDS/prime-reportstream). PostgreSQL database.
profile.operator: U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), built by the U.S. Digital Service (USDS) and contractors.
profile.data_model: HL7-structured public health messages with patient, specimen, test, result, and provider data. FHIR Bundle resources for modern interoperability. Configurable schema mapping per jurisdiction.
profile.users_scale: Connected to thousands of labs and healthcare facilities. Routes millions of reports monthly to 60+ state and territorial health departments across the United States.
profile.access_model: Free for U.S. public health entities. API-based onboarding. Open-source codebase.

L1_Surveillance.d: Stanford-led national wastewater surveillance tracking 12+ infectious disease targets

profile.overview: WastewaterSCAN is a Stanford University-led national wastewater surveillance network launched in 2020 by Professors Alexandria Boehm and Marlene Wolfe. It monitors infectious disease trends through wastewater analysis across the United States.
profile.functional_scope: Tracks 12+ infectious disease targets in wastewater including SARS-CoV-2, RSV, Influenza A/B, Norovirus, Mpox, and emerging pathogens. Provides national/regional/site-level trend dashboards with pathogen concentration time series. Regional breakdown (Midwest, Northeast, South, West).
profile.tech_stack: RT-qPCR and digital PCR for pathogen quantification. Genomic sequencing for variant tracking. Custom data pipeline and interactive web dashboard (React-based). Statistical normalization methods for population adjustment.
profile.operator: Stanford University (Boehm Lab, Wolfe Lab), with Emory University. Funded by CDC, NIAID, Stanford. Academic research network with public health partnerships.
profile.data_model: Wastewater pathogen concentration time series, normalized per capita. Site-level metadata (population served, collection frequency). Regional and national aggregations. Downloadable datasets.
profile.users_scale: Monitors wastewater from facilities serving millions of Americans. Public dashboard used by health departments, researchers, journalists. Cited in 50+ peer-reviewed publications.
profile.access_model: Free public access to dashboard and data at data.wastewaterscan.org. Downloadable datasets. Open publications. Research collaborations welcome.

L1_Surveillance.d: Commercial wastewater epidemiology platform for population health monitoring

profile.overview: Biobot Analytics is the world's first and most advanced commercial wastewater epidemiology company, founded in 2017 by MIT researchers Mariana Matus and Newsha Ghaeli. Headquartered in Cambridge, MA, it pioneered population-level health monitoring through wastewater analysis.
profile.functional_scope: Tracks 50+ human health trends including 24+ pathogens (SARS-CoV-2, RSV, Influenza, Norovirus, H5N1, Mpox, HIV, Measles, EEE, Dengue, Zika, etc.) and 25+ substances (fentanyl, cocaine, methamphetamine, benzodiazepines). Genomic sequencing for variant surveillance. National and localized trend dashboards.
profile.tech_stack: RT-qPCR, digital PCR, and next-generation sequencing. Proprietary data normalization and analytics platform. Cloud-hosted dashboards. Automated sample processing laboratory.
profile.operator: Biobot Analytics Inc., Cambridge, MA, USA. VC-backed ($70M+ raised). Partners include CDC, HHS, state/local health departments, pharma companies.
profile.data_model: Wastewater pathogen/substance concentration time series. Population-normalized metrics. Geographic granularity from national to treatment plant level. Trend indicators and regional comparisons.
profile.users_scale: Partners with 500+ communities across all 50 US states. Data used by CDC, multiple federal agencies, state health departments, pharma companies. Real-time data for population health monitoring.
profile.access_model: Commercial SaaS subscription. Government contracts (CDC National Wastewater Surveillance System). Free public dashboard for select national indicators. Enterprise API for programmatic access.

L1_Surveillance.d: DTRA Biosurveillance Ecosystem - multi-source disease forecasting platform correlating epidemiological, environmental, and social data for military operations

profile.overview: The Biosurveillance Ecosystem (BSVE) is the US Defense Threat Reduction Agency's (DTRA) integrated multi-source disease forecasting and biosurveillance platform. Serves as a one-stop analytical environment for DoD biological threat analysis.
profile.functional_scope: Multi-source data fusion for biosurveillance: integrates clinical surveillance, social media signals, news mining, environmental data, genomic data, and epidemiological models. Disease forecasting, outbreak detection, geospatial analysis, and biological threat assessment.
profile.tech_stack: Cloud-based analytical environment with plugin architecture. Integrates DTRA-funded models and tools. GIS/mapping layer, NLP for open-source intelligence, epidemiological modeling engines. Classified and unclassified instances.
profile.operator: Defense Threat Reduction Agency (DTRA), US Department of Defense, Fort Belvoir, VA, USA. Developed with multiple defense contractors and academic partners.
profile.data_model: Fused multi-source data: epidemiological reports, genomic sequences, satellite imagery, social media feeds, clinical records, weather/climate data. Standardized event objects with spatiotemporal metadata.
profile.users_scale: Primary users are DoD analysts, combatant commands, intelligence community, and select interagency partners (CDC, DHS, State Department). Serves hundreds of military/civilian analysts.
