profile.overview: EnteroBase is a web-based platform for the genomic epidemiology of enteric pathogens, developed by Mark Achtman's group at the University of Warwick (now hosted with DSMZ for Mycobacterium). It assembles, stores, and provides integrated analysis of bacterial genomic data at massive scale.
profile.functional_scope: Automated genome assembly from raw reads (SRA), MLST typing (7-gene, cgMLST, wgMLST, rMLST), Hierarchical Clustering (HierCC) for population structure, SNP-based phylogenetics, GrapeTree visualization (minimum spanning trees), metadata search and filtering. Covers Salmonella, E. coli/Shigella, Clostridioides, Vibrio, Yersinia, Helicobacter, Moraxella, Streptococcus, and M. tuberculosis.
profile.tech_stack: Custom web application (Python/Flask), PostgreSQL database, automated assembly pipeline (Velvet, SPAdes), custom MLST calling engine, GrapeTree (JavaScript MST visualization). High-performance computing cluster for genome assembly. REST API.
profile.operator: University of Warwick (Mark Achtman lab) for most species; DSMZ (Leibniz Institute) for M. tuberculosis. Funded by Wellcome Trust and BBSRC.
profile.data_model: Over 1.65 million bacterial strains with assembled genomes, MLST profiles (multiple schemes per species), HierCC clusters, and metadata (source, country, date, serovar). Linked to SRA, BioSample, and PubMLST.
profile.users_scale: Contains 1,656,331+ strains. Used by thousands of researchers and public health agencies globally for tracking Salmonella, E. coli, and other enteric pathogen outbreaks.
profile.access_model: Free, web-based access. Login required for querying and analysis. Data downloads available. API for programmatic access.

all.d: DTU/Novo Nordisk Foundation funded Global Pathogen Analysis Platform providing free AI-based genomic analytical toolkit for scientists and public health authorities worldwide

profile.overview: The Global Pathogen Analysis Platform (GPAP) is a web-based bioinformatics resource developed by the Technical University of Denmark (DTU) National Food Institute, funded by the Novo Nordisk Foundation's Genomics Challenge Programme.
profile.functional_scope: Automated analysis of bacterial pathogen genomes for public health surveillance: species identification, MLST/cgMLST typing, AMR prediction (ResFinder), virulence gene detection, plasmid identification, phylogenetic analysis. Focused on food safety and One Health surveillance.
profile.tech_stack: Web-based platform integrating DTU's Center for Genomic Epidemiology (CGE) tools: ResFinder, MLST, VirulenceFinder, PlasmidFinder, KmerFinder. Nextflow pipelines. Docker containers. REST API.
profile.operator: Technical University of Denmark (DTU), National Food Institute, Center for Genomic Epidemiology. Funded by Novo Nordisk Foundation Challenge Programme.
profile.data_model: Bacterial genome assemblies (FASTA) and raw reads (FASTQ) as input. Structured output: species ID, sequence types, resistance genes, virulence factors, plasmid replicons. Standardized reports in JSON/TSV.
profile.users_scale: Used by European food safety laboratories, WHO collaborating centres, ECDC, and national reference labs in 30+ countries. Part of the CGE ecosystem serving thousands of users monthly.
profile.access_model: Free web-based access at gpap.global. Registration required for analysis. Open-source tool components. No cost for academic or public health use.

all.d: Web-based tool for identification of acquired antimicrobial resistance genes

profile.overview: ResFinder is a web-based tool for identification of acquired antimicrobial resistance genes in bacterial whole genome sequence data, developed by the Center for Genomic Epidemiology (CGE) at DTU, Denmark. One of the most widely used AMR detection tools globally.
profile.functional_scope: Identifies acquired AMR genes from assembled genomes or raw reads using curated database of known resistance genes. Reports gene identity, coverage, drug class affected. Complemented by PointFinder (chromosomal mutations) and DisinFinder (disinfectant resistance).
profile.tech_stack: Python backend, BLAST-based sequence alignment. Web interface built on CGE server infrastructure. Available as command-line tool, Docker container, and web service. REST API. Database updated regularly.
profile.operator: Center for Genomic Epidemiology (CGE), DTU National Food Institute, Technical University of Denmark, Lyngby, Denmark.
profile.data_model: Curated database of acquired resistance gene sequences organized by drug class. Input: FASTA/FASTQ files. Output: gene matches with percent identity, coverage, drug class, and phenotype predictions. Tab-separated results files.
profile.users_scale: Millions of analyses performed. Cited in 2,000+ publications. Used by WHO GLASS network, ECDC, national reference laboratories, and academic researchers globally. Key tool in European genomic surveillance.
profile.access_model: Free web access at cge.food.dtu.dk. Command-line version available on GitHub. Database downloadable. No registration required for basic web analysis. Open-source under Apache 2.0.

all.d: US Army Medical Research Institute of Infectious Diseases - premier military biodefense laboratory at Fort Detrick with BSL-4 capabilities

profile.overview: USAMRIID (United States Army Medical Research Institute of Infectious Diseases) is the DoD's lead laboratory for medical biological defense research, located at Fort Detrick, Maryland. Its biosurveillance capabilities support detection, identification, and characterization of biological threat agents.
profile.functional_scope: Research on medical countermeasures (vaccines, therapeutics, diagnostics) against biological threat agents. BSL-2 through BSL-4 laboratory capabilities for working with the most dangerous pathogens. Diagnostic reference laboratory for biological threat agents. Training and consultation for biodefense response.
profile.tech_stack: BSL-4 maximum containment laboratories, whole genome sequencing (Illumina, PacBio, Nanopore), mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF), PCR-based diagnostics, cell culture and animal model facilities. Integrated with DoD medical surveillance systems.
profile.operator: U.S. Army Medical Research and Development Command (USAMRDC), Fort Detrick, Maryland, USA.
profile.data_model: Pathogen characterization data, diagnostic assay results, genomic sequence data, therapeutic efficacy data. Classified and unclassified data handling.
profile.users_scale: Serves the entire U.S. military and supports national biodefense. Reference laboratory for CDC's Laboratory Response Network (LRN). International partnerships.
profile.access_model: DoD facility with controlled access. Published research in open literature. Diagnostic services available to DoD and federal agencies.

all.d: DTRA Biosurveillance Ecosystem - multi-source disease forecasting platform correlating epidemiological, environmental, and social data for military operations

profile.overview: The Biosurveillance Ecosystem (BSVE) is the US Defense Threat Reduction Agency's (DTRA) integrated multi-source disease forecasting and biosurveillance platform. Serves as a one-stop analytical environment for DoD biological threat analysis.
profile.functional_scope: Multi-source data fusion for biosurveillance: integrates clinical surveillance, social media signals, news mining, environmental data, genomic data, and epidemiological models. Disease forecasting, outbreak detection, geospatial analysis, and biological threat assessment.
profile.tech_stack: Cloud-based analytical environment with plugin architecture. Integrates DTRA-funded models and tools. GIS/mapping layer, NLP for open-source intelligence, epidemiological modeling engines. Classified and unclassified instances.
profile.operator: Defense Threat Reduction Agency (DTRA), US Department of Defense, Fort Belvoir, VA, USA. Developed with multiple defense contractors and academic partners.
profile.data_model: Fused multi-source data: epidemiological reports, genomic sequences, satellite imagery, social media feeds, clinical records, weather/climate data. Standardized event objects with spatiotemporal metadata.
profile.users_scale: Primary users are DoD analysts, combatant commands, intelligence community, and select interagency partners (CDC, DHS, State Department). Serves hundreds of military/civilian analysts.
profile.access_model: Restricted to authorized US government and military personnel. Classified and unclassified versions. No public access. Access via DoD network credentials and need-to-know basis.

all.d: bioMérieux defense division providing FDA-cleared rapid multiplex PCR panels for field-forward biothreat agent identification

profile.overview: BioFire Defense is a subsidiary of bioMerieux specializing in syndromic molecular diagnostic and biothreat detection solutions for military and public health applications. Its FilmArray and SPOTFIRE platforms use multiplex PCR to deliver rapid, comprehensive pathogen identification from clinical and environmental samples.
profile.functional_scope: Clinical IVD panels (respiratory, blood culture, GI, meningitis/encephalitis), biothreat IVD panels (detecting biological warfare agents), CBRN field detection (RAZOR MK II portable PCR for field deployment), and external quality controls. TORCH system for high-throughput laboratory testing. SPOTFIRE for point-of-care testing.
profile.tech_stack: Proprietary nested multiplex PCR in sealed pouch system (single-use reagent pouches). FilmArray TORCH (high-throughput automated system), RAZOR MK II (portable, battery-powered field PCR), SPOTFIRE (point-of-care). Custom firmware and analysis software. CLIA-waived operation for some panels.
profile.operator: BioFire Defense LLC (subsidiary of bioMerieux), Salt Lake City, Utah, USA. Serves U.S. DoD, DTRA, DHS, and allied military forces.
profile.data_model: Test results: pathogen panel results (detected/not detected), sample metadata. Encrypted result data. Chain of custody tracking for CBRN applications.
profile.users_scale: Deployed across U.S. military medical facilities, field hospitals, mobile laboratories, and allied nation forces. TORCH/SPOTFIRE systems in civilian hospitals worldwide.
profile.access_model: Commercial product. Government contracts for military applications. Clinical systems available through bioMerieux distribution. Training and support included.

all.d: University of Sydney AI-powered open-source epidemic observatory

profile.overview: EPIWATCH is an AI-powered open-source epidemic observatory developed by the University of Sydney's Biosecurity Program, led by Professor Raina MacIntyre. Uses AI and machine learning for early detection of emerging disease outbreaks globally.
profile.functional_scope: Real-time AI-driven epidemic intelligence scanning open-source data (news, social media, official reports) in multiple languages. Automated anomaly detection, disease event classification, outbreak early warning, and risk assessment. Focused on pandemic preparedness and biosecurity.
profile.tech_stack: Machine learning and NLP classifiers for disease event extraction. Web scraping and data aggregation pipelines. Interactive web dashboard. Cloud-hosted analytics platform.
profile.operator: University of Sydney, Kirby Institute/Biosecurity Program, Sydney, Australia. Led by Professor Raina MacIntyre. Funded by Australian government and research grants.
profile.data_model: Structured disease event records from unstructured sources. Each event tagged with pathogen, location, date, severity, and source. Time series of outbreak signals with geolocation. Exportable alert feeds.
profile.users_scale: Used by Australian health authorities, WHO, and international public health partners. Thousands of users accessing the public dashboard. Active research output with regular publications.
profile.access_model: Open-source platform with free public access to alerts and dashboards at epiwatch.org. API access for institutional partners. Enhanced analytics for government subscribers.

all.d: Canada's integrated rapid infectious disease analysis platform connecting genomic data, epidemiological metadata, and analytical pipelines for public health genomics

profile.overview: IRIDA (Integrated Rapid Infectious Disease Analysis) is an open-source bioinformatics platform designed to make genomic epidemiology accessible to public health workers, epidemiologists, and clinical microbiologists. Developed primarily by the National Microbiology Laboratory of Canada.
profile.functional_scope: Sequencing data management (upload, organize, share), integrated bioinformatics pipelines for public health genomics (SNVPhyl for SNP phylogenetics, SISTR for Salmonella typing, refseq_masher for species identification, assembly/annotation), metadata management with genomic epidemiology ontology, data visualization (phylogenetic trees, metadata overlays), and REST API for external tool integration.
profile.tech_stack: Java/Spring Boot backend, AngularJS frontend, Galaxy for pipeline execution, PostgreSQL database. Docker deployment. REST API. Open-source on GitHub under Apache 2.0 license.
profile.operator: Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC) / National Microbiology Laboratory (NML), in collaboration with Simon Fraser University. Funded by PHAC and Genome Canada.
profile.data_model: Project-based organization with samples, sequence files (FASTQ), metadata, and analysis results. Ontology-based metadata harmonization linking genomic, epidemiological, lab, and clinical data.
profile.users_scale: Deployed in Canadian public health laboratories and international partners. Public demonstration instance hosted by Simon Fraser University.
profile.access_model: Free, open-source. Self-hosted deployment. Role-based access control with project-level sharing.

all.c: US DoD CBRN Defense Acquisition Executive
all.d: Joint Program Executive Office for CBRN Defense - manages $1.6B+ Chemical and Biological Defense Program delivering CBRN defense capabilities to all US services

profile.overview: The Joint Program Executive Office for Chemical, Biological, Radiological, and Nuclear Defense (JPEO-CBRND) is the US Department of Defense's principal acquisition office for CBRN defense equipment and capabilities, managing a $1.6B+ annual portfolio.
profile.functional_scope: Manages development, procurement, and fielding of CBRN defense capabilities: biological detection systems (JBPDS, IBAC), chemical detectors, decontamination systems, medical countermeasures (vaccines, therapeutics, diagnostics), protective equipment, and early warning systems.
profile.tech_stack: Portfolio of defense technologies spanning point detection (bio-aerosol), standoff detection, medical diagnostics, next-gen sequencing (for bioforensics), modeling/simulation tools, and integrated C2 systems.
profile.operator: US Department of Defense, reporting to the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment. Located at Aberdeen Proving Ground, MD. Joint service program supporting Army, Navy, Air Force, Marines.
profile.data_model: Acquisition program data, test & evaluation results, threat characterization databases, medical countermeasure stockpile data. CBRN threat libraries and defense capability assessments.
profile.users_scale: Equips all US military branches plus allied forces through Foreign Military Sales. Programs protect millions of service members. Coordinates with BARDA, HHS, DHS for dual-use technologies.
profile.access_model: Government/military acquisition program. Equipment procured through defense contracting. Technical data controlled (ITAR/EAR). Public program information at jpeocbrnd.osd.mil. Congressional oversight reporting.

all.d: Commercial cloud platform for microbial identification and AMR analysis

profile.overview: One Codex is a commercial cloud-based metagenomics analysis platform founded in 2014, specializing in microbial identification and antimicrobial resistance analysis for clinical, pharmaceutical, and research applications.
profile.functional_scope: Metagenomic classification using curated database of 130,000+ microbial genomes. Species identification, AMR gene detection, microbiome profiling, pathogen detection from clinical samples. Supports shotgun metagenomics, 16S, and targeted panels. CLIA-validated workflows for clinical use.
profile.tech_stack: Proprietary k-mer based classification algorithms. Curated reference database (One Codex Database). Cloud computing infrastructure (AWS). RESTful API. Web dashboard and Python CLI. Jupyter notebook integration.
profile.operator: One Codex (Reference Genomics Inc.), San Francisco, CA, USA. VC-backed. Partnerships with clinical labs, pharma companies, and academic institutions.
profile.data_model: Taxonomic classification results with abundance estimates. AMR gene presence/absence. Sample metadata. FASTQ/FASTA input. JSON/CSV output. Interactive visualizations (Krona charts, heatmaps, PCA).
profile.users_scale: Used by top academic labs, biotech companies developing live biotherapeutics (LBPs), and clinical laboratories. Customers include research institutions, pharmaceutical companies, and diagnostic labs.
profile.access_model: Commercial SaaS platform with tiered pricing. Free tier for academic/small-scale use (limited analyses). Enterprise plans for clinical/pharma. API access included. CLIA-validated option for clinical laboratories.

all.d: Platform for managing Nextflow bioinformatics pipelines at scale (formerly Nextflow Tower)
st: Horizontally scalable architecture supporting growing data volumes

profile.overview: Seqera Platform (formerly Nextflow Tower) is the commercial workflow orchestration platform built by Seqera Labs, the creators of the Nextflow workflow language. Provides enterprise-grade management of bioinformatics pipelines at scale.
profile.functional_scope: Pipeline orchestration and monitoring for bioinformatics workflows. Manages Nextflow pipelines across cloud (AWS, GCP, Azure) and on-premises HPC infrastructure. Provides data management (Data Studios), compute environments, workflow launchpad, run monitoring, and team collaboration. AI agents for scientific workflows.
profile.tech_stack: Built on Nextflow (Groovy/Java). Supports AWS Batch, Google Life Sciences, Azure Batch, Slurm, PBS, LSF, SGE. Kubernetes deployment. REST API. Web UI (React). Integrates with nf-core community pipelines (100+ validated workflows).
profile.operator: Seqera Labs, Barcelona, Spain. Founded by Paolo Di Tommaso (Nextflow creator). Backed by $50M+ in funding. Partners include Roche, AstraZeneca, Moderna, Sanger Institute, Oxford Nanopore.
profile.data_model: Workflow definitions (Nextflow DSL2), pipeline parameters, compute environment configs, run metadata, task-level resource consumption. Supports all bioinformatics data formats through pipeline encapsulation.
profile.users_scale: Used by world's leading scientific teams: Roche, AstraZeneca, Moderna, Sanger Institute, SciLifeLab, CRG. Nextflow has 5,000+ GitHub stars and is the most widely used scientific workflow manager.
profile.access_model: Commercial SaaS (Seqera Cloud) and self-hosted (Seqera Enterprise) options. Free community tier for small teams. Enterprise licensing for large organizations. Nextflow itself is open-source (Apache 2.0).

all.d: Cloud-based, no-code genomic pathogen surveillance platform from Israel

profile.overview: Solu Genomics (now Solu) is an Israeli cloud-based genomic pathogen surveillance platform providing no-code bioinformatics tools for public health laboratories and researchers.
profile.functional_scope: Cloud-based no-code platform for genomic pathogen surveillance. Automated analysis pipelines for pathogen identification, lineage assignment, phylogenetic analysis, AMR detection, and outbreak investigation. Supports SARS-CoV-2, TB, foodborne pathogens, and other infectious agents.
profile.tech_stack: Cloud-native SaaS platform. Automated bioinformatics pipelines (assembly, mapping, variant calling). Web-based interface with drag-and-drop functionality. API integration. No local computational requirements.
profile.operator: Solu Genomics Ltd., Israel. Startup backed by venture capital. Partners with national public health agencies and international organizations.
profile.data_model: FASTQ sequence reads as input. Automated processing to assemblies, variant calls, lineage assignments, phylogenetic trees. Structured reports with resistance profiles and epidemiological context.
profile.users_scale: Deployed in public health laboratories across multiple countries. Used by national reference laboratories for routine surveillance. Focused on low-resource settings needing accessible bioinformatics.
profile.access_model: Commercial SaaS subscription model. Institutional pricing for public health labs. Free trial/demo available. Cloud-based (no local installation required).

all.d: Interactive exploration of extremely large phylogenetic trees in the browser

profile.overview: Taxonium is an open-source web-based tool for exploring extremely large phylogenetic trees, developed by Theo Sanderson at the Francis Crick Institute/Wellcome Sanger Institute. Capable of rendering trees with millions of nodes interactively in the browser.
profile.functional_scope: Interactive exploration of massive phylogenetic trees (millions of tips). Search by metadata, zoom/pan, color by attributes, time-scaled views. Supports SARS-CoV-2 global trees (USHER/UShER), bacterial phylogenies, and any Newick/Nexus tree with metadata.
profile.tech_stack: WebGL-based rendering for performance with millions of nodes. React frontend, Node.js backend. Supports Newick, Nexus, and UShER protobuf tree formats. Deployed on Vercel. Open-source on GitHub (theosanderson/taxonium).
profile.operator: Theo Sanderson, Francis Crick Institute / Wellcome Sanger Institute, London, UK. Community open-source project.
profile.data_model: Phylogenetic tree structures (Newick/Nexus/protobuf) with associated metadata (CSV/TSV). Supports date, location, lineage, and custom annotations. Client-side rendering with server-side data processing for large trees.
profile.users_scale: Widely used for SARS-CoV-2 phylogenetics (6M+ sequence global tree). Used by genomic epidemiologists, public health agencies, and researchers. Taxonium.org public instance for SARS-CoV-2.
profile.access_model: Free and open-source (MIT license). Public instance at taxonium.org. Self-hostable. No registration required. Upload your own trees or explore public datasets.

all.d: Ultra-fast taxonomic classification and abundance estimation for metagenomic data
wk: Command-line interface only; no web GUI limits accessibility for non-technical users

profile.overview: Kraken2 is an ultrafast taxonomic classification system for metagenomic sequences, developed by Derrick Wood and Steven Salzberg at Johns Hopkins University. Bracken (Bayesian Reestimation of Abundance with KrakEN) provides abundance estimation from Kraken2 output.
profile.functional_scope: Taxonomic classification of DNA/RNA sequences against reference databases using exact k-mer matching. Classifies reads to species/genus/family level in seconds. Bracken re-estimates species-level abundance using Bayesian statistics. Supports custom database building.
profile.tech_stack: C++ implementation with memory-mapped hash tables. Minimizer-based k-mer indexing (16GB standard database, 8GB mini). Bracken in Python. Available via Bioconda, Docker. Source on GitHub (DerrickWood/kraken2). Integration with Galaxy, QIIME2.
profile.operator: Center for Computational Biology (CCB), Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, MD, USA. Derrick Wood, Steven Salzberg lab. Open-source community contributions.
profile.data_model: Input: FASTQ/FASTA reads. K-mer based lookup against reference databases (RefSeq complete genomes, NCBI nt). Output: per-read classification, Krona-compatible reports, species abundance tables. MPA-style reports for downstream tools.
profile.users_scale: One of the most-cited metagenomics tools globally (10,000+ citations). Used by virtually every metagenomics research group. Integrated into clinical metagenomics pipelines, CDC workflows, and thousands of research projects.
profile.access_model: Completely free and open-source (MIT license). Pre-built databases available for download. Bioconda, Docker, and source installation. No restrictions. Standard reference databases hosted at Johns Hopkins.

all.d: UK Health Security Agency's national-scale bioinformatics pipeline for standardized processing of pathogen whole-genome sequencing data across England

profile.overview: The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA, formerly Public Health England/PHE) national-scale bioinformatics pipeline for standardized genomic analysis of pathogens. Powers the UK's world-leading genomic surveillance infrastructure.
profile.functional_scope: National-scale standardized bioinformatics for pathogen genomic surveillance: assembly, mapping, variant calling, typing (MLST, cgMLST), AMR detection, phylogenetics, and cluster detection. Supports surveillance of SARS-CoV-2, TB, Salmonella, E. coli, Listeria, and dozens of other pathogens.
profile.tech_stack: Nextflow-based pipelines running on UKHSA cloud/HPC infrastructure. Integrates ARIBA, Snippy, SPAdes, Trimmomatic, BWA, SAMtools. SnapperDB for SNP database clustering. Internal pipeline registry. Integration with CLIMB cloud computing.
profile.operator: UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA), London, UK. Part of the Department of Health and Social Care. Genomic services division.
profile.data_model: Standardized output per pathogen: assemblies, VCFs, typing results, cluster assignments. Integration with UKHSA's SGSS (Second Generation Surveillance System). Submits to ENA/INSDC. Structured reports for public health action.
profile.users_scale: Processes all pathogen genomic data for England (population 56M). During COVID-19, processed 3M+ SARS-CoV-2 genomes (more than any other country per capita). Serves NHS labs, PHE reference laboratories.
profile.access_model: Internal UKHSA system. Some pipeline components open-sourced on GitHub. Data submissions to public archives (ENA). Aggregate surveillance data published through GOV.UK. Not directly accessible to external users.

all.d: FAO/WHO/WOAH Global Early Warning System for health threats at the human-animal-environment interface

profile.overview: GLEWS+ (Global Early Warning System) is a joint FAO/WHO/WOAH (OIE) initiative for early detection and assessment of health threats at the human-animal-environment interface, established as part of the One Health Tripartite collaboration.
profile.functional_scope: Early warning for zoonotic diseases and other health threats at the human-animal-ecosystem interface. Risk assessment, event verification, joint rapid risk analysis, and coordinated response across the three agencies. Monitors emerging diseases, AMR, food safety.
profile.tech_stack: Event-based surveillance integrating FAO EMPRES-i, WHO Disease Outbreak News, WOAH WAHIS data streams. Risk assessment frameworks, joint analysis protocols, GIS mapping.
profile.operator: FAO (Rome), WHO (Geneva), WOAH (Paris) - Tripartite One Health partnership. Supported by international donor governments.
profile.data_model: Multi-source event data: animal disease outbreaks, human disease alerts, environmental signals. Structured joint risk assessment reports. GIS-referenced event records.
profile.users_scale: Serves 194 WHO member states and FAO/WOAH member countries. Used by national veterinary and public health authorities worldwide. Core One Health surveillance coordination.
profile.access_model: Intergovernmental system. Published risk assessments available via FAO/WHO/WOAH portals. Alert feed access for national focal points. Technical reports freely downloadable.

all.d: CDC pipeline for whole-genome sequencing analysis of bacterial isolates
wk: Command-line interface only; no web GUI limits accessibility for non-technical users

profile.overview: PHoeNIx (Public Health bioinformaticsvNIx) is CDC's production WGS analysis pipeline for bacterial pathogens, hosted on GitHub under CDCgov. Standardizes genomic analysis for public health surveillance and outbreak response.
profile.functional_scope: Complete bacterial WGS analysis: quality control, de novo assembly, species identification, MLST/cgMLST typing, AMR detection (AMRFinderPlus), virulence gene identification, and phylogenetic analysis. Supports food-borne, healthcare-associated, and emerging pathogens.
profile.tech_stack: Nextflow pipeline with Docker/Singularity containers. Integrates SPAdes, Trimmomatic, AMRFinderPlus, MLST, Kraken2. Runs on HPC and cloud. CDC's internal production pipeline. Open-source on GitHub.
profile.operator: CDC Division of Healthcare Quality Promotion and other CDC divisions. CDC Advanced Molecular Detection (AMD) program.
profile.data_model: FASTQ input, structured output with QC metrics, assembly stats, typing results (MLST, cgMLST), AMR genes, species ID. TSV/JSON reports. Integration with CDC's internal surveillance databases.
profile.users_scale: Used by CDC for routine national surveillance. Adopted by state public health labs via AMD program. Open-source for international adoption.
profile.access_model: Open-source on GitHub (CDCgov/phoenix). Free to download, use, and modify. No registration. MIT-like public domain license. CDC provides documentation and training.

all.d: DARPA program to develop platform that can halt any viral pandemic within 60 days using nucleic acid-encoded medical countermeasures

profile.overview: DARPA P3 (Pandemic Prevention Platform) is a Department of Defense research program aimed at developing an integrated platform to halt viral pandemic outbreaks within 60 days of pathogen identification. Launched in 2017, it focuses on rapid discovery, characterization, production, testing, and delivery of nucleic acid-encoded medical countermeasures.
profile.functional_scope: Rapid antibody discovery from convalescent blood samples (B-cell sorting, single-cell sequencing), antibody characterization and optimization using computational biology, rapid manufacturing of DNA/RNA-encoded antibodies, pre-clinical and clinical testing acceleration, and field-deployable delivery systems. The program demonstrated the ability to discover, manufacture, and begin testing antibody treatments in under 60 days.
profile.tech_stack: Single-cell B-cell sequencing (10x Genomics), computational antibody engineering (Rosetta, machine learning), DNA/mRNA expression platforms, mammalian cell manufacturing (CHO cells), and advanced clinical trial infrastructure. Multiple contractor teams: AbCellera, Duke DHVI, Vanderbilt VUMC, MedImmune/AstraZeneca.
profile.operator: Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA), U.S. Department of Defense. Biological Technologies Office.
profile.data_model: Antibody sequence libraries, structural data (cryo-EM, X-ray), binding affinity measurements, neutralization assay results, pre-clinical and clinical trial data. Classified and unclassified data tracks.
profile.users_scale: Multiple contractor teams at Duke, Vanderbilt, AbCellera, MedImmune. Results directly applied to COVID-19 response (AbCellera's bamlanivimab was first FDA-authorized monoclonal antibody).
profile.access_model: Government-funded research program. Results published in peer-reviewed journals. Some technologies transitioned to commercial partners (AbCellera -> Eli Lilly).

all.d: European Commission Health Emergency Preparedness and Response Authority - EU crisis response for cross-border biological threats and MCM procurement

profile.overview: The Health Emergency Preparedness and Response Authority (HERA) is the European Commission body established in 2021 to prevent, detect, and rapidly respond to health emergencies. The EU's equivalent to BARDA.
profile.functional_scope: EU-wide health emergency preparedness: threat intelligence and assessment, medical countermeasure development and procurement (vaccines, therapeutics, diagnostics, PPE), stockpiling, manufacturing surge capacity, R&D coordination, and international cooperation.
profile.tech_stack: Regulatory and procurement frameworks. Joint procurement agreements. Emergency framework contracts. Health threat intelligence platform. Integration with ECDC surveillance data.
profile.operator: European Commission DG SANTE, Brussels, Belgium. Works with ECDC, EMA, member states, and industry. Funded by EU budget (multi-billion EUR).
profile.data_model: Health threat assessments, MCM stockpile inventories, procurement contracts, R&D pipeline data. Standardized threat reporting formats aligned with ECDC protocols.
profile.users_scale: Serves all 27 EU member states plus associated countries (~450M population). Coordinates with WHO, US BARDA, international agencies. Manages EU Strategic Reserve.
profile.access_model: Intergovernmental EU body. Public information at EC health portal. Procurement via official EU channels. Intelligence products for EU member state health ministries.

all.d: WHO's Global Antimicrobial Resistance and Use Surveillance System, collecting standardized AMR data from national surveillance systems in 120+ countries

profile.overview: GLASS (Global Antimicrobial Resistance and Use Surveillance System) is WHO's comprehensive global surveillance initiative for antimicrobial resistance. Launched in October 2015, it provides the first worldwide standardized approach to collecting, analyzing, and sharing AMR and antimicrobial consumption data.
profile.functional_scope: Multiple surveillance modules: GLASS-AMR (routine AMR data from clinical samples), GLASS-AMC (antimicrobial consumption monitoring), GLASS-EAR (emerging resistance reporting), GLASS-FUNGI (invasive fungal infections), EGASP (gonococcal resistance surveillance), One Health (ESBL-E. coli Tricycle project), PPS-AMU (hospital antibiotic use surveys), and AMR Burden estimation studies.
profile.tech_stack: WHO GLASS platform with data submission portals, WHONET integration for data collection, R/Shiny interactive dashboards for visualization, custom analytical frameworks. Standardized data submission formats.
profile.operator: World Health Organization (WHO), Department of Surveillance, Prevention and Control of AMR. Supported by WHO AMR Surveillance Collaborating Centres Network.
profile.data_model: Standardized AMR data: pathogen-antibiotic combinations, resistance rates by specimen type. Epidemiological metadata (country, year, setting). AMC data in DDD (Defined Daily Doses) per 1,000 inhabitants.
profile.users_scale: 127 countries enrolled. Published annual reports since 2017 informing global AMR policy. Data from thousands of laboratories worldwide.
profile.access_model: Free enrollment for WHO Member States. Data submission through GLASS portal. Public dashboards and annual reports freely available. Country-level data published with consent.

all.d: WDL workflows for genomic characterization and surveillance (TheiaCoV, TheiaProk, TheiaEuk)

profile.overview: Theiagen Public Health Bioinformatics (PHB) is a collection of WDL (Workflow Description Language) workflows developed by Theiagen Genomics for genomic characterization and surveillance of pathogens in public health laboratories.
profile.functional_scope: WDL workflows for: SARS-CoV-2, TB, influenza, RSV, and bacterial pathogen analysis. Includes assembly, mapping, lineage assignment (Pangolin, Nextclade), AMR detection, quality control, and data submission pipelines. Runs on Terra.bio cloud platform.
profile.tech_stack: WDL (Workflow Description Language) workflows. Cromwell execution engine. Terra.bio cloud platform. Docker containers. Integrates Nextclade, Pangolin, AMRFinderPlus, VADR. Open-source on GitHub.
profile.operator: Theiagen Genomics, Highlands Ranch, CO, USA. Social enterprise supporting public health labs. Partnerships with CDC, state health departments, and international agencies.
profile.data_model: FASTQ/FASTA input. Structured output: lineage assignments, QC metrics, AMR profiles, assembly statistics. Terra.bio data tables. Integrated data submission to NCBI/GISAID.
profile.users_scale: Used by 50+ US state/territorial public health labs and international partners. Key tool in CDC AMD program. Thousands of samples processed weekly during COVID-19.
profile.access_model: Open-source on GitHub (theiagen/public_health_bioinformatics). Free to use. Runs on Terra.bio (free tier available for public health). Professional support available from Theiagen.

all.d: CDC national arboviral surveillance system tracking human, animal, and mosquito data

profile.overview: ArboNET is CDC's national arboviral surveillance system tracking human disease cases and animal/mosquito infections for West Nile virus, Zika, dengue, chikungunya, EEE, and other arboviruses in the United States.
profile.functional_scope: Case-based surveillance for arboviral diseases: human neuroinvasive and non-neuroinvasive cases, veterinary cases, dead bird surveillance, mosquito surveillance. Collects clinical, epidemiological, and laboratory data. Annual and provisional reporting.
profile.tech_stack: Web-based case reporting through state/territorial health departments. Integrated with CDC NNDSS for notifiable conditions. CDC WONDER for data access. Standard CDC surveillance architecture.
profile.operator: CDC Division of Vector-Borne Diseases (DVBD), Fort Collins, CO, USA. All state, territorial, and local health departments report.
profile.data_model: Case-level data: demographics, symptoms, lab results (IgM, PRNT), exposure history, travel. Animal/mosquito surveillance data with collection site, date, species. Annual summary statistics.
profile.users_scale: Covers all US states and territories. Tracks tens of thousands of arboviral cases annually. Data used by state/local health departments, vector control programs, and researchers.
profile.access_model: Aggregate data publicly available via CDC website and CDC WONDER. Case-level data restricted to authorized personnel. Annual summaries freely published.

wk: Primarily US-focused network with limited international participation
all.d: CDC's national molecular subtyping network using whole-genome sequencing to detect and investigate foodborne disease outbreaks across all US states

profile.overview: PulseNet is CDC's national laboratory network for detecting foodborne, waterborne, and One Health-related disease outbreaks through molecular surveillance. Established in 1996, it uses DNA fingerprinting (now primarily whole genome sequencing) to connect illness cases and identify outbreaks across the United States and internationally.
profile.functional_scope: Whole genome sequencing (WGS) of foodborne pathogens, SNP-based clustering for outbreak detection, allele-based typing (cgMLST), real-time comparison of isolate fingerprints across the network, outbreak investigation support, and antimicrobial resistance monitoring. PulseNet International coordinates similar activities globally across 86 countries.
profile.tech_stack: WGS-based surveillance using Illumina sequencing. Bioinformatics pipeline: NCBI Pathogen Detection for clustering, BioNumerics for legacy data. Cloud-based data sharing via NCBI. PFGE (legacy) and WGS databases.
profile.operator: U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), with 83 participating state and local public health laboratories. PulseNet International extends to 86 countries.
profile.data_model: Isolate data: organism, WGS/PFGE fingerprint, patient demographics (anonymized), food source, date, location. Clusters defined by SNP distance thresholds. Integrated with NCBI Pathogen Detection trees.
profile.users_scale: Detects ~1,750+ clusters annually in the U.S. Credited with preventing an estimated 270,000+ illnesses per year. All 50 U.S. states plus territories participate.
profile.access_model: Network access for participating public health laboratories. Cluster data shared with epidemiologists via secure systems. Summary data publicly available through CDC.

all.d: CDC's Data Modernization Initiative for unified public health data infrastructure

profile.overview: CDC's Data Modernization Initiative (DMI) aims to transform public health data systems into a unified, cloud-based, real-time infrastructure. The 'One CDC' data platform concept unifies siloed surveillance systems.
profile.functional_scope: Modernizing US public health data infrastructure: cloud migration, API-based data exchange, FHIR interoperability, automated case reporting, real-time dashboards, AI/ML analytics, and electronic case reporting (eCR). Replacing legacy systems.
profile.tech_stack: Cloud-first architecture (Azure/AWS). FHIR R4 standard, HL7v2 messaging, API gateways. ReportStream for data routing. DCIPHER data platform. Snowflake data warehouse. Tableau dashboards.
profile.operator: CDC Office of Public Health Data, Surveillance, and Technology (OPHDST), Atlanta, GA. $200M+ annual investment authorized by Congress.
profile.data_model: Unified data model spanning multiple surveillance domains. FHIR-based electronic case reports. Standardized message formats. Cloud data lakes with analytics layers.
profile.users_scale: Serves all US state, tribal, local, and territorial health departments. Impacts 330M population. Thousands of public health professionals and data systems connected.
profile.access_model: Government infrastructure program. Systems accessible to authorized public health officials. Public APIs for standardized data exchange. Open-source components (ReportStream).

all.d: PREventing EMerging Pathogenic Threats - aims to predict and prevent zoonotic spillover events before they reach human populations

profile.overview: DARPA PREventing EMerging Pathogenic Threats (PREEMPT) program aims to prevent viral spillover from animal reservoirs to human populations by developing technologies that can detect and suppress viruses in animal hosts before they make the jump.
profile.functional_scope: Developing technologies to: predict zoonotic spillover events, identify circulating viruses in animal reservoirs, create broad-spectrum antivirals for animal populations, and deploy prophylactic interventions in wildlife to prevent human infection.
profile.tech_stack: Metagenomics for virus discovery, computational spillover risk models, broad-spectrum antiviral development, self-spreading vaccine concepts, environmental DNA sampling.
profile.operator: DARPA Biological Technologies Office (BTO), Arlington, VA, USA. Multiple performer teams from universities and biotech companies.
profile.data_model: Viral surveillance data from animal reservoirs, spillover risk models, antiviral efficacy data, environmental sampling results. Integration with USAID PREDICT virus databases.
profile.users_scale: Research program with multiple performer teams. Results feed into DoD biodefense planning and broader pandemic preparedness. Published in peer-reviewed journals.
profile.access_model: DARPA research program with controlled access. Research results published in open literature. Technology transitions to DoD and civilian agencies upon completion.

all.d: Nanopore sequencing protocols and bioinformatics pipelines for outbreak genomics

profile.overview: The ARTIC Network provides open-source nanopore sequencing protocols and bioinformatics pipelines for real-time genomic epidemiology of outbreak pathogens. Founded in 2017 by Nick Loman (Birmingham), Andrew Rambaut (Edinburgh), and others.
profile.functional_scope: Tiling amplicon schemes and sequencing protocols for rapid pathogen genome sequencing using Oxford Nanopore Technology. Covers SARS-CoV-2, Ebola, Zika, Mpox, and other outbreak pathogens. Bioinformatics pipeline (fieldbioinformatics) for consensus genome generation.
profile.tech_stack: Oxford Nanopore MinION/GridION sequencing. Primal Scheme for primer design. Medaka/Nanopolish for variant calling. Minimap2 for mapping. Python pipeline (fieldbioinformatics). GitHub-hosted protocols.
profile.operator: Academic consortium: University of Birmingham (Nick Loman), University of Edinburgh (Andrew Rambaut), Fred Hutch, and others. Funded by Wellcome Trust, ARTIC consortium.
profile.data_model: Amplicon scheme BED files, nanopore FAST5/FASTQ reads, consensus FASTA sequences with quality metrics. Integration with GISAID/INSDC for data sharing.
profile.users_scale: Used globally during COVID-19 (millions of SARS-CoV-2 genomes generated using ARTIC protocols). Deployed in 100+ countries. Standard protocol for WHO and national surveillance programs.
profile.access_model: Fully open-source. Protocols at artic.network and GitHub. Free primer schemes. Community-supported. No licensing restrictions.

all.d: Open-source platform for managing and analyzing large-scale biomedical data

profile.overview: Arvados is an open-source platform for managing, processing, and sharing large-scale biomedical data, developed by Curii Corporation (formerly Curoverse). Used for genomic data management and reproducible bioinformatics workflows.
profile.functional_scope: Cloud-native data management and workflow execution for genomics. Supports CWL (Common Workflow Language) pipelines. Provides data provenance tracking, access control, and reproducibility. Scales from small datasets to population-level genomics.
profile.tech_stack: Go/Ruby/Python backend. CWL workflow engine (Crunch2). Keep distributed storage system. Kubernetes/Docker orchestration. REST API. Web workbench UI. AWS/GCP/Azure/on-premises deployment.
profile.operator: Curii Corporation, Somerville, MA, USA. Open-source community with contributions from academic and commercial users.
profile.data_model: Content-addressed storage (Keep) with collections of files. CWL workflow definitions. Structured metadata and provenance chains. Access control lists for data sharing.
profile.users_scale: Used by Veritas Genetics, Harvard Personal Genome Project, clinical genomics labs, and research institutions. Processes petabyte-scale datasets.
profile.access_model: Open-source under Apache 2.0 license. Self-hosted or Curii-managed cloud. Community edition free. Enterprise support available. Public playground for testing.

all.d: Interactive web-based visualization tool for phylogenetic trees and genomic epidemiology data, part of the Nextstrain ecosystem

profile.overview: Auspice is the open-source interactive visualization application developed by the Nextstrain team for exploring phylogenomic datasets. It renders phylogenetic trees with temporal, geographic, and mutation annotations, powering all Nextstrain.org visualizations and available as a standalone tool via auspice.us.
profile.functional_scope: Interactive phylogenetic tree visualization with zoom, filter, and animation. Geographic mapping of pathogen spread over time. Mutation frequency tracking. Diversity panels showing entropy and mutation distribution. Support for narratives (guided walkthroughs). Drag-and-drop local file viewing (no data uploaded to server).
profile.tech_stack: React.js frontend, D3.js for tree rendering and charts, Leaflet.js for geographic maps, deck.gl for transmission lines. WebAssembly for performance-critical operations. Installable via npm or usable via auspice.us. Open-source under AGPL v3.
profile.operator: Nextstrain project (Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center & University of Basel). Funded by NIH, HHMI, Wellcome Trust.
profile.data_model: Auspice JSON format: tree topology with node annotations (mutations, dates, locations, clade assignments, confidence intervals). Sidecar files for additional metadata, frequencies, and tip counts.
profile.users_scale: Powers all Nextstrain.org builds viewed by millions. auspice.us used by thousands of researchers for private dataset exploration.
profile.access_model: Free, open-source. auspice.us requires no login, all processing client-side (privacy-preserving). NPM package for self-hosting.

all.d: Biological Threat Reduction Program - strengthens partner nation biosurveillance and biosecurity across 30+ countries to prevent BW proliferation

profile.overview: The Biological Threat Reduction Program (BTRP) is a key component of the Defense Threat Reduction Agency's (DTRA) Cooperative Threat Reduction program, working with partner nations to counter biological threats through biosafety, biosecurity, and disease surveillance capacity building.
profile.functional_scope: Strengthens partner nation biosurveillance capabilities: upgrades laboratories to BSL-2/3 standards, trains personnel, establishes electronic disease surveillance, provides diagnostic equipment, and builds sustainable public health infrastructure in former Soviet states and other regions.
profile.tech_stack: Laboratory equipment (biosafety cabinets, PCR, sequencing), electronic surveillance systems, training curricula, biosafety/biosecurity assessment frameworks. Integration with national public health infrastructures.
profile.operator: Defense Threat Reduction Agency (DTRA), US Department of Defense. Cooperative agreements with 30+ partner nations. Nunn-Lugar legislation authority.
profile.data_model: Cooperative biological engagement program data: lab assessments, capacity metrics, disease surveillance reports, training records. Integration with WHO IHR core capacity indicators.
profile.users_scale: Active in 30+ countries across Central Asia, Southeast Asia, Africa, and the Middle East. Has upgraded 50+ laboratories. Trained thousands of public health professionals.
profile.access_model: Government-to-government cooperative program. Not publicly accessible. Reports to Congress annually. Some program data shared through unclassified channels.

all.d: Computational tool for profiling microbial communities from metagenomic shotgun sequencing
wk: Command-line interface only; no web GUI limits accessibility for non-technical users

profile.overview: MetaPhlAn 4 (Metagenomic Phylogenetic Analysis) is a computational tool for profiling microbial communities from metagenomic data, developed by the Huttenhower Lab at Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. One of the most cited metagenomics tools.
profile.functional_scope: Species-level taxonomic profiling of microbiomes from metagenomic shotgun sequencing. Maps reads to unique clade-specific marker genes. Provides relative abundance estimates. Also supports strain-level profiling via StrainPhlAn.
profile.tech_stack: Python/Cython with Bowtie2 for read mapping. SGB (Species-Level Genome Bins) database. Integrated with bioBakery suite (HUMAnN, StrainPhlAn). Available via Bioconda, Docker, Galaxy.
profile.operator: Huttenhower Lab, Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, Boston, MA, USA. Funded by NIH/NHGRI, Broad Institute.
profile.data_model: Input: FASTQ metagenomic reads. Database: species-level genome bins (SGBs) with marker genes from 200,000+ reference genomes. Output: species abundance profiles (TSV), phylogenetic placement.
profile.users_scale: 20,000+ citations. Standard tool in Human Microbiome Project and MetaHIT. Used by virtually every microbiome research group. Integrated into thousands of bioinformatics pipelines.
profile.access_model: Free and open-source (MIT license). Available on GitHub (biobakery/MetaPhlAn), Bioconda, Docker. Database freely downloadable. No restrictions.

all.d: USAID-funded program that discovered 1,200+ novel viruses across 35 countries by sampling 160,000+ animals - foundational to pandemic preparedness

profile.overview: USAID PREDICT was a pioneering global zoonotic virus discovery program (2009-2020) that identified 1,200+ novel viruses across 35 countries. Led by UC Davis One Health Institute with EcoHealth Alliance, Metabiota, WCS, and Smithsonian.
profile.functional_scope: Zoonotic virus surveillance in wildlife and high-risk human populations. Collected 170,000+ biological samples from bats, primates, and other wildlife. Virus discovery using metagenomic sequencing. Risk characterization and spillover modeling.
profile.tech_stack: PCR-based and metagenomic virus detection. Field sampling protocols for wildlife. Bioinformatics for novel virus characterization. Country-level laboratory capacity building. Training curricula.
profile.operator: USAID-funded ($200M over 10 years). Led by UC Davis One Health Institute. Partners: EcoHealth Alliance, Metabiota (now Ginkgo), Wildlife Conservation Society, Smithsonian Institution.
profile.data_model: Viral sequence data, sample metadata (species, location, date), host ecology data. Published in NCBI GenBank. PREDICT Virus Host Database. Country reports.
profile.users_scale: Operated in 35 countries. Trained 6,500+ people. Strengthened 60+ labs. Discovered 1,200+ novel viruses including new coronaviruses. Data available post-program.
profile.access_model: Program ended 2020. Data and publications freely available. Viral sequences in GenBank. Reports on USAID website. Legacy data continues to inform pandemic preparedness.

all.d: Co-visualization of phylogenetic trees with genome data

profile.overview: Treenome Browser is an extension of Taxonium that enables co-visualization of phylogenetic trees with genome-level data, allowing researchers to explore mutations and evolutionary patterns alongside tree topology in the browser.
profile.functional_scope: Co-visualization of phylogenetic trees with genomic data: mutations mapped onto tree branches, genome browser integration, nucleotide-level exploration of variants alongside evolutionary context. Enables rapid identification of convergent evolution and lineage-defining mutations.
profile.tech_stack: Extension of Taxonium (WebGL, React). Genome browser component integrated with tree viewer. Handles trees with millions of tips. Open-source on GitHub (theosanderson/taxonium).
profile.operator: Theo Sanderson, Francis Crick Institute / Wellcome Sanger Institute, London, UK. Community open-source project.
profile.data_model: Phylogenetic trees (Newick/protobuf) linked with genome annotations. Mutation data mapped to tree nodes. Supports VCF-style variant data alongside tree topology.
profile.users_scale: Used by SARS-CoV-2 genomics community. Integrated with Taxonium.org public instance. Cited in genomic epidemiology publications.
profile.access_model: Free and open-source. Available at taxonium.org/treenome. Self-hostable. No registration required.

all.d: UK Defence Science and Technology Laboratory at Porton Down - premier UK biodefense research facility providing CBW defense capabilities to British military

profile.overview: The Defence Science and Technology Laboratory (Dstl) at Porton Down is the UK Ministry of Defence's primary science and technology organization for chemical and biological defense research. Operational since 1916.
profile.functional_scope: CBRN defense R&D: biological agent detection and identification, medical countermeasure development, decontamination technology, protective equipment, threat assessment, forensic analysis. Supports military operations and national security.
profile.tech_stack: BSL-4 containment facilities. Advanced analytical chemistry, genomic sequencing, proteomics, immunology. Environmental sampling and detection systems. Classified R&D programs.
profile.operator: UK Ministry of Defence, Porton Down, Salisbury, Wiltshire, UK. Executive agency of MoD. ~3,500 staff including world-leading CBRN scientists.
profile.data_model: Classified research data on biological agents, countermeasures, and threat characterization. Some publications in open literature. Intelligence products for UK government.
profile.users_scale: Serves UK Armed Forces, Home Office, intelligence agencies, and NATO allies. Supported Novichok investigations (Salisbury 2018). Key partner in Five Eyes CBRN network.
profile.access_model: Government classified facility. Research outputs mostly restricted. Select publications in peer-reviewed journals. External collaboration through contracted research programs.

all.d: Bayesian evolutionary analysis for molecular clock and phylodynamic inference

profile.overview: BEAST 2 (Bayesian Evolutionary Analysis Sampling Trees) is an open-source software platform for Bayesian phylogenetics, molecular clock analysis, and phylodynamics. Successor to BEAST 1, developed by an international team led by Alexei Drummond and Remco Bouckaert.
profile.functional_scope: Bayesian inference of molecular phylogenies with evolutionary time estimates. Molecular clock models (strict, relaxed), coalescent demographic models, birth-death models, phylogeography, and structured population models. Extensible via packages.
profile.tech_stack: Java application with XML configuration. MCMC sampling algorithms. BEAUti GUI for model setup. Tracer for convergence diagnostics. FigTree/DensiTree for visualization. 300+ community packages. Open-source on GitHub.
profile.operator: International developer community. Led by University of Auckland (Drummond) and ETH Zürich (Stadler). Funded by Marsden Fund, ERC, and community contributions.
profile.data_model: Input: aligned sequences (NEXUS/FASTA) with metadata (dates, locations). XML model specification. Output: posterior tree distributions (NEXUS), parameter traces (log files). MCC summary trees.
profile.users_scale: 30,000+ citations. Standard tool for molecular epidemiology and viral phylodynamics. Used by every major genomic surveillance group including Nextstrain, COG-UK, CDC.
profile.access_model: Fully open-source (LGPL 2.1). Available at beast2.org. Cross-platform (Java). Free to use. Extensive tutorials and documentation.

all.n: Google Earth Engine / Health Map AI
all.d: Google Earth Engine combined with AI models for environmental and epidemiological surveillance - used by WHO/FAO for vector-borne disease risk mapping and climate-health forecasting

profile.overview: Google Earth Engine (GEE) is a cloud-based geospatial analysis platform combined with AI/ML models for environmental and disease ecology applications. Provides petabyte-scale satellite imagery analysis for health-related geospatial intelligence.
profile.functional_scope: Environmental determinants of disease: land use change, deforestation tracking, climate variables, water body detection, urbanization monitoring. Disease ecology modeling, mosquito habitat mapping, pollution monitoring, and climate-health associations.
profile.tech_stack: Google Cloud infrastructure. JavaScript/Python API. Petabytes of satellite imagery (Landsat, Sentinel, MODIS). TensorFlow integration for ML models. Earth Engine Code Editor.
profile.operator: Google LLC, Mountain View, CA, USA. Free for research and nonprofit use. Earth Engine for Health partnerships with WHO and academic institutions.
profile.data_model: Raster satellite imagery collections with temporal metadata. Vector datasets (boundaries, infrastructure). User-uploaded assets. Computed raster/vector outputs. Time series exports.
profile.users_scale: Millions of registered users. Used by WHO, FAO, World Bank, NASA, and thousands of research institutions. 100,000+ published studies. Key tool for environmental health research.
profile.access_model: Free for research, education, and nonprofit use. Commercial licensing via Google Cloud. Browser-based Code Editor. Python/JavaScript API. Google account required.

all.d: Joint CBRN Defence Centre of Excellence in Brno, Czech Republic - NATO accredited hub for CBRN defence doctrine, training, and capability development

profile.overview: The NATO Joint CBRN Defence Centre of Excellence is a multinational military organization in Brno, Czech Republic, accredited by NATO to provide education, training, analysis, and lessons learned in the CBRN defense domain.
profile.functional_scope: CBRN defense knowledge hub: develops doctrine, conducts exercises, provides training courses, performs threat analysis, supports capability development, and maintains interoperability standards across NATO allies. Manages CBRN reachback and advisory services.
profile.tech_stack: CBRN simulation and modeling tools. Hazard prediction software (HPAC, JEM). Training management systems. NATO standards and STANAG implementation.
profile.operator: NATO-accredited Centre of Excellence, Brno, Czech Republic. Sponsored by Czech Republic. Contributing nations include 21+ NATO members and partners.
profile.data_model: CBRN threat characterization data, exercise scenarios, lessons learned databases, capability assessment frameworks. NATO-standardized reporting formats (STANAGs).
profile.users_scale: Serves all 32 NATO member states and partner nations. Conducts 20+ courses annually. Supports NATO exercises and real-world operations. 200+ staff from contributing nations.
profile.access_model: NATO military and government access. Training courses available to NATO members and partners. Publications and reports through NATO channels. Some unclassified products public.

all.d: Public Health Alliance for Genomic Epidemiology developing data standards and pipelines

profile.overview: PHA4GE (Public Health Alliance for Genomic Epidemiology) is a global coalition developing open data standards, bioinformatics tools, and best practices to enable interoperable genomic epidemiology for public health. Founded in 2019.
profile.functional_scope: Develops standardized metadata templates, contextual data specifications, bioinformatics tool benchmarks, and quality assurance protocols for pathogen genomic surveillance. Focus on SARS-CoV-2, AMR, food safety, and other public health priorities.
profile.tech_stack: GitHub-hosted specifications and tools. YAML/JSON metadata schemas. Validation tools (DataHarmonizer). Bioinformatics pipeline specifications. Integration with INSDC, GISAID standards.
profile.operator: Community-driven alliance with representation from CDC, ECDC, WHO, Wellcome Sanger Institute, academic institutions, and public health labs globally.
profile.data_model: Standardized metadata specifications for pathogen sequences (organism-specific templates). Contextual data dictionaries. Quality control metrics. Ontology-based controlled vocabularies.
profile.users_scale: Members from 40+ countries. Standards adopted by major surveillance programs (COG-UK, PulseNet, national reference labs). Used to harmonize SARS-CoV-2 data globally.
profile.access_model: Fully open and community-driven. All specifications on GitHub (pha4ge). Free to use and contribute. No membership fees. Working groups open to participation.

st: Horizontally scalable architecture supporting growing data volumes
all.d: Broad Institute's cloud-native bioinformatics platform for scalable genomic analysis, enabling collaborative research on pathogen genomics and population health data

profile.overview: Terra is a secure, scalable, cloud-based platform for biomedical data analysis, developed by the Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard in collaboration with Microsoft and Verily (Alphabet). It is the world's most trusted platform for large-scale genomic and clinical data analysis.
profile.functional_scope: Cloud-based data analysis with support for WDL (Workflow Description Language) and Cromwell execution engine. Jupyter notebooks, RStudio, and Galaxy integration. Secure data enclaves for sensitive datasets. Access to major genomic datasets (TCGA, gnomAD, 1000 Genomes, All of Us). Federated analysis framework for multi-institutional collaboration without data movement.
profile.tech_stack: Google Cloud Platform (primary) and Microsoft Azure. WDL/Cromwell workflow engine. Jupyter/RStudio interactive analysis. React.js frontend. Python/Scala backend. SAM (Security and Access Management) for authorization. Open-source components on GitHub.
profile.operator: Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard, in partnership with Microsoft and Verily (Alphabet/Google). Funded by NIH, NCI, and NHGRI.
profile.data_model: Workspace-based model with data tables, reference data, workflows, and analysis outputs. Supports any file format. Terra Data Repository for managed datasets with FAIR principles.
profile.users_scale: 50,000+ registered users. Hosts petabytes of biomedical data. Used by NIH All of Us, TCGA, AnVIL, and BioData Catalyst programs.
profile.access_model: Free to register. Pay-per-use cloud computing (Google Cloud billing). Free tier for small analyses. Institutional billing available. Secure workspaces for controlled-access data.

all.d: UVA Biocomplexity Institute / DTRA-funded agent-based pandemic simulation engine for large-scale what-if epidemic scenario modeling

profile.overview: EpiHiper is a high-performance agent-based epidemic simulation platform developed by the University of Virginia Biocomplexity Institute, funded primarily by DTRA and NIH. Scales to simulate entire national populations.
profile.functional_scope: Agent-based pandemic simulation at national/global scale. Models disease transmission, intervention strategies (vaccination, social distancing, school closures), hospital capacity, supply chain impacts. Supports COVID-19, influenza, and other pathogens.
profile.tech_stack: C++/MPI high-performance computing. Simulates 330M+ agents (entire US population). Runs on supercomputers (up to 100,000+ cores). Contact network generation. Scenario analysis engine.
profile.operator: Biocomplexity Institute, University of Virginia, Charlottesville, VA. Led by Prof. Madhav Marathe. Funded by DTRA, NIH/NIGMS, NSF.
profile.data_model: Synthetic population data with demographics, households, workplaces, schools. Contact networks. Disease natural history parameters. Intervention scenarios. Time-series output of infections, hospitalizations, deaths.
profile.users_scale: Used by CDC, FEMA, DoD, state governments for pandemic planning. Provided COVID-19 projections to White House. Key tool in US pandemic modeling consortium.
profile.access_model: Government research contracts. Custom analyses for federal/state agencies. Some components open-source. Academic publications freely available.

all.d: Interactive visualization of minimum spanning trees for genomic epidemiology

profile.overview: GrapeTree is an interactive minimum spanning tree (MST) visualization tool designed for large-scale genomic epidemiology datasets, developed by the EnteroBase team. It efficiently handles trees with hundreds of thousands of nodes and provides integrated metadata overlay.
profile.functional_scope: Visualization of minimum spanning trees for MLST/cgMLST profiles, interactive node coloring by metadata, dynamic filtering and searching, tree layout optimization for large datasets (MSTree V2 algorithm for 100,000+ nodes), export in SVG/PNG, and integration with EnteroBase.
profile.tech_stack: JavaScript/D3.js web application, MSTree V2 algorithm (C++ with Python bindings for efficient MST computation), Electron desktop app. Open-source on GitHub.
profile.operator: EnteroBase team, University of Warwick. Developed by Zhemin Zhou and Mark Achtman.
profile.data_model: Input: allelic profiles (MLST/cgMLST) and metadata tables. Output: interactive MST visualization with node annotations.
profile.users_scale: Standard visualization tool for EnteroBase (1.6M+ strains). Widely used in the microbial genomics community.
profile.access_model: Free, open-source. Available as web app, desktop app (Electron), and embeddable component.

all.d: HHS/ASPR program providing $5.6B+ in funding to incentivize private-sector MCM development against CBRN threats

profile.overview: Project BioShield (established 2004) provides $5.6B+ in funding for development and procurement of medical countermeasures against CBRN and pandemic threats. Managed by BARDA within HHS/ASPR.
profile.functional_scope: Incentivizes development and procurement of vaccines, therapeutics, diagnostics, and other MCMs against CBRN threats and pandemic pathogens. Maintains Strategic National Stockpile. Manages advanced development contracts through BARDA.
profile.tech_stack: Regulatory and procurement frameworks. Advanced development contracts (OTA, BAA). Clinical trial infrastructure. Manufacturing scale-up facilities (CIADM network).
profile.operator: Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority (BARDA), Administration for Strategic Preparedness and Response (ASPR), US HHS. Washington, DC.
profile.data_model: MCM pipeline data: development stages, procurement contracts, stockpile inventories, threat assessments. Integration with FDA regulatory data. PHEMCE requirements.
profile.users_scale: Protects US population (330M) through Strategic National Stockpile. Has funded development of COVID-19 vaccines, anthrax MCMs, smallpox treatments, nerve agent antidotes.
profile.access_model: Government procurement program. Contracts awarded through competitive process. Public information at medicalcountermeasures.gov. Industry engagement through BARDA TechWatch.

all.d: Africa Genome Archiving for Response and Insight - continent-wide pathogen genomic data sharing platform launched Nov 2025 with biosecurity integration

profile.overview: AGARI (Africa Genome Amplification Resource for Infectious Diseases) is the Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention's continental genomic surveillance initiative, aimed at building genomic sequencing capacity across African nations for infectious disease detection and monitoring.
profile.functional_scope: Continental coordination of pathogen genomic sequencing, laboratory capacity building, bioinformatics training, data sharing across African Union member states, variant tracking and early warning, integration with Africa CDC's continental surveillance systems.
profile.tech_stack: Oxford Nanopore and Illumina sequencing platforms deployed across member state laboratories. Bioinformatics training using Galaxy, Nextstrain, and Nextclade. Data shared through GISAID and regional databases.
profile.operator: Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention (Africa CDC), African Union, Addis Ababa, Ethiopia.
profile.data_model: Pathogen genomic sequences with epidemiological metadata from African Union member states.
profile.users_scale: Covers 55 African Union member states. Building sequencing capacity in laboratories across the continent.
profile.access_model: Access through Africa CDC and member state public health agencies. Data sharing coordinated at continental level.

all.d: Defence Science and Technology Group CBRN research program - Australia's military CBRN defense research including biological detection and decontamination

profile.overview: Australia's Defence Science and Technology Group (DSTG) CBRN Defence research program conducts research to protect Australian Defence Force personnel from chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear threats.
profile.functional_scope: CBRN defense research: biological agent detection, medical countermeasure assessment, protective equipment testing, decontamination technologies, threat characterization, and environmental monitoring. Supports ADF operational capability.
profile.tech_stack: PC2/PC3 biological research facilities. Field-deployable detection systems. Analytical chemistry and molecular biology platforms. Modeling and simulation tools.
profile.operator: Defence Science and Technology Group (DSTG), Australian Department of Defence, Edinburgh, South Australia. Part of the Five Eyes S&T partnership.
profile.data_model: Classified defense research data. Published scientific papers in open literature. Technical reports for ADF and Five Eyes partners.
profile.users_scale: Serves Australian Defence Force and allied forces through Five Eyes and ABCANZ partnerships. ~400 CBRN-focused researchers. Supports NATO standards adoption.
profile.access_model: Government/military research organization. Most work classified. Some publications in peer-reviewed journals. Collaboration through Five Eyes defense agreements.

all.d: Centers of Excellence for Influenza Research and Response / Influenza Data Processing Coordination Center

profile.overview: The Centers of Excellence for Influenza Research and Response (CEIRR), succeeding CEIRS, is an NIAID-funded network of multidisciplinary research centers focused on influenza and other respiratory pathogens, including the Influenza Data Processing and Coordination Center (iDPCC).
profile.functional_scope: Influenza and respiratory virus research: surveillance, virus characterization, vaccine development, antiviral research, animal models, computational analysis. iDPCC provides centralized data management and bioinformatics support.
profile.tech_stack: Genomic sequencing (Illumina, Nanopore), antigenic characterization, structural biology, computational modeling. iDPCC data platform with analysis tools. FASTA/GenBank submissions.
profile.operator: NIAID/NIH-funded network. Multiple US academic centers (St. Jude, Emory, Icahn Mt Sinai, Scripps, U. Rochester). iDPCC at Northrop Grumman/academic partners.
profile.data_model: Influenza viral sequences, antigenic data, surveillance metadata, animal model results. Centralized in iDPCC. Submissions to GISAID and NCBI GenBank.
profile.users_scale: Five major research centers plus associates. Generates thousands of viral sequences annually. Data shared globally through GISAID. Informs WHO vaccine strain selection.
profile.access_model: NIAID-funded research program. Data shared publicly through GISAID/GenBank. Publications in open literature. iDPCC provides tools and analysis to network members.

all.d: Real-time tracking and visualization of SARS-CoV-2 variants, mutations, and lineage distributions globally with clear frequency plots and mutation tables

profile.overview: CoVariants is an open-source web resource providing an overview of SARS-CoV-2 variants and mutations of interest. Created by Dr. Emma Hodcroft (University of Basel and Swiss Institute of Bioinformatics), it tracks variant prevalence globally using data from GISAID, with Nextstrain clade nomenclature.
profile.functional_scope: Variant information pages with mutation details and literature links, per-country variant frequency distributions over time, per-variant geographic spread visualization, shared mutation analysis across variants, 3D protein structure viewer showing mutation locations, and links to focused Nextstrain builds for each variant.
profile.tech_stack: React.js/Gatsby static site, D3.js for charts and visualizations, Python scripts for data processing from GISAID, 3D protein visualization using Mol* viewer. Hosted on GitHub Pages. Open-source on GitHub.
profile.operator: Dr. Emma Hodcroft, University of Basel / Swiss Institute of Bioinformatics. Independent research project.
profile.data_model: Aggregated variant frequency data by country and time from GISAID sequences. Mutation definitions and annotations per Nextstrain clade. Cross-referenced with WHO variant designations.
profile.users_scale: Hundreds of thousands of monthly visitors. Widely cited by public health agencies and media for variant tracking during the COVID-19 pandemic.
profile.access_model: Free, open-source, publicly accessible. No login required. All code and derived data on GitHub.

all.d: Saudi Arabia Health Electronic Surveillance Network for notifiable diseases and Hajj monitoring

profile.overview: The Health Electronic Surveillance Network (HESN) is Saudi Arabia's national digital disease surveillance system operated by the Ministry of Health for real-time monitoring of notifiable diseases and health events.
profile.functional_scope: National electronic disease surveillance: notifiable disease reporting, real-time case tracking, outbreak detection and response, Hajj/Umrah mass gathering surveillance, syndromic surveillance, and AMR monitoring across all Saudi healthcare facilities.
profile.tech_stack: Web-based electronic reporting system. Integrated with hospital information systems. GIS mapping. Real-time dashboards. Mobile reporting applications.
profile.operator: Saudi Arabia Ministry of Health (MOH), Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. Mandatory reporting from all public and private healthcare facilities.
profile.data_model: Case-level surveillance data for notifiable diseases. Syndromic surveillance signals. Laboratory results. Demographic and geographic data. Mass gathering health monitoring.
profile.users_scale: Covers all Saudi Arabian healthcare facilities and population (~36M). Critical for Hajj surveillance (2-3M pilgrims annually). Reports to WHO IHR focal point.
profile.access_model: Government surveillance system. Accessible to authorized MOH personnel and healthcare facilities. Aggregate data shared with WHO. Not publicly accessible.

all.d: CEPI-led Pandemic Preparedness Exchange - world's first global AI-factory platform for rapid infectious disease risk identification supporting G7 100 Days Mission

profile.overview: The Pandemic Preparedness Exchange (PPX) is CEPI's (Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations) AI-powered global platform for pandemic preparedness R&D coordination, aiming to compress vaccine development timelines to 100 days.
profile.functional_scope: AI-driven R&D coordination: pathogen threat prioritization, vaccine candidate tracking, clinical trial optimization, manufacturing capacity mapping, regulatory pathway harmonization. Supports CEPI's 100 Days Mission for rapid vaccine development.
profile.tech_stack: AI/ML platform for data analytics and prediction. Cloud-based collaboration tools. Integration with clinical trial databases, manufacturing registries, and regulatory databases.
profile.operator: Coalition for Epidemic Preparedness Innovations (CEPI), Oslo, Norway. Funded by governments (Norway, UK, Germany, Japan, etc.), Gates Foundation, Wellcome Trust.
profile.data_model: Vaccine R&D pipeline data, manufacturing capacity inventories, clinical trial registries, pathogen threat assessments, regulatory approval timelines. Integrated multi-source datasets.
profile.users_scale: Serves global vaccine ecosystem: CEPI-funded developers, WHO, regulators, manufacturers. Supports preparedness for 25+ priority pathogen families.
profile.access_model: CEPI institutional platform. Access for CEPI partners and stakeholders. Public-facing threat assessments and reports available via cepi.net.

all.d: Autonomous Diagnostics to Enable Prevention and Therapeutics - rapid troop-level diagnostic platform development

profile.overview: DARPA ADEPT (Autonomous Diagnostics to Enable Prevention and Therapeutics) developed rapid point-of-care diagnostic technologies that can identify pathogens and produce genetic countermeasures in field conditions.
profile.functional_scope: Rapid field-deployable diagnostics: pathogen identification from clinical samples in under 1 hour, nucleic acid-based therapeutics production (mRNA, antibodies), autonomous diagnostic platforms for forward-deployed military units.
profile.tech_stack: Microfluidic diagnostic devices, isothermal amplification, portable sequencing, cell-free protein synthesis for rapid antibody/therapeutic production. Field-deployable form factors.
profile.operator: DARPA Biological Technologies Office (BTO), Arlington, VA. Performer teams from academia and industry. Technologies transitioned to DoD medical systems.
profile.data_model: Diagnostic assay specifications, pathogen identification results, therapeutic production parameters. Integration with military medical C2 systems.
profile.users_scale: Research program with defense transition pathways. Technologies adopted by JPEO-CBRND and military medical commands. Influenced commercial rapid diagnostic development.
profile.access_model: DARPA research program. Some technologies transitioned to defense acquisition. Research publications in open literature. IP managed through government contracts.

all.d: DARPA SIGMA+ expanding advanced sensor networks for real-time detection of chemical, biological, radiological and nuclear threats in urban environments
